若往下游看，會發現大量資金都流向Asia。最直接受惠就是台積電及三星及SKHynix。不論是晶片或記憶體，全球最重要的供應能力都在幾家公司手上。台積電佔台股42%，Samsung及SKHynix佔南韓一大半市值。魏哲家說下面幾年都很好，要買股票請繼續。

台積電的權值魔力：MSCI 指數視角

看全世界資金討論投資台灣時，看的是MSCI Taiwan Index。在指中台積電權重高達57%。對國外基金經理人來說，投100美元就有57美元進到台積電。

台積電佔比被放大原因是因為MSCI Taiwan看重的權值股其市值遠遠超過其他公司。

台積電之所強，靠的是高度分工合作幾十年立起的科技產業聚落。拿Nvidia的AI伺服器為例，從晶片設計到封裝、散熱、交換器，關鍵環節全部集中在台灣。黃仁勛名單越來越長。若說台積電是大腦，台灣科技供應鏈就是神經、血管、器官。

印度為何掉隊：稀缺性決定話語權

印度雖有人口規模及IT外包服務能力，但在這輪AI行情中，目前市場買的是誰掌握最稀缺的基礎設施。印度也努力推動半導體與ASML合作建立晶圓廠，但半導體並非近期可就位。

贏家是長建立握瓶頸的： - 石油時代掌握油田的有定價權 - 工業時代掌握製造的有 - AI時代掌握晶片算力基礎設施的有

台韓這輪超車印度，代表AI產業正從模型競賽進入基礎設施及實世界移動競賽。

第二階段：Physical AI 與南韓的角色

Nvidia不只是GPU公司。黃仁勛南韓見的人、事、公司，代表高頻寬記憶體、機器人、工業製造都在南韓。這也是實體AI要落地的強項。

Physical AI需要製造、機電、感測系統。這些都是南韓強項公司代表的方向。Nvidia要推自駕車平台，目的就是要讓AI模擬並在真實世界行動。

第一階段台韓提供算力，第二階台韓在落地的製造上極其重。台韓超車印度乃是全球資本重價值。

結語：選擇比努力重要

投資要了解世界變化並在可承受風險中參與。不論你喜不喜歡，AI正在發生，改變資本流向。生在台韓與亞洲是幸運的，黃仁勛甚至說AI需求讓台灣今年GDP可來到10%。選擇比努力重。

大家趁大趨勢出現時加學習。也記得按讚訂閱及透過資訊欄學習如何操作及防詐諮詢。下次見。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觀的觀察：台灣的勝利，本質上是「地理規模的詛咒」變成了「祝福」
大家都在歡慶台灣超車印度，但真正詭異的事實是——**台灣贏，恰恰是因為它「太小了」**。
印度有15億人口、南韓有5200萬，這些「規模」在過去40年是經濟學的聖杯。但AI時代發生了一件人類經濟史上很少見的事：**「產業聚落的密度」打敗了「國家的體量」**。台積電所在的新竹到台南這條走廊，騎機車3小時可以走完，但這條走廊裡塞著全球90%的先進製程、封裝、散熱、PCB、機構件供應鏈。
這就是為什麼Broadcom財報一打噴嚏、台股就重感冒——台灣不是「一個國家」在參與AI，而是**「一個產業園區」被資本市場錯認成一個國家**。台股42%是台積電一家，這個比例在任何金融教科書裡都會被標註為「極度危險的單一風險暴露」。但這同時也意味著：台灣不是在分散風險，而是在All-in一個賭注，而這個賭注剛好押對了。
## 二、跨領域連接：南韓「螞蟻」、明朝「東學黨」、與Reddit上的GameStop散戶——同一場全球運動的三個切片
影片提到南韓「東學螞蟻運動」很有意思，但作者沒挑明一件事：**這是全球年輕世代第

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] Anker / Steven Yang: Consumer Electronics Death & birth, The Third Category, Product Philosophy
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"易模式陷阱"——杨萌真正的对手不是 DJI，而是 15 年前那个"聪明"的自己

杨萌坦言他选了 easy mode，DJI 选了 hell mode。表面看这是路径选择题，但有一个反讽极少被人注意到：前 11 年的顺风顺水，恰恰是 2022 年痛苦的根源。 一家公司的"舒适债"和金融债一样会累积复利。当 DJI 在最初几年用最痛苦的方式补齐底层技术债时，Anker 则用最舒适的方式累积了 15 年"我不需要核心技术"的肌肉记忆。

所以 2022 年那场危机的本质不是产品线扩张失败，而是 easy mode 惯性对企业精神肌肉的慢性侵蚀。杨萌真正在搏斗的对手，是那个 2011 年做出"先卖货、后做产品"决策的自己。

二、妈妈要回 300 万但不要股份——一次教科书级的"反向尽调"

这是整段访谈里最被低估的细节。妈妈作为天使投资人投了 300 万，最后只拿回本金、放弃所有股权。常规叙事会把它解读成"母爱"，但更精确的描述是：这是一次反向尽职调查。她在测试一件事——你这事到底是做给我看的（亲情债），还是真的属于你？她抽走"亲情锚点"，让杨萌必须独自面对市场。

跨领域类比：这与硅谷顶级投资人故意拒绝早期 follow-on 以"逼迫"创始人独立融资的逻辑完全一致——拿走安全垫，才能长出真正的肌肉。一个中国母亲，无意中完成了一次极其专业的 VC 动作。

三、他在芯片层和组织层在做同一件事——废除"冯·诺依曼"

这是访谈里最让人震惊的同构性，但杨萌本人似乎都没明说：

  • 芯片层：打破存储与计算的分离（存算一体）
  • 组织层：打破业务单元的决策与执行分离（President & Federation）
  • 战略层：打破微创新与极致创新的分离（5 系→7 系）
  • 方法论层：打破"分治法"对端到端的禁锢

他口口声声讲"第一性"，但他骨子里真正信奉的第一性原理其实是 "反分治、求一体"。AI 时代之于他不是"用大模型"，而是终于在物理层面验证了他长期以来排斥的那种"切碎—管理—缝合"的世界观。

divide-and-conquer 是过去 80 年工程学的圣经，杨萌却在三个完全不相干的维度上同步把它推翻。这才是 Anker 真正的护城河——不是技术、不是品牌，而是整体论世界观的彻底执行力。

四、"选择性遗忘电影"暴露了他自己的运行架构

"我看过的电影几乎记不住，半年后再看像新的一样"——这句被他当玩笑讲出，却是整场访谈最锋利的自我披露。

他在描述一个事实：他对抽象结构的记忆是连续的、坚固的；对具体内容的记忆是被主动压缩、定期清空的。

这是什么？这就是大语言模型的"参数化记忆 vs 上下文记忆"的人类版本。他把具体事件压缩进抽象框架（参数），丢弃原始 token（上下文）。一个 INTP 通过几十年训练，把自己活成了一台 MoE 模型：专家是行业知识，门控是第一性思维，KV cache 被周期性清空。

而他和东萍的搭档关系——一个抽象，一个具体——本质上是他在外部部署了一块"长上下文记忆体"。东萍负责储存所有他不愿存储的"具体"。这不是性格互补的温情故事，这是一个 INTP 工程师主动给自己外接的一块内存条。

五、"创造者拿 7、股东拿 3"不是慷慨，是消费电子规律的精算结果

杨萌讲这个比例时用了"价值观"框架，但有一个更冷峻的解读：消费电子是一个 10–15 年品类必死的行业。MP3、CD、磁带、移动电源…… 都难逃此命运。所以股东的长期主义不可能寄托在某个具体品类的复利上，只能寄托在"创造者的连续性"上。

换句话说，这本质上不是分配问题，而是期权定价问题：

  • 当品类生命周期足够短，资本的边际价值持续衰减；
  • 而人才的"再创造期权"持续升值；
  • 理性的股东应该主动让渡多数收益以锁定创造者。

所以 7:3 不是"杨萌人好"，而是消费电子赛道的数学结构倒推出来的近似最优股权契约。

理解了这一层，你才能明白他为什么坚持上市——上市的真正动机不是融资，而是把"创造者拿 7"这件事法律化、合约化、可跨品类继承化，让它能跨越任何一个具体品类的死亡。这是创始人用治理结构对抗熵增。

六、一个意外的跨领域连接：Anker 与晚清"洋务运动"的同构性

杨萌反复强调"我们必须补技术课"——2023 年建实验室、2024 年做芯片、2025 年改使命愿景。表面看这是企业升级，但放在更长的时间尺度里看，它复刻了一个完整的"商业→制造→技术→精神"的递进序列。

晚清的失败在于"中体西用"——只补硬件、不补内核。而 Anker 这次升级的特殊之处在于：它先补了商业（前 11 年）→ 再补技术（2023 起）→ 最后补精神内核（使命愿景价值观）。

注意这个顺序是反向的：杨萌不是先有愿景再做产品，而是产品做了 11 年才反推出真正的愿景。这暗示了一件反直觉的事——对于工程师型创始人，使命愿景不是起点，而是反复试错后的"涌现产物"。这也是为什么很多 Day 1 就把愿景做得很漂亮的公司反而活不长：它们的愿景是"声明"出来的，不是"长"出来的。

📝 转录文本

开场

Hello everyone I am Xiaojun. In the previous episodes, together with you, I met many very young faces. They are in the AI ​​era — native entrepreneurs and technology leaders. But AI's penetration into society is all-round; it's not just about new products and new technologies.

Our guest today is Yang Meng, the founder and CEO of Anker Innovation. He was born in 1982, started his own business in 2011, and now he is at the helm of a technology company with a market value of more than 60 billion yuan.

In the face of new AI paradigm changes — what would Yang Meng do? Especially in consumer electronics, on the battlefield of infinite war. Next is my interview with Yang Meng.

一段开场独白

This world is particularly fair. What I just said — we actually didn't have any difficulties in financing before, right? Then everything went smoothly. In fact, our life has been quite painful these past few years.

I think DJI is a player from day one — I chose a difficult mode, even a hell-difficulty-mode company. This is the first category I chose. It's actually a very, very difficult category. So the first few years must have been very painful.

Becoming a third-category company generally needs a CEO. What kind of character and personality is he?

自我介绍

Xiaojun: Hello Steven, say hello to the viewers.

Steven: Hello Xiaojun. I am the founder of Anker Innovation and my name is Yang Meng. I'm glad to be on your show.

Xiaojun: Yes, I saw you said you want to transform from a business company into a deep-technology company. Has the world changed or have you changed? Everyone's first reaction when they heard Anker — it seems that I first got to know this brand because of the power bank. Then when I heard that Anker was making chips, making embodied intelligence, doing these complicated things, I will be very curious.

Steven: Actually we think this is a company that evolves naturally — step by step, stepping up, a natural process.

从北大到 Google：好学生的路径

Xiaojun: Let's talk about it from the beginning. We also want the audience and listeners to know who Anker is, who you are. From what I saw about your growth experience, it seems that you have always been a good student in the eyes of others — Changsha Yali Middle School, Peking University Department of Computer Science, then to the United States to study for a Ph.D. Although you're a graduate, you worked at Google for 5 years and won Google's highest award, the Founders Award. Did you grow up as an out-of-the-ordinary person, or are you an out-of-the-ordinary person?

Steven: Am I a good student — I must be an above-average person, I must be the kind of good student that everyone sees. And in my heart, it seems that I also use good students as the standard to demand myself. By the way, the Founders Award is actually a collective award — it is not an award for individuals.

I majored in computer science as an undergraduate, then after graduation I went to the United States to study with everyone — I followed everyone, because in our department at that time the top one-third of the students may have applied to overseas schools and then went to the United States to study for Ph.D. This is not necessarily the case now, but we were in 2003, that was almost 23 years ago.

Then I went to UT Austin. At that time the CS ranking in North America was pretty good. My professor's name was Raymond Mooney. He was a pioneer in the machine-learning world. He served as the chairman of ICML for several terms. Machine Learning — using machines to do AI — has always been something I particularly like and am very excited about.

But after I went there, I didn't feel particularly good. Because we were doing something at that time with a very small data set. It's hard for you to imagine — it's an entire data set of maybe more than 20,000 Reuters articles. Then the adjustment of your various algorithms is based on a 20% test set of more than 4,000 articles. The difference between one point and two points above — you feel it seems that you can't feel its actual meaning.

So in 2005 I went to Google for an internship. I jumped to it immediately. It was to do algorithms from 4 billion web pages. You know that Google at that time — it was hundreds of millions every day, in search volume. You can change even one thousandth of the search; it's actually a big number. So I felt that it was special, and of practical value. It was special and really useful.

退学：一个 I 人独自做出的决定

Xiaojun: So you didn't go back to study for a Ph.D. — you just dropped out, right?

Steven: In fact, dropping out of school was an accident. It was not a well-thought-out choice. It should be said that it was an inner calling. When you do something very practical today that creates value, then going back to an ivory tower and doing something as sharp as a hornet's nest, as if you don't know what the value is — you feel a little reluctant.

Xiaojun: Did anyone object at the time? Is this the first rebellious decision you made?

Steven: It must be a big decision. My family wanted to say, "hey, it seems like you've studied for a Ph.D., you finish it and then talk." But I felt I really couldn't continue reading. I think my choice is actually very consistent — I must create special and real value, and not something illusory or far away. I definitely hope to be able to stand where I am today and see that there is a real value we can achieve.

In fact, the purpose of studying for a Ph.D. is to do scientific research. I think doing scientific research may be divided into two types of students: one kind has the ability to invent a completely new architecture or framework — to open up a new direction. Another category is to improve in the existing direction, or combine it with specific problems. There are very, very few people in the former category. Maybe every five or ten years a big star comes out and really opens up a huge direction. Most people are like me — doing a lot of additions in the back direction.

In our time, the big vein was called Support Vector Machine (SVM). After the decision tree, it was a big breakthrough in the machine-learning world — probably invented in 1997 or 1998. Very popular, very effective, could solve many practical problems. But by the time I got my Ph.D. in 2003–2004, basically this mine had been dug almost as much as you can see. I felt I hadn't found something that seemed particularly capable of creating actual value. Going back to Google, you think — wow, he can really change the lives of many people.

Xiaojun: How did you face the opposition around you on that decision?

Steven: Actually, because I am an I person, this decision was made without consulting many people. Even though my parents objected, after all, I had grown up.

在 Google 的五年

Steven: I started internship in 2005. Officially in 2006. Summer internship in 2005, then they gave me an offer, so I went to work at Google at the beginning of 2006.

Xiaojun: What did you learn at Google?

Steven: I feel that I have learned too many things at Google. Whether from the corporate culture — "don't do evil," work hard to create — or the specific methods, the way of thinking about problems. There are many ways to solve a problem, and they were all subtle, buried at that time.

And then there is the atmosphere of the company. You know that Google has always been a very egalitarian, open and transparent atmosphere. So when I started my own business later, I also brought this kind of atmosphere into my own company. I don't have my own office — I sit with everyone. We are also very equal when we have meetings. And all our meeting rooms are not walls — they're glass; you can see through them at a glance, you can see what's going on inside. Equality and transparency bring openness, and an environment for collision and creation.

So I think the first company in my life had a huge impact on me. I'm very grateful for it.

Xiaojun: I heard you worked with Jeff Dean?

Steven: There are also some direct contacts. I submitted a changelist to Jeff Dean — I modified his code, after the modification he helped me do a code review. If this is considered contact, then it actually means that you may not necessarily be able to come into contact with, or may have come into contact with quite a few people in the company.

After you joined Google you found it particularly magical — because all the backbone code, from the very beginning, Google File System, MapReduce, the file headers of the main types of files in Bigtable, the first author is either Jeff Dean or Sanjay Ghemawat. So he is the great one. You feel like the whole world was created by him. Then we are in the world he created, doing a lot of improvement work. Same feeling as in academia — someone can create a world, and we go inside to make the world a better place.

Xiaojun: But do you want to be the one who creates the world?

Steven: I don't think I have that strong desire — that feeling that I can create a world.

Xiaojun: What kind of engineer were you at Google?

Steven: I actually don't write too much code. Because I am biased toward abstract thinking, architectural thinking. I am willing to discuss with everyone the definition of the problem, the dismantling of the problem. Our manager quickly promoted me to a "Tech lead," which means we lead four or five people and then take on the specific tasks of a module. So I'd be doing things like communicating issues externally, and doing the system architecture internally. Google engineers don't have to worry — everyone is very willing to do creative things. But how to clarify a direction with our customers and come back to our team — today we all reached a consensus. Of course, we write part of the code ourselves; this has to be done by someone.

安克的开端：从电商到产品

Xiaojun: How did you start doing Anker's early work? Were you still at Google at that time?

Steven: My girlfriend at that time, later wife, was in Silicon Valley. Because she is a liberal-arts student, she hadn't been working for a while. We were thinking — find something she can do together.

When I went to the United States in 2003, I just started buying things online — buying on many different websites. By 2008 and 2009 you find that basically you shop on Amazon — its experience, richness, price, including its logistics, are the best. Then we discovered: actually there are a lot of things on Amazon whose selection is not the best.

So my girlfriend at that time (later my wife) and I wanted to say — can we start selling some things on Amazon? And help her, as a liberal-arts classmate who has difficulty finding a job in the United States, find something to do.

So the very beginning was selling. You soon found out that selling itself is not the difficult problem. Where to get the best products — that's the difficult thing. In fact, I quit Google in 2011. At that time the person in charge of sales in Google China was named Zhao Dongping. Dongping and I — I make products, he is responsible for global sales. We founded the Anker brand together.

Anker was originally an e-commerce company. At that time our name was Haiyi E-commerce. We started looking for it from China. Soon you find that you can't find good ones — so you have to go to the factory to customize, to customize the best products. Then sell to the United States. Then of course it won't be that expensive.

Because there were no relevant e-commerce talents anywhere in the country, we wanted to say — let's build the company in Changsha, we'll train e-commerce talents ourselves. So after so many years, indeed our e-commerce team in Changsha is very stable — these are school admissions we have trained ourselves.

做充电宝的决定

Xiaojun: At that time, when you were a Google engineer returning to China to start a business — do you understand the idea of ​​making a power bank?

Steven: Actually, to be honest, when introducing it to everyone many years later, it seems that I don't even carry a power bank on purpose. Including today, we definitely want to label the power bank "being able to tear it off." Because what we actually want to do is create real customer value — something that can really satisfy people and bring value. I just don't have anything that sounds fancy and creates great value. In the channel, the most clear things we see that can bring value to customers are actually charging products.

Because there were smartphones at that time. Actually if you go back to 2011, smartphones were developing rapidly. At that time the electricity would not last a day. So the power bank is something that everyone needs very much. We want to quickly fill in this value blank.

Xiaojun: When you quit at that time, did your family say anything?

Steven: In our family, my father is a teacher and my mother doesn't really care about my studies. So my father still expressed his objections — "Do you think you should talk about it first?" Actually, when I quit my PhD before, my family didn't say much.

On the other hand, my mother — actually, she is the person in charge of sales of a state-owned enterprise. She asked me, "Do you need money to start a business?" I said I still need some start-up capital — there were investors outside willing to invest. My mother was very happy at the time. She said, "Is this true?" I said I thought it was pretty reliable. I said it could definitely be done. My mother said, "Let's do that reliable thing with our own money." So she gave me 3 million. Then she is our angel investor.

Xiaojun: Oh, your mother is your angel investor.

Steven: Yes. But in the end she asked for the money back, but not the shares. So everyone didn't see this.

Xiaojun: Why did she want the money back?

Steven: From her perspective, she just wanted to support me — just get this thing done. It's not like she was going back on it. She wanted to support me in doing this, and she said, "In the long run, how can I do this better?"

Xiaojun: The seeds for starting your own business — at what moment were they planted?

Steven: Actually I've always been about truly bringing value to customers. This has always been a special, exciting point for me — visible, satisfying others. So actually I've always had this motivation to start a business.

We first started with e-commerce. Because China didn't have any overseas business at the time, we returned to Changsha. Dongping also moved his family from Beijing to Changsha. We incubated in Changsha — I brought out an e-commerce team. Soon I moved to Shenzhen at the beginning of 2012. Because we found that selling is not a difficult thing — a good product is the most difficult thing. And if you really want to have good products, the place to create products is Shenzhen.

与东萍的搭档

Xiaojun: What kind of person is Dongping? Why did you choose to pull him in when starting your business?

Steven: Dongping is fun. My personality is an I person, INTP — thinking in a special logical framework, very rational.

Xiaojun: And very naughty?

Steven: Yes. Dongping is the complete opposite of me. He is an ESFJ. To both of us they are like the poles of the earth — Antarctica and North Pole. The difference in style is huge.

Xiaojun: And you find, we've been together for 15 years — do you think your personalities are closer now, or more in opposite directions?

Steven: It probably went in both directions. We definitely found — after 15 years — because there is someone who can look at your back safely, when there is a person who can see your not-so-good side, you are more bold and go towards what you are good at.

Xiaojun: How did you meet?

Steven: At that time, I returned to start a business. Our colleagues at Google thought I should be doing overseas business. My classmates all wanted to buy Google ads, and they introduced me to who Google was — at the time the advertising sales team in China. They thought this classmate seems more interesting — he didn't just want to sell things, he wanted to make good products. They introduced me to their team leader at the time, Dongping. Dongping is very good at sales and selling things. He thought, "Hey, then you can find someone to start a business together — the engineer is responsible for making it, and he is responsible for selling." Sales is actually a good combination. So we started a business together.

Xiaojun: Someone mentioned Dongping to me. That means he should be very important in your company.

Steven: Because he is responsible for the construction of all overseas channels — that's our company. Just kidding. I'll be in charge of product-related matters. Dongping will be in charge of all the things I don't care about. We are a very good entrepreneurial partner — we've been together for so many years. And we actually sit side by side all the time in the company — sitting for many years. Because we don't have our own offices. We all sit outside — this is the workstation we are talking about. His workstation and mine are right next to each other.

第一款产品与第一款爆品

Xiaojun: The first product you created was Anker's charger?

Steven: In fact, the first product we made was a power bank. It was actually very advanced back then — stuck on the back of the phone, very thin, with a built-in cable. Basically after 10 years, you just saw the whole market heading in that direction.

This is the first product we developed ourselves. But objectively, it was not particularly popular — because the form of this product was quite advanced. Back then all power banks were cordless. Although we made it, our marketing capability back then — to bring the real value of a product to users and let them really understand and like it — was relatively weak. Even if the wine is good, the alley is too deep. So that product was not particularly popular in the end.

Later, of course, we built an R&D team. We continued to launch one product after another.

Xiaojun: Which is the first hot product in your eyes?

Steven: The first one that was particularly explosive — actually a power bank we call "lipstick." It's made of a cylindrical battery core, about the same size as a lipstick. Very convenient to put in the bag and can replenish about half of the phone's battery. Long life cycle — it should have sold millions of units. That must have been 2013, two years after starting the business.

Xiaojun: Your company's revenue and profits in those two years — pretty good?

Steven: When starting from scratch, the growth rate must be very fast. I don't actually remember the numbers clearly. In my impression, it should be over 100 million in revenue in 2012.

Dongping and I have chosen this path — the certainty is quite strong. You can see the demand on the left, you can find it with certainty on the right (including customized good supplies), and the bridge in the middle — there is no difficulty in setting up by ourselves. So the certainty should be quite high.

Xiaojun: So you are a team that can easily find business opportunities?

Steven: I think we are a very pragmatic team. We stand on our own abilities every time, moving forward to achieve what we should be able to achieve. We always say in the company: we are the ones who step firmly on each step. I will definitely work hard to reach the next level.

融资与上市

Xiaojun: At the beginning of your business, did you consider financing?

Steven: Our cash flow was positive from the beginning. Every cycle could actually produce good profits. We had no real need for financing.

Our first financing was in 2017. It's the same as DeepSeek's financing today — it doesn't necessarily require money. Because we started with e-commerce, every cycle is the process from procurement to sales. Both have pretty good gross profits, so the company's cash flow has always been positive.

By 2017 we raised money for the first time — not because we needed funds, but because a lot of shares were issued to employees at that time. How much is this thing worth? Let's find the leading VC at the time to invest some money in us — equivalent to setting a price. In this case, the shares in your hands seem to have a clear value.

The advantage of pricing is: the students who get the shares can truly believe it. The shares are worth so much money because you told him so — he always feels, "Do you think it's reliable?" But today when a head VC like IDG spends real money buying these shares, and you tell him "you see the shares I gave you are worth so much money" — it's easier for him to believe.

Xiaojun: This is in line with DeepSeek's financing strategy today.

Steven: I don't think so. Does DeepSeek need money? Actually, I don't know much. A large-model company logically needs to raise a lot of money for training. I just felt what I said was not quite right. If we go back to this time last year, that pricing may actually have been necessary because there was no complete pricing on the market. But at the beginning of the year, after Zhipu and others were all launched, DeepSeek's classmates can refer to Zhipu's price. I don't think the demand is that strong.

At that time, we — when we didn't have such a high demand for funds and came out for financing and pricing — there was no comparable frame of reference. There was no other product company like us. Because companies doing e-commerce at that time, everyone kept thinking about buying and selling. Product companies in fact were very few.

Xiaojun: You launched the listing. Was this already decided when you raised funds?

Steven: Our listing is very clear — we hope employees can realize their shares. I chose China as the place of listing. Maybe employees can cash out their stocks for more.

1357 框架：用户分层

Xiaojun: If you take your company's 15-year development history and divide it into nodes, how would you divide it?

Steven: I think the first ten years are one stage. The first ten years: we went from one category to many categories, from one channel to many channels, from one country to many countries. A very natural extended process.

First we divide all user groups into four — we call them 1 / 3 / 5 / 7 series people. The 1-series only wants to spend the minimum amount of money — buy something that basically works, the cheapest thing. The 3-series wants the best value for money. The 5-series wants good and stable performance and high quality, willing to pay a certain premium. The 7-series is all about the most expensive and best things.

For example, milk tea: the cheapest is Mixue Bingcheng (6 yuan), the value-for-money is around 10–12 yuan, the very-stable (5-series) is "the aunt in Shanghai" at 15–16 yuan, finally Heytea (7-series) at 20 yuan.

Another example, cars: the cheapest four-wheeler is the QQ at 40,000 yuan (1-series). The most expensive high-volume cars — Mercedes, BMW, Audi at around 400,000 (7-series). In the middle, BYD around 100,000, Volkswagen / Toyota around 150,000–160,000, often using the same engine. So 1-series is 40,000, 7-series is 400,000.

If you're going to buy a BYD, what do you think of people who buy Volkswagen? And vice versa — what do Volkswagen buyers think of BYD buyers? Looking at this abstractly across categories, the crowd, depending on consumption habits, can be divided into these four groups — 1 / 3 / 5 / 7.

Anker 是几系？为什么"海外小米"的标签不准

Xiaojun: Many of your products are mid-to-high end. That was only the impression you gave in recent years. What about the beginning?

Steven: In the first ten years, many people said Anker is an "overseas Xiaomi." If that's the case, we'd be a 3-series brand — because Xiaomi was famous for cost-effectiveness (before going high-end). But students who really buy Anker overseas know: Anker's stuff isn't cheap. Often 20% or even 30% more expensive than market average. There's very good quality behind it. Anker products on Amazon are usually 4.5 stars or higher.

So actually, in the first ten years we were a 5-series company — very good quality, very stable, with a certain (but not too big) premium, expanded into many categories.

This is also related to the channel we were born in. Because on Amazon, you definitely choose the product with the best star rating. One option is to brush orders to 4.5 or even 5 stars. But Dongping and I have always felt: we want to do things for a long time. Otherwise, we might as well go back to working at a company. We can't do things that cut corners or cross the line. So we never swiped screens or placed fake orders. If you don't brush the screen and still want this product to achieve 4.5 stars — you are forcing your product manager and R&D team to make the quality extremely good. That's Anker in the first ten years.

为什么先做海外、后做中国？

Xiaojun: People used to say Anker isn't well-known in China but has a high reputation overseas. Was this a subjective choice?

Steven: Definitely a subjective choice. When we first chose Amazon, countries like the US, Japan, European countries — Amazon was most developed there. Amazon in China should have shut down, right? So as we grew from this channel, our overseas countries grew faster.

Xiaojun: Have you ever considered going to China?

Steven: We didn't do China for a long time — until 2018 or 2019. At that time many people purchased our products from overseas and brought them back to China to sell on Taobao. We felt this shouldn't happen, so we only started working on the Chinese market in 2019–2020.

Xiaojun: Why so late? Many people may think you found the Chinese market too complicated.

Steven: China's consumption upgrade only started around 2017–2018. Very simple — you go to Taobao to buy a power bank, the earliest average price was maybe 30–50 yuan. Only after 2017–2018 did you see everyone's pursuit of better products emerge. Only then did a market for 5-series products begin to exist in China.

So we made a product positioned as 5-series, which may not have matched the Chinese market at that time but matched European, American, and Japanese markets. These positionings are analyzed after the fact, put forward when summarizing. At the time, you only knew you wanted to make the product well. You find a good product on Amazon — it's actually a 5-series product.

公平的世界

Xiaojun: Hey, why? This looks like a blue ocean. In that era, why did you discover this business opportunity and others did not?

Steven: It must be a very blue blue ocean. I buy things on Amazon all day because I'm too lazy to go to offline stores. After a few years on Amazon, you find you can't buy the best thing for you. Then I do it myself.

This is looking back. If we thought it over carefully then, with the vision and strategic thinking of today's business — I would most likely not choose this category. Because you find it not fun.

This world is particularly fair. What I just said — we actually didn't have any financing difficulties before. Things went smoothly. In fact, we have had quite a painful life these past few years.

Xiaojun: How many years have you been smooth?

Steven: Until 2022. I've been smooth for 11 years.

品类扩张的本能阶段

Xiaojun: During those 10 smooth years, how did you choose a category or product?

Steven: The extension of our categories was quite natural. In the earliest days, charging-related products. After power banks, you found everyone also lacked chargers, lacked charging cables — so we started this line. Following the smartphone, you discovered: everyone still lacks Bluetooth headphones, speakers. There were also protective cases, but we weren't very successful in that category.

In 2016, everyone said it was the Internet of Things era — all products today should be equipped with IoT. So naturally we entered the IoT category.

Xiaojun: Have you ever thought about making mobile phones or cars?

Steven: Subconsciously we felt — those particularly large categories, we shouldn't touch.

2020：第一次真正做战略

Steven: By 2020, the first time we really did strategy and thought systematically — we realized that behind this intuition is actually supported by a logical system. We call them super categories and small-and-medium categories — also called deep seas and shallow seas.

Open up an industry and look. Very large categories — super categories. In consumer electronics: mobile phones $500B, PC $200B, TV $100B (first, second, third). The fourth is tablets at only $60B. From $500B to $60B is a 10× deterioration in scale. This is the deep sea.

Shallow sea — categories under $50B. You can count one to two hundred. Power banks are a $5B category. Charging cables $5B. Headphones maybe $20–30B. Consumer drones — globally less than $5B annual sales, also shallow sea.

Many people misunderstand: "shallow" means simplicity. No — our "shallow" represents small size. The market size determines the category. In a smaller shallow category, the saturation investment won't be too large. Vice versa — making a tablet, TV, phone, or car, at minimum you need a team of thousands.

We have an analogy. It's like a Texas Hold'em poker table. Each category is a table. The shallow sea is a blind of millions. You play the whole game, your win or loss may be hundreds of millions. The super category — a blind bet might be 500 million. You go to the table with 5 billion to fight, maybe play three games and that's it.

In the first ten years, we moved forward based on intuition, never reviewed. Until 2020, we started periodic review and summary — and summarized the "shallow sea strategy." Looking back as CEO, what I didn't do well is I didn't seriously, systematically involve people in the company to think together: how many categories we do, why we do so many, which categories we do, why those.

消费电子的"必死"恐惧

Xiaojun: What kind of company do you think Anker was at that time?

Steven: In 20 years we built a logical framework. Four super categories in consumer electronics. We've done about a dozen small-and-medium categories. We made quite a few 5-series products. A very small number of 7-series (our security products in North America were very expensive, sold pretty well). And some categories essentially as 3-series.

So we were a relatively divergent company — not focused, no clear idea of where it was going and why.

Xiaojun: What does it have to do with your personality, the CEO?

Steven: Starting in 2016 — I went to Hupan to study business school, second-term student. I was asked: "Why will your category become a hundred-billion category?" My answer was scary. I said: my category — just the power-bank category — not only will not become hundreds of billions, it will most likely be dead in a few years.

Because from that time you start to look at things abstractly. Consumer electronics is an industry where things appear very quickly and disappear very quickly. Live fast, die fast. For example, MP3 — and if you're like me, you've probably bought a tape player or CD player. From when you start buying until you stop, the span is only ten years.

So the power bank — we started buying in 2012. We thought at that time it might only have a 10–15 year life cycle, so between 2022 and 2027 no one would buy it. Seems a bit over-thought afterwards — the power bank hasn't reached that dead node. But we believe the category will definitely disappear.

This creates some fear. When you start asking about strategy, you're afraid of your own category. There were a few years in between when we thought: how can we do something that is a category that will never die?

I actually saw one — electronic cigarettes. Because nicotine, I think, is a need humans will never lose. Before, nicotine was ingested through combustion; switching from combustion to electric heating, we feel this change is likely permanent. So logically this category should stabilize. But later I found this category is heavily regulated — not suitable for startups.

There are categories that won't disappear — air conditioners, washing machines. But these already have companies doing them.

2019 学华为 IPD：把能力沉淀进组织

Steven: We found the answer in 2019. I saw books about IPD and Huawei — how to improve your ability to succeed in a category, systematize it, so that organizations can produce more categories good. In other words: precipitate people's abilities into the organization, so this organization can produce more categories.

We started promoting organizational change. At that point we had several business divisions. The heads made product decisions. We developed product lines under business departments. Back to sales — organizations growing business lines. In short: transform from a small number of people making decisions to clearly divided decision-making units, giving leaders greater decision-making authority.

Let leaders make 98% or more of the decisions themselves.

Xiaojun: Before that, you alone were in charge of products?

Steven: I was not in charge of front-line products. We started changes in 2020 — not just adding a product-line layer, but letting the company's business really run in a systematic way.

2022 的危机

Xiaojun: You mentioned the first 11 years were smooth but became unsatisfactory in 2022. So do you think the strategy and organization you proposed at that time was correct?

Steven: Looking at today, it seems correct. But I was very doubtful in 2022 — or rather, very desperate.

Why did we encounter those crises in 2022? From the phenomenon — it was the product lines we built. In real business competition you discovered we couldn't compete with outside unicorn companies. People asked: why does our product line lose to outside unicorns? We encountered a very systematic failure — created a very depressing, even desperate feeling.

But then you understand: it's not that you have process and a system, that you have a formation. If your company doesn't have a clear mission, vision and values — why this company is what it is, how to make it like this, and how these are reflected on people — then no matter what process method you have, you have no way to win.

How to improve combat effectiveness: - A company dealing in few categories may only have a few products a year — no need for many decisions. Believe a group of very capable people to make decisions, no need to design a system. - A company with many categories — if you make decisions alone or with a few, you can't sustain. You need to design a system to let people at different levels make their own decisions.

You said we had 27 product lines, 20 of them couldn't beat others. Is this because the product line was expanded too much? It must have something to do with "many." But the biggest difference isn't more vs less. If you go down — do you have core leading technology? If you go up — does your organization have a specific mission, and a behavior that can really achieve it?

从五系到七系：游戏难度升级

Xiaojun: How did you solve the difficulties of 2022?

Steven: We made changes from mission, vision and values. Substitution is the easiest, but usually not the right answer — the purpose is.

In the first ten years, we were a 5-series company — making it with good quality, with some micro-innovative products. In the second decade — we don't want to be a 5-series company. We want to take the next step. We need to do a good job in user value, do extreme innovation. So we want to be a 7-series company.

Xiaojun: It feels like your company is playing a game. Maybe at the beginning you didn't choose hard mode, you chose easy mode. You played so well you wanted medium difficulty. Now medium is pretty good, you want to play hard.

不偶遇核心技术的"丢人"时刻

Steven: How do you choose which categories to enter? Most categories today have the ability to choose — or rather, were already done before we had a sense of choice.

Let me tell you something interesting. This is a true story. We make UV printers — print on any object, beautiful patterns with slight 3D. The nozzle is provided by Epson. In February this year, Epson's CEO came to visit. He introduced their technology — Epson made Romax technology, derived multiple categories from it. When it came to me, I said: we did charging, audio-video, home automation. Under each direction we made these categories.

He sincerely asked: "Yang-san, you're so awesome, you do so many categories."

My reaction: I told him — listening to you, combining my understanding today, I must be very ashamed. Looking back, the better way is: today you have a good underlying technology, and based on it you expand to 1, 2, or 3 categories.

Jack Ma made an analogy: starting a business is like being on the first floor of a building. Your vision is the first floor. If you do well, you rise — to the 10th, 20th, 30th floor. After reaching higher floors, your understanding of the world should improve.

Xiaojun: What floor are you on now?

Steven: Today the difficulty of the game has reached "hard" and is rising. But not nightmare yet.

Xiaojun: Which level do you think Tuozhu is on?

Steven: Tuozhu is taking a completely different route. We chose easy and went up layer by layer. They chose a very difficult mode — very difficult in front, but after you get through, it's much easier later.

第三类公司

Steven: A successful company that deals in a few categories vs many categories — management methods are naturally different.

In the past ten years we made about a dozen small-and-medium categories, focusing on 5-series. One category (security) at 7. Several at 3. So we were divergent.

We have a framework: - Category 1 company: very few super categories. Apple, Tesla. - Category 2 company: small number of small-and-medium categories. Most companies. - Category 3 company: many small-and-medium categories, can create good customer value.

In consumer electronics there have been such companies — Sony, Philips. Across industries — Procter & Gamble (many categories, many brands), Nike (many sub-categories), Texas Instruments. We hope Anker can become one of them.

第三类公司需要什么样的 CEO

Xiaojun: Becoming a third-category company generally needs a CEO — what kind of character?

Steven: Not a centralized person.

Three levels: - Bottom (terrain): which you can't change. - Middle: three different forms of companies grow. - Top: two different management models — King and Knights (king + knights decide everything) vs President and Federation (president responsible for overall, each state operates its own field).

Third-category companies that succeed long-term all go to the President and Federation model.

Behind this is character: are you willing to step back, empower others? Because all smart homes are worth redoing — but starting a global brand from scratch in hardware is extremely difficult.

Let's look at a company's value chain: the different positions and roles you have to go through to create customer value. For hardware: strategy → product development → user research → tech research → R&D → manufacturing → quality → sales → marketing → service.

Consumer electronics has a very long value chain. Anker has 6,000 people, 200 different positions. Cars and rockets are even longer.

Starting in a long-value-chain company: you need to polish most of the chain to "basically usable" within limited runway. Difficulty grows non-linearly (squared or more) with chain length — because each point doesn't just talk to the next, it talks to a patch of other points.

After AI comes, creators face choices. Building this whole chain alone vs joining a mature platform. On our platform we've successfully implemented many categories — we have a mature value chain, people who run it well, intelligence precipitated into it. We can support entrepreneurs better.

23 年的两件事：深度技术 + 使命愿景

Steven: Setbacks in 2022 contributed to our biggest changes. We shrank to fewer categories, then dug deep into deep technology and extreme innovation. In 2023 we established a lab to do deep tech — the storage-compute integrated chip we just released is one outcome.

Another aspect — changes in mission, vision, values. The 1357 concept came out in 2020 when we seriously started thinking about strategy.

Going from 5-series to 7-series is a very difficult process. At the most basic level, 5-series — everyone can do a great job by improving quality, doing micro-innovation. Our values at that time: "be reasonable, pursue excellence, grow together."

Three "transcendences": - Surpass yesterday's self - Surpass the best peers - Surpass the most essential needs of consumers

The first two are on a deterministic road. But "surpass essential needs" requires breaking away from existing tracks, taking a completely different path that no one has walked — some have died on it. You must dare to see this road, and your entire organization must dare to walk it.

For a company that has walked a certain path for 10 years — such a mental change isn't just "the strategy has changed." Your whole organization, the atmosphere, what everyone believes — must shift. How can thousands of people pass through? It's something we've been exploring for the past few years. Painful, but I think we've gained some good results today.

极致创新者的三层结构

Steven: To make 7-series products is to do extreme innovation. Looking at successful companies — Steve Jobs, Musk — and our own: their characteristics boil down to three points:

  1. How you think: Don't follow what others do. Don't start from what you did in the past. Go back to user value. Study first principles (essence of physics). Build the framework layer by layer. You'll find a missing piece — this missing piece is generally what no one has done.

  2. How you do things: Dare to take huge risks. Try your best to get it done.

  3. How you deal with yourself: In this difficult process — your emotions, difficulties, fear of the unknown.

The third layer requires: - Long-termism: see the future situation, bear short-term pain for long-term interests. - Self-awareness and self-evolution: First find the problem within yourself. Change yourself to solve problems. Sounds like nonsense but very hard. Today, when something goes wrong — do I look for the cause within myself first, or outside? - Continuous learning — we put this together as "growing together."

This framework is quite stable to describe creators around the world — the only way to create.

充电产品的 7 系：Prime

Steven: Some categories are relatively easy to make 7-series. Charging, for example. In 2020, the most expensive charger on the market — a power bank — was only $50–60. We had to do it. When we launched 7-series we thought: make it really extreme. In this field we have the strongest R&D. The team makes progress every year. The most extreme product in the industry.

Our Anker charging line is called Prime — this is the 7-series product line. Average price over $100. A stable 7-series position.

But for other categories like headphones, the 7-series is already occupied by Apple AirPods. You have to be as good as them or better — a very long, very difficult process. We spent several years on it. By this year, when this show airs, I believe the product we released should be done.

与爱普生 CEO 的对话再谈

Xiaojun: You raised a few questions — how many categories to enter, why, which not to enter. How do you make these choices today?

Steven: Honestly, most categories today were inherited. Let me tell an interesting true story.

We make UV printers. The nozzle is provided by Epson. In February this year, Epson's CEO came to visit. He introduced their technology first — Epson made Romax technology, derived 1, 2, 3 categories from it. When it came to me, I said: we did charging, audio-video, home automation. He sincerely said: "Yang-san, you're so awesome, you do so many categories."

I said: combining my understanding today, I must be very ashamed. The more ideal way: today you have a good underlying technology, and based on it you expand to 1, 2, or 3 categories.

做芯片的故事：存算一体

Xiaojun: You've done a lot of things for me recently. I feel very uneasy. For example, you released a chip. How was this decision made?

Steven: Made three years ago. The value guided us. We're in the audio-video field — we need good acoustic algorithms. In 2021 we established our acoustic algorithm team.

Noise reduction has several steps: 1. Beamforming — figure out where the sound comes from 2. Reduce background noise 3. Echo cancellation 4. Voice enhancement

Ten years ago these were mathematical models, programs. In 2021 we started converting to deep-learning models on sub-problems.

By 2023, ChatGPT came out. We firmly believed: the future is an era of end-to-end problem solving.

The first 80 years of computers were the divide-and-conquer era — divide a big problem into many small problems, divide down further. Search was natural language processing, divided into sub-problems, divided further. A team of thousands inside.

After ChatGPT, we should no longer use divide-and-conquer. We move from divide-and-conquer to end-to-end. End-to-end essentially is: first learn from data, then reinforce in the real world. Data learning + reinforcement learning constitutes the paradigm of future learning.

Applied to acoustics: throw away all programs and small models of divide-and-conquer; replace with a large end-to-end model. (Large here is on a completely different scale than language models.)

When you run this model in wearables like headphones — our hardware conditions at the time could run a model with hundreds of K parameters. Huge power consumption. The model can't run in the headset.

Why? Sound is divided into many time slices per second. Process each slice — encode into dozens of tokens, pass through all parameters of the model. Every second, every frame — move all model parameters from memory to NPU once. This move accounts for the largest power consumption.

So our challenge: we can make a few-megabyte, significantly-better end-to-end model — but it actually cannot run in the headset chip. Because hundreds of inferences per second × parameter moves × too much movement = power consumption too high. We could only downgrade to 300K or 500K. Performance greatly affected.

冯·诺依曼架构与"脑"的类比

Steven: Back to values: you want to make a bigger model to solve the problem end-to-end. Today's chips can't run it because they have to keep moving data. So the very natural method is — can I stop moving data?

If you go back, the architecture of computers in the first 80 years is the von Neumann architecture — separation of storage and calculation. Put program and code in memory, do calculation in CPU. von Neumann + divide-and-conquer + software files (programs and data) — these three form a stable triangle. At any point in time you only need to run a small part of the program.

But in the end-to-end era, this stable triangle should be broken. The problem-solving method is no longer divide-and-conquer; it's data learning, reinforcement learning, end-to-end. What you produce is not millions of lines of code, but a neural network. Even with MoE (Mixture of Experts), activated parts represent millions or billions of parameters.

With von Neumann architecture, every calculation requires pouring all parameters from memory to CPU. Natural efficiency is very low.

Analogy: you store all knowledge in your left brain. In divide-and-conquer, every time you solve a problem, only a small part of knowledge/code is used — move that part to right brain and calculate. Seemed reasonable.

But today your knowledge is no longer broken into fine pieces; it's a whole — an end-to-end model. When solving each problem, you need to move the entire left brain (or a large part) to the right brain to calculate. Starting to become unreasonable, inefficient.

Look at the human brain: storage is your model parameters in your neurons. The same neurons play the role of calculation. The brain doesn't carry any parameters when calculating — naturally more efficient.

So we separate from von Neumann's separated storage-calculation, convert to a storage-calculation integrated architecture — like a brain. Power consumption significantly reduced. Now we can run a model with large parameters on small devices like headphones.

这颗芯片的具体场景

Xiaojun: How big are the parameters of your model?

Steven: Less than 2 million. But for a model running inside a headset, this is already huge.

Xiaojun: Why headphones first?

Steven: Start from consumer pain points. Use the best headphones today — make a call in a noisy place, at the airport — the other side hears your voice with the airport background. Or in video conferences — someone comes up, background noise is noisy, vocals unclear.

The best 7-series headphones today should be AirPods Pro. Some say Sony or Bose. Test call effects in noisy environments — actually very challenging. Background noise often not filtered; if forcibly filtered, vocals become incomplete, unnatural.

To effectively filter background noise while keeping voice clear and natural — you must need a large model. Million parameters. Must run inside the headset. Must have storage-calculation integrated architecture.

Look around — no one in the world had made such a chip. We searched the market for the strongest company in that field and customized with them.

Project started in August 2023. Today April–May 2026. Three years. The product equipped with this chip is finally on the market.

Effect: in any noisy environment, accurately separate your vocals from ambient noise. Plus soft call — you sit and speak softly; the other party hears clearly. Soft calls + extreme noise reduction — this essentially represents the first application of a new problem-solving paradigm.

未来：从知觉到大脑

Xiaojun: Will this chip be used in other scenarios in the future?

Steven: Call noise reduction is one scenario. When you can run a million-parameter model in a very limited power-consumption environment — many more features.

We've seen small-parameter models like Qwen 7B — highly intelligent. In the future, different scales are needed: - Super brain in cloud: trillions to tens of trillions of parameters, solving the hardest problems - Brain on terminal: billions to tens of billions of parameters, real-time on-site - Perception/control organs: millions to tens of millions of parameters

Like humans: the brain is a deep neural network. Cerebellum is different magnitude. Eyes, ears, muscles — controlled by deep neural networks one by one. Eyes have optic nerves that process visible light; only a small amount sent to brain. Brain doesn't control every cell directly — muscles have nerve cells that control specific movement.

So in the future, different sensing/control organs should match the complexity of their problems with appropriately-scaled neural networks. Eye nerves, ear nerves vs brain nerves — differ by orders of magnitude.

(One school says: don't dismantle these nerves individually; collect all sensor raw data and import to a large model. We don't think it's the best way long-term — frequencies and complexities differ.)

So these small models should run on independent hardware in different places. Including the embodied brain — billions of neural parameters. Should also run on storage-calculation chips.

Currently our chip can only run nervous system. But we believe along this road, it will reach billions or tens of billions of parameters — carrying a brain.

自用 vs 外卖：苹果模式

Xiaojun: The storage-calculation chip — is it for your own use or for everyone?

Steven: For our own use. Completely customized from our model — operators and structures adapted to our model. Our own "little CUDA." If I sold this chip to others, I'd essentially be giving them the model — I'd become a chip company.

Look at Apple's A-series, M-series; Huawei's Kirin series — used only in their own hardware. From a closed-loop perspective, this generates the best user value (best performance) and best business value. Selling the chip alone vs putting it in your own device — the latter generates much greater value.

Inference side is more suitable for storage-calculation. Different model scales. Trillion-level — savings hard to achieve. We start with millions, then scale to billions and tens of billions over the next few years. Inference future is competitively storage-calculation.

Our chip total: 4 trillion parameters. Different types. Maximum single model around 2 trillion.

Long-term vision: a trillion-level super-brain at home, costing a few thousand dollars to deploy. In each embodied robot and complex device — billion-parameter brains. In various devices — large numbers of perception/control neurons.

"智能家居"其实是"可调家居"

Steven: We always talk about "smart home." Are our things smart?

For example, your toilet has more than a dozen buttons. Smart? Not smart. It's a function — actually adjustability. Including today's "smart home systems" — set in advance, always like that. So today's so-called smart home is essentially adjustable, preset furniture.

Products have three stages: 1. Non-adjustable 2. Adjustable (a dozen buttons grew suddenly — but not smart) 3. Self-adjusting — sense what you're doing, adjust by itself

Analogy: a stool. Non-adjustable stool → ergonomic chair with a dozen adjustments (but you can't adjust well, don't think to adjust until your back hurts) → truly smart stool that senses you, retreats when you come, pushes forward, lays back when you play games.

So the home in the future should be: perceive what you do, plan, control itself.

Xiaojun: Why isn't our home really smart in 2026?

Steven: No model on them. To achieve true intelligence requires three abilities: perception, planning, control. Plus hardware below: sensors, controllers. Plus software/models above.

These earliest matured in self-driving cars — perception/planning/control is the typical three-step process of autonomous driving. After maturing inside cars, it's time to overflow — sensors below, model capabilities above — to all products in our homes. After remaking, products become truly smart.

All non-adjustable or human-adjusted consumer products can be remade.

AI 时代的核心技术

Steven: This will make us rethink: what should our underlying core technology be?

The story I told about Epson — that company has a history driven by core technology. We didn't have that. But today, we clearly see such a core technology: perception, planning, control — from chip to model to application stack. When polished and mature, this becomes the company's core technology.

Why don't others have it? Logically everyone can. But from chip to model — many obstacles. First cognitive challenge. Plus very hard work to do it.

第一款 AI 时代的产品

Xiaojun: Is this headset your first product for the AI era?

Steven: You may not know much about our security system. We're the second-largest security system in the world. Looking at the high-end market (>$500 systems), we have over half the market share.

Security comes equipped with a local large model — we built a sub-2B model running on our security base station. It processes all your videos locally. Your videos don't have to leave home. You don't have to look for videos — you can ask via text. "Is my daughter back?" "Where's the cat?" "Where's the dog?" AI really falling on your family.

Our company has nearly 300 AI-related engineers — 260–270. Started recruiting AI in 2017 for security. Need AI for person detection, motion detection.

Xiaojun: Yingqi (Chairman of Step Star) told me the last generation of AI companies all fought security and were defeated by domestic security giants. How did you develop overseas?

Steven: We do consumer-grade security — not industrial-grade like Hikvision/Dahua. In consumer there are giants too — Amazon acquired Ring. They rely on Amazon's cloud, do 3-series at low price — security where all data is uploaded to cloud.

As a Chinese company, we want sensitivity, and to differentiate from Ring. So we make security that doesn't call cloud. Place a base station in user's home: - Long-distance WiFi connection — can install cameras far away, still send data back - Local storage - Local AI analysis

Launched 2018. Successful, at least from market results.

AI Native 是不是第一性？

Xiaojun: Looking at observations from the past, how do you understand AI? What changes will AI bring to the consumer electronics industry?

Steven: Customers don't care about AI. Customers care about better experience.

The clear path: AI empowers your devices. Devices today need adjusting — in the future they really adapt to you. It knows what you're doing, adjusts itself. You use it almost without feeling.

Xiaojun: It becomes a living thing.

Steven: A service entity. But not "alive" — embodied robots and dogs won't have independent will. A self-adjusting chair as a living thing? A bit exaggerated.

We'll add AI to existing hardware. Like 10 years ago — IoT was emerging, everyone said all hardware should be redone. But what happened over 10 years: those with solid scenes — companies that can evolve — beat those who only had IoT tech. The side with scenes wins more.

So AI native is not the first principle. Customers really care: how do I feel more comfortable, efficient, happier?

To change a category needs three things: 1. Leading technology 2. Real scenes — productization capability + understanding 3. Good global business capability

For us, business is strong. In areas needing change, we lack technology. So 2021–2022 — we still lack tech. There's no perfect company. So past few years — AI team grew, chip team built. Today many things need doing that no one has done yet.

My main purpose coming to this show: tell our vision so more people join to make this vision reality.

AI 与具身智能投入

Xiaojun: How much investment are you willing to make in AI chips, embodied intelligence?

Steven: Hard to give a total. From specific problems we need to solve. We most likely won't train trillion-level models. Today's training: from millions to tens of millions parameters; embodied — billion-level. Investment probably hundreds of millions.

AI broadly hard to count. Over 200 engineers × hundreds of thousands per capita = much, much money.

机器人业务

Xiaojun: Tell us about your robot business.

Steven: Robots — three forms: 1. 2D planar: sweepers, lawn mowers (2.5D: pool robots). First stage. 2. 3D mobile/interactive: robot dogs, pets. Second stage. 3. Operating robots: humanoid. Final crown jewel.

Stages 1, 2, 3 — we're doing.

Humanoid: pre-research. The tech stack hasn't converged at all. Brain state, body, waist joints — everyone's solutions completely different. Not yet productizable.

Dogs (Stage 2): each company's forms are close. Hardware tech stack relatively converged. Agent runs on a model — basically consistent approaches. Should be ready for productization. Started a team last year.

What role does a dog play in our life today? Pets — emotional value. But when dogs first entered families they were caretakers — functional value. Today's pet dogs lost functional value largely.

We make security systems in millions of 7-series homes worldwide. Problem: your home is full of cameras and alarms, but if someone breaks in, very little you can do.

When you have a guard dog — security detects intruder → dog drives them away → closed loop of real user value.

User research: many 7-series users are willing to spend a considerable amount for a family guard dog. We're sure it can be sold in large quantities. Creates particularly clear customer value.

浅海战略与超级品类

Xiaojun: Why shift from shallow sea to deep sea — has the attitude changed?

Steven: "Deep" doesn't mean category complexity. Deep represents category scale — over $50B annual sales. Like Texas Hold'em blind size — millions to tens of millions RMB (shallow) vs 500M-1B (deep).

Watch dogs / security — total market most likely under $50B globally. Still shallow sea. Shallow sea strategy continues.

Will you go into deep sea? Two visible future deep seas: 1. Humanoid robots — tech stack hasn't converged. Dog products extend our tech into that field. Hope to reach the convergence point. 2. Smart glasses — the 4th personal interactive terminal. Three waves of giants competing: phone makers, internet giants, model companies. They want to grab the entry point. To enter you must beat all three waves. So we won't do smart glasses today.

Deep sea, if entered, most likely not by current company. Spin off — like Huawei's mobile business spun off from operator business.

Xiaojun: Is this something you must do?

Steven: Not 100% must. But when time is right, when we have the ability, with the right leader — we're willing to challenge.

产品方法论

Xiaojun: Summarize your product methodology in concise language.

Steven: Complex system behind. If one sentence: look at segmented user groups; create unique value.

Going back to Steve Jobs returning to Apple in 1996 — facing dozens of products. He divided users into two groups (professional vs ordinary), two scenes (mobile vs fixed). Four product slots. Each niche — unique value.

For all product people, if only one principle: segmented people in definite scenes, create unique value.

Xiaojun: Have you experienced product failure? Win rate?

Steven: After 2022 contraction, we never reduced categories again. More cautious now. Clearer principles:

Start from both ends: - People: users who already bought your product, basic understanding and trust - Technology: underlying tech stack, common and extreme innovation

Best category to enter: high overlap with existing users + high overlap with tech stack. Different people + different tech = high challenge.

把公司当作产品

Xiaojun: Are you the genius-product-manager type?

Steven: My product sense is good. But consumer electronics is endless — 10 years ago new products, today new, 10 years later new. I'm a long-term person.

What product to make that can be used forever? Today I regard this company as my product. A platform where many young product/tech creators continue to create. The platform itself is a product. Difficult and challenging — far bigger than making a specific product.

Realized in 2020 strategic discussions — how many categories we've done, will do, in how many fields.

Three (now four) fields: - Charging & energy storage - Audio-video - Home automation - Health (incubating)

Each field — 10–20 categories normal. Total maybe 40–60 categories. The third-category company.

长价值链的难度

Steven: Consumer electronics — long value chain. 200 different positions. With limited time, money, opportunities you must polish most to "basically usable." Difficulty rises non-linearly with chain length — every dot communicates with a patch of dots.

Look at hardware startup success rate. Internet era of smart hardware — 10 years ago — many invested companies, very few alive and well today.

In AI era, creators face choices. Option 1: build value chain from scratch. Option 2: join a mature platform like ours with precipitated intelligence. Then solve distribution — distribute most creation fruits to creators themselves.

好产品经理

Xiaojun: What's a good product manager?

Steven: A good PM is first dedicated to delivering customer value — not broad customer value. Must focus on specific group + specific scene + unique value.

Opposite of Internet products. Taobao, Douyin — not selective, all groups. Small/medium hardware — must select people and scenes.

We have lessons from failures. Background, academic credentials, even interview process — not enough valid proof.

Why values? To test creativity. Most effective: 1. First-principle thinking — your past products, what made you different, derivation 2. Strive for perfection — overcome huge difficulties 3. Long-termism + self-awareness + self-evolution + continuous learning ("growing together")

These are the qualities of best creators globally. When you want to do something 0 to 1 — you need these.

创业者为什么来安克？

Xiaojun: If they can start their own business, why come to Anker?

Steven: Consumer electronics — long value chain. Building it means probability of success is challenging.

After AI, will this change? Not easily — there are people in the value chain. So our platform may be a better way. Anker is already a global consumer brand. Global channels help commercialize good products.

上市的回顾

Xiaojun: If you hadn't done financing/capitalization, what would Anker be today?

Steven: Financing and listing give external pressure. But we're not a group driven by external pressure. Dongping and I would still reach today's path.

Xiaojun: Bad effects on retention if not listed?

Steven: Good question. Bonus distribution — we already distribute large part of current profits to employees. But after listing, share realization distributes profits of next 5–10 years. Big attraction.

When we listed, China regulation: unlisted company ≤ 200 shareholders. We had over 170 employee shareholders that year.

Some left after enough money — reconsidered life. But bigger ratio: not about money, but whether this place can keep him growing and happy.

Interesting story: a finance/economics classmate. After IPO felt he had enough. Resigned. Went to Central Academy of Drama to study screenwriting. Worked as screenwriter for years. Then came back. Found screenwriting wasn't fun. Missed us, came back.

上市选择

Xiaojun: Now for AI hardware companies, profitable companies — when should one consider listing?

Steven: Two perspectives.

Employee perspective: almost always want IPO. Without listing, hard to liquidate shares.

Founder perspective: different. Taiwan has many 80-year-old chairmen of listed companies. Went public at 40. Company still here decades later. But didn't grow big. Family won't take over. Colleagues can't pick up. Even though you enjoyed ringing the bell at 40, when you're older you still have to pay for this company.

Listed and locked in for 10/20/30 years — give the company a long-term path.

Cannot the second generation? If company is on stable smooth road, maybe a second generation or professional manager can. But if path of change (category changing, AI wave) — second generation or professional manager really can do this well?

Xiaojun: What are the benefits of not being listed?

Steven: Without listing, more exit options — sell to a larger third-category company. Look at P&G, Texas Instruments — growth via M&A bringing new categories. China's current environment isn't particularly suitable for M&A; everyone still thinks IPO is better.

When we listed, we thought clearly: we can use systematic approach across many categories, become a third-category company. Listing was an intermediate process — relatively certain.

Xiaojun: Why isn't DJI listed?

Steven: Ask Wang Tao himself.

For employees in unlisted company — we issued stock options to first wave; they got rich and free. For the next wave, no individual liquidation. So we drew an equity pool for new categories. When new category passes investment period and generates profits, per listed-company rules we buy back employee shares — give good returns.

The best creators want opportunity first — challenge something difficult and never done. Then growth — when company supports them, helps them grow. Then rewards. And finally meaning — you also want a place to brag at dinners, talk to children, feel parents are proud.

A good company provides creators: opportunity, growth, reward, meaning. The best company sustains this 2, 3, 4, 5 times — turning him into a powerful person. If he starts a business again, higher success rate.

护城河三层

Xiaojun: What's the moat of a consumer electronics company today?

Steven: Three layers:

Layer 1 — unique leading technology. Only you can do it at this stage. Effective moat. But technology hits diminishing marginal benefits — perception is analog with an upper limit. When tech reaches perception limit, no room to advance; others catch up.

Examples: - Apple's screen — more than 10 years ago, everyone thought iPhone's screen was better. Today? Apple itself coined "Retina" — beyond a resolution, normal eyes can't distinguish. - Chargers — make 140W small as a card. Twice smaller still makes sense? Smaller still — doesn't make much sense.

Technology surplus gets wiped out over long enough time. First principle.

Layer 2 — brand. Trust in users' hearts. The emotion when users think of you. Very hard to build, easy to lose. Consumers may need many positive experiences to trust your brand — but you may do wrong once or twice and lose trust forever.

Layer 3 — mission, vision, values, and a group of people who truly believe them. Look at Nokia, Sony, Philips at certain points — brands still trusted, but probability of choosing them isn't high. Final question: does this company still have a group of people maintaining extreme innovation, confident in first principles, seeking perfection and growing together? If this group stays for 10, 20, 30 years — that's the longest moat.

Anker 三层板分别在哪？

Steven: Test by our own example. Each layer is from 0 to 1, gradually approaching.

Tech (Layer 1): Since 2023 with the lab. Storage-calculation chip is one result. Maybe 0.2–0.3. Huge space remaining.

Brand (Layer 2): 15 years of hard work. Anker's global brand trust is quite high.

Example: February this year released household energy-storage products. Customer price $10,000. No offline stores. Just released — buy on official website. Imagine — $10K spend online, sight unseen. First month: 1,000+ customers placed orders online. Trusted brand.

Brand is our longest board — maybe 0.6–0.7.

Mission/values (Layer 3): When we started transforming in 2020, proportion and concentration of "right people" was quite low. Past few years, painful change. Many old colleagues left after IPO; we brought in many creators. Quantitative to qualitative change. Past two years faster. Because we speak out — what kind of company Anker wants to be, what environment we provide creators, what rewards.

But just talking about vision sounds empty. Eventually products speak. Examples from last year: - UV printer — first consumer-grade in this category. Industrial-equipment-sized device shrunk to suitcase size on a table. - Storage-calculation chip + headset using it.

These are real creator outputs.

I hope when I'm on this show again in 2 years — let our products speak; let our creators in each field stand on stage proudly telling their own creation stories. That opportunity should be theirs, not mine.

文化：层层授权

Xiaojun: Is your company bottom-up or top-down culture?

Steven: Hierarchical authorization culture. Each layer makes its own decisions, delegating maximally.

CEO decides: what the company looks like, strategic/competency/organizational/talent frameworks, return design, which big macro categories. Business department decides: specifically how to do in that category. Product line decides: specific product details.

Many opportunities to be independent across three tiers. Best growth for creators is being independent.

中国土壤里长出的全球品牌

Xiaojun: As a Shenzhen-grown company, why did you build a good brand especially in European and American markets?

Steven: Brand legacy is trust with customers. Every decision should accumulate user trust. The first principle.

Tested by big decisions. Last year we recalled millions of power banks globally. Economic loss hundreds of millions. You could choose not to recall. Result: many users face overheating, even burning risk. We started from user value — just do it. After repeated discussion and debate.

When this happens at every level, output is a trustworthy brand. Whenever we find a problem violating principles, we correct it. Probabilistically — when we work hard most of the time to earn trust of most users — eventually we created a trustworthy brand.

AI 中台

Xiaojun: You established an AI middle platform. Do you spend a lot of time on AI? How do you learn?

Steven: My background is in AI. After this big wave, I firmly believe in its future. Past six months — much time spent here.

Started September last year. Internal tokens (not consumer service tokens) — by airing should exceed 10 trillion. About 150 billion daily internally. Rising rapidly. 6,000 people → ~25M tokens/person/day → ~500–600M tokens/month.

Top models: ~1/3. Mid models: ~40–50%. Entry models: ~20%.

Used not just for code — every functional line in the company uses AI today.

Not opening various accounts on various platforms. Built a middle platform — connected various models, frontend provides chat, local command line, web page, various access. Everyone in company uses AI on same platform.

OpenClaw / real-time orchestration: tells him a requirement, compiles a process. Challenge: temporary, unstable, doesn't fully understand enterprise data.

So enterprise-level applications should be largely pre-programmed agents — integrating business process and data into the intelligent agent, deterministically generating expected value. Huge difference from personal AI use today.

Xiaojun: Was there management objection to all-employees unlimited tokens?

Steven: Many asked how we measure output. "Because you believe, you see." Up to now, not strictly measured what tokens produced. We believe future organizations must be AI-enhanced organizations. Start early.

This year's spending: several hundred million.

Xiaojun: What's the upper limit?

Steven: Matched to value. If we create good customer value and earn well, no upper limit.

AI Native 组织

Xiaojun: What's your view on AI native organizations?

Steven: What's an AI native organization? Like AI native hardware — there must be unchanging things. Think clearly about those, then how AI changes them.

For organizations, what's unchanged: 1. User value — make users happy and satisfied 2. Value chain — to create user value, you go through these points one by one. Hardware: user research → product definition → tech R&D → quality → manufacturing → marketing → sales → service. Even with AI, these value points still exist. 3. Scrums — small collaborative teams delivering each point.

So: - Scrums won't disappear. They will shrink. Software: traditional Scrum 8–10 roles → AI integrates roles → 1 person plays several → smaller team. Hardware: harder for AI to suddenly let an electrical engineer draw structure diagrams or do embedded code — physical world experience required. Hardware Scrums change less or maintain size. - Internal Scrum operation changes — AI defines goals, dismantles projects/OKRs, helps manage and complete. Higher efficiency. - Cross-Scrum collaboration becomes more efficient.

Xiaojun: You're transforming now. How's progress?

Steven: After half a year, clear view of the path. Hopefully complete transformation by year-end. Great if achieved.

中层管理与初级岗

Xiaojun: Some think middle managers become more important; some think they'll disappear.

Steven: Jack Dorsey's famous article: company controlled by world model, eliminate middle managers. Honestly — today's model context length is unlikely capable.

Context length analysis: a project needs N tokens. A Scrum running several projects — more. A department with several Scrums — more. Whole company — over 1 billion, tens of billions, possibly trillions. Today's model: context over 1M tokens — capabilities lost. You can say "compress," but what's left? This is a value choice — models can't do it well yet.

Conway's law: organizational shape determines output. Who defines organizational form? Not CEO alone — managers cultivate organization layer by layer.

So middle managers won't disappear due to AI — may become more powerful.

What disappears: hope the last to disappear are repeated boring jobs.

Junior positions: Many think AI eliminates juniors → no seniors in future. False assumption — that seniors must grow from juniors.

Counter-example: construction. Junior — building walls, plastering, operational. Senior — architectural design. Ancient times: architects did rise from frontline craftsmen (10 years building walls). Modern: architectural design is its own category — don't need to build walls first.

Future: in a field, you can use AI to deliver complete solutions for customers — skip traditional juniors. School admissions today: students with zero programming experience use AI to support business and deliver complete systems.

So I'm not worried — no senior talent in future.

N 倍人 / 大新人 vs 小新人
