What does the consequences on a let's say short to medium-term basis look like globally and what are the ripple effects of that on the global economy? Right, I mean we've also already seen the stress in the bond market — but what else can we expect?

Lynn：财政主导 + 供给短缺 = 真正的通胀

Well, so on average when you have fiscal dominance, when you have money supply growth uh it's all easier uh for policy makers and for the public uh if you have productivity growth, if you have disinflation from better technology, abundant uh energy and materials and all of that — because you have all that inflationary money supply growth, but then it's offset by just things are getting more organized and there's abundance of the things we need.

When you have that money supply growth, but then you also have shortages in the things you need, that's when you tend to feel that inflation more and it shows up more in actual price increases.

美国页岩油的"过剩时代"已结束

Um, and you know, for a while the world was in a state of of energy oversupply. Um uh just because you know US shale oil came online very rapidly uh for a number of years uh and that kind of threw a wrench into OPEC uh and then OPEC would would you know try to curtail some of their potential supply mainly Saudi Arabia. um uh and the Strait of Hormuz kind of comes along and challenges that whole notion.

战略储备终将耗尽

um and so uh you know in the near term I don't think anything major because I mean it's already reflected in higher energy prices that leads to a little bit of demand destruction, that leads to a little bit more of that kind of risk premium put into energy.

Uh I continue to be long energy securities. Uh many of them have good balance sheets. Um you know, they could make money at $80 oil. They make even more money at say $120 oil if it goes there and stays there or higher. um uh and they often move inversely to other types of equities uh because they can benefit from some of the chaos that other equities are punished by and sometimes vice versa.

Um the longer this goes on as I mentioned the risk becomes that there were existing stockpiles — all the tankers around the oceans had existing oil in them uh and other products in them. Uh some countries like the US and China uh have strategic reserves. Some countries mandate uh the private sector maintain reserves like Japan has you know certain mandates on how many months of just kind of usage that they need to have available.

Uh and the longer this goes on, some of those existing uh formal or informal stockpiles diminish uh and that diminishing stockpile you know that offsets obviously the lack of new production that's coming out of the Strait. Uh but once those stockpiles are drawn down you don't have that offset anymore. Uh and so if we reach a point where those reserves aren't going down anymore, uh but there's still not product coming out of the Strait, uh you could get another wave higher in prices and potentially another wave of shortages, energy curfews uh and things like that.

韩国例外：科技股与本地经济的脱钩

Um and you know, I don't think it affects like the best performing stocks out there. They're going to trade on their own merits. uh you know — I remember they were looking at South Korea and they're saying well they import a lot of energy so maybe their stock market's going to suffer — but their stock market is mainly a handful of of tech stocks that are completely decoupled from whatever happens to Korea's economy specifically.

Um so it doesn't always show up in stock prices per se. Uh but it's not an accident that we have record low consumer sentiment in the US. Uh people are already pained by these higher energy prices. uh given everything else uh and if we have another wave higher uh in but in developed and especially in developing countries uh people are going to be squeezed and there's going to be less offset.

um so I'm in no position to uh try to estimate when the trade's going to open uh like you mentioned there's a deal there's no deal there's a deal there's no deal it's all headline risk so I don't try to predict the odds that things are going to still be closed in another 94 days uh but we can kind of map out that should they still be closed — there is a risk of another leg higher in prices and shortages.

UAE 退出 OPEC：卡特尔的式微

And this obviously has repercussions also on other assets, on commodities as I mentioned also earlier on, but the cost of food right as well. Um Japan I've also seen a chart come across as well recently that also had like a low um hitting a low point in terms of imports of oil that also reaching a critical point for their own industry as well that dropped about 20% in the last weeks.

Um but just in general um what are let's say the implications now from the fact that on OPEC side the UAE for example uh left after 60 years being part of the OPEC uh built um like also as part of this alliance um what do you make out of out of this and the impact it has on oil?

Well, UAE's been trying to uh they've had a goal of growing their production for a long time. OPEC has been somewhat of a constraint on that. Um so they've kind of finally uh broken away. Uh that'll leave the remaining OPEC as increasingly kind of uh Saudi Arabia centered.

Um uh and they're the major swing producer. Um I mean in general uh you know like cartels are unsustainable. uh that was a particularly you know it's been a particularly long-lasting and effective one uh and I don't think it's going away but I think it is diminishing uh over time first with the rise of US shale uh and then of course uh you know with members leaving and things like that.

um all else being equal uh you know a diminished OPEC uh is you know negative for prices because it means that there's less organization to take a certain amount of oil off the market to keep prices up at a certain level. There's a little bit more free-for-all competition. Uh but in general, I don't — you know, I think we're getting past the point where there's tons to spare capacity. We don't have just US shale uh production just keep linearly rising like it was before. Uh we're back toward a more equilibrium stage uh and a handful of million barrels of oil uh per day uh really matter.

食物价格：比 2022 年慢，但发展中国家仍有风险

Um, so I do think that over the long run is a bearish force, but that's up against the fact that there's the bullish force of US shale oil's no longer rising. Obviously, then the more immediate one is the Strait — if that's open or closed. I mean, that affects everything else. But say let's get past that. Let's say it reopens at some point. It's really about that dynamic between US shale and existing OPEC production.

Um, and I, you know, it's not that I expect major spikes anytime soon other than the Strait. Uh, but I do think that energy is not going to be as abundant, say 5 years — like in terms of oversupply, 5 years from now, as it was uh in say the late 2010s, the early 2020s, um, uh, you know, outside of the Russian invasion of Ukraine when we had that brief energy spike.

Um the good thing about food shortages — I mean so most food prices is still below that 2022 peak and that's because when Russia invaded Ukraine, I mean that's also a big bread basket area. So let alone all the potential oil disruption that came from that and energy disruption for Europe uh like LNG uh and natural gas in general. Um that was also a big disruption for food.

So right now we have a slower disruption for food in the sense that you know a lot of fertilizer and fertilizer components are tied to the Strait uh but it's not quite that immediate impact that we saw in 2022. Uh I mean in Egypt for example like things always ripple farther than you think — like Egypt had basically a food price crisis uh in 2022 and it was in large part because they get a lot of their grain from Ukraine.

Um and so at least this isn't quite causing those types of issues yet. Uh so there's more time for economies and supply chains to adjust. Uh it's kind of like a train wreck that everyone kind of sees coming uh far out and therefore they can kind of prepare for it more so than they could in 2022.

Um but uh basically it does put upward pressure on food prices which is less relevant for the developed world. I mean it is relevant uh when people's grocery bills go up. Um but people aren't going to really go hungry for the most part. Uh the bigger risk there is for developing countries where you have you know maybe one-tenth of the GDP per capita, 1/5th of the GDP per capita. There's a bigger ratio of income spent on food and there's more risk of people literally just being without calories uh because prices have gone up and shortages have gone up. uh I don't expect in the near term but it is a risk to keep watching should the Strait remain uh closed for an indefinite period of time.

五、AI 股、能源与估值脱节

And now I'd like to tie this back to stocks, energy and AI. Lynn：差距存在，但没那么大

韩国 KOSPI 暴涨的真相：只有两只股票

Um, and you know, I think that when talking about South Korea, I mean, that's really driven by two stocks. It was Samsung and SK Hynix who were responsible for the vast majority of that rise and that's really one product — RAM.

Um, so of course the early stage of AI was GPUs, uh, just a much higher demand for GPUs. As AI kind of evolved from not just chat bots but to agents, uh, there's more persistence of memory there. So, they need a lot more RAM. So that's when we saw this like massive increase in RAM usage and certain other types of chips. Uh that's where South Korea really benefited. It was generally in line with earnings and revenue growth. um uh because there really is demand for their products.

真正的泡沫在哪里？

Um and you know, while some of these stocks will get ahead of themselves, uh I have more concern around like the unprofitable AI models themselves and their trillion-dollar valuations more so than the hyperscalers and more so than the chip producers. Um but basically I you know a lot of it despite the speed being surprising does make sense.

Um, and you know, sometimes bubbles, uh, it's not that they fall back down, but they just start going sideways for a long period of time. And that can certainly happen to many of these industries as they kind of already front-run a lot of what's going to happen for the next few years.

六、Michael Burry 的指控与 AI 商业模式

So you well that case of bubble — I guess you mentioned the AI bubble. um a lot of charts coming across. I can see like people putting on top of each other like the dot-com bubble with what we see in AI and saying, "Oh, see how it's fitting."

Um and then recently Michael Burry just said that Elon Musk and Nvidia's deal is built on fake numbers. I'm not sure if you've seen a few threads about that and more so that he calls this a bit of an entire structure of Fugazi where um he's alleging that billions of dollars in Nvidia chips are being hidden off balance sheets and that yeah um retirement funds — American retirees are owning, funding this whole thing.

Uh what do you think of all that? Do you think um Michael Burry has a good point in that?

Lynn：边际上他有道理，但我没那么悲观

Well, I think around the margins some of that is certainly true. uh you know he takes the more bearish case than I do personally. Um but you know when you're talking about trillion-dollar companies there's not a lot of margin of error. Uh they have to put really big numbers in order to not kind of fall back down to earth.

"卖 10 美元的 20 美元钞票"

Um the one of the challenges here is when you look around and say well who's making money — right? So um the chip producers are making money for the most part. Uh the AI companies are losing money. They're still venture funded. So it's still all this money is flowing in and funding a lot of this not out of the cash flows but out of the expectation that there will be cash flows.

And of course the challenge there when a company's in growth phase is they purposely underprice their product. You know they basically the business of selling $20 bills for $10 and they're saying look how big the numbers are. Look how many people want our product. And it's like well sure — if you're selling $20 bills for $10 you're going to have a lot of demand. The question is, how long can you keep doing that? How long can you keep convincing investors to give you money?

价格正常化 = 增长放缓

And sometimes they nail that transition. They get really big, they get really branding, they get the economies of scale, and then they're able to raise prices up to what they're supposed to be. Uh the challenge there is that once you kind of normalize prices, um you generally get slower growth because then you're no longer selling $20 bills for $10. There's less just outright demand for your like, you know, purposely underpriced product cuz it's not underpriced anymore. And then it starts to reflect reality.

So for a while now AI has been subsidized by these external investors uh where you get you know as a business user, as a personal user you get quite a lot of functionality for what is a pretty low price uh you know compared to everything else that you pay things for and eventually that price goes up to kind of reflect how much it actually costs to deliver that service.

Hyperscalers：高营收却无利润

Uh and then speaking of that, you have the hyperscalers. Uh you know, they uh for in the 2010s, the really big internet companies, um even though their capex numbers on paper were large in the absolute sense just because they're such big companies, they were pretty small as a share of revenue and as a share of market cap. So they were highly profitable. They didn't have to reinvest a ton of it. They were able to just stockpile huge cash balances. They were able to keep buying their own stock back year after year.

Um and but going forward they have to redeploy pretty much everything they're earning into their data center build-out into their chip acquisition. Um many of them are not anticipated to have free cash flows for the next 2-3 years. Um uh some of them have obviously turned to the bond market or turned to equity issuance rather than share reduction uh to fund a lot of this.

So, you know, they're not yet making money off of this. I mean, they are making like Google's reporting really great kind of cloud revenues from this. I mean, they're reporting revenues from this, but they're not really reporting profits from this yet because they have to reinvest that revenue that they're making.

谁在赚钱、谁在烧钱：一张图

So, the users are benefiting from an underpriced AI product. The chip producers are benefiting from just profitable growth. Uh whereas the AI companies and the hyperscalers are not yet profitable. Their revenue numbers are great. uh their valuation numbers are great. Um but the question is can — in order for this to be sustainable in the long run they also have to make money uh and they generally will make money at higher prices which will generally slow down growth.

历史类比：铁路、电气化、互联网、AI

Um, so it's not that I think the whole thing is fugazi per se, but that, you know, whenever you have a rapidly changing situation, whether it's the build-out of the railroads in the 1800s, whether it's electrification, uh, whether it's the rise of the computer industry, whether it's the rise of the internet, whether it's the rise of the mobile internet, whether it's the rise of, in this case, AI, um, things will get ahead of themselves from time to time and there will be like, you know, partial bubbles along the way, but at the end of the day, there's something real and new here.

Uh, it is worth trillions. How many trillions is up for debate, but it is worth trillions. Uh, and then the question is where does that go?

经济护城河在芯片，不在模型

I generally think that the economic moat is not really in the AI models themselves. Uh, there's pretty low switching costs that people can do once one is better than another, they can just switch to the other one. So they have to just constantly redeploy their wealth into kind of keeping their model as good as possible.

Um I think that chip producers do have pretty good economic moats. It's not easy just to come up with another, you know, GPU company or another RAM company or another company that makes the equipment that is used to make the GPUs and the RAM and everything like that. That is a pretty um constrained area. Um uh but you know I think in general you know a lot of — drill some pockets — are excessive.

"泡沫破裂在时间维度上，不在方向维度上"

So do you then uh think that it will take some time then until like this AI bubble like really pops? Is it the bubble in the first place? You mentioned yes but um maybe like some clarification on that and compared to also like the US stock valuations in general on the broadest sense uh AI stocks have been kind of driving the US stock in the last two years um and yeah has been like the big moat uh in that growth as well uh so what is let's say your analysis on that?

Well I think that the likely scenario is that the bubble pops not in terms of direction but in terms of time. Meaning that instead of, you know, a lot of these AI companies giving back all the gains they've made over the past year or two, uh I think they just will in general stop making the types of gains that they have been, they could give up some of their gains and then go sideways for a few years as the market kind of absorbs uh some of what has happened here.

Um you know, when you look at like RAM companies, um you know, back when Micron was under $500 a share, it had already risen a ton. And I had written at the time like you know this thing really could keep rising. I mean all this is — the numbers are real. Uh so it's not that I viewed that as a bubble per se and eventually you know now it's getting a little bit excessive but again a lot of that was driven by actual numbers.

真正的泡沫可能在 Costco

The ironic thing is I mean some of the bubbles show up in places that are not even tech — like Costco is trading at 50 times earnings. Uh you know uh and so that's in some cases more of a bubble than the really genuine rise in certain chip companies that are actually experiencing that explosive revenue and profits growth that kind of justifies the stock price increase.

Um so yeah my overall base case is that um over say the next five years the excessive returns we've seen in say South Korea or certain parts of the US market will slow down uh might give some of those back but they won't just completely revert to where they were kind of before the rise of AI and before the higher demand for GPUs and RAM uh and data centers uh and everything like that.

七、比特币：四年周期的"功能性死亡"

And so now I would like to uh discuss Bitcoin. Um Bitcoin has been — yeah, as I mentioned before — like seen about 50% from its peak. um and has been kind of going uh sideways since a few months especially now since the Hamas — the Strait of Hormuz crisis, the Iran war — uh which a lot of people would have expected it's actually quite a a good let's say performance considering it's a risk on asset.

But um there's a bit of a divide right between the correlation between the US stock market and let's say risk assets and Bitcoin, and Bitcoin hasn't been following that trend. Um what is your 360 now analysis of Bitcoin's performance in the last year and where do you see it headed?

Lynn：流动性相关性 + AI 的"最快马"竞争

Well, the short answer is I mean Bitcoin has this volatile track record. It has higher highs and higher lows along the way, but it has these really big washouts. Um, historically it's pretty correlated with liquidity. Um, at least 83% of the time it's correlated with kind of overall macro liquidity. About 17% of the time it's not. Right now it's kind of in a period where it's not.

Um, in addition, I mean, for a long time, Bitcoin was the fastest horse really uh that you could invest in. It was kind of the best performer. Uh, and the rise of AI companies has challenged that because there are other places to put capital uh that can give you a 10x return or that have given investors a 10x return. Uh, and so people say, well, why would I hold Bitcoin when I can hold a chip stock or something like that.

自托管的独特价值

Uh and of course the advantage of Bitcoin is that uh it gives you what a stock does not which is that you can self-custody it uh like gold uh and uh you know with advantages or disadvantages versus gold. I mean gold's obviously got the longer term track record.

Uh the challenge of course is that it's uh not as mobile uh without you know relying on custody. Uh you know I use the example that I live part of the year in the US. I live part of the year in Egypt. uh if I have self-custodial gold, I mean, there's going to be a time where I travel and it's just it's out of my personal possession at that point. Uh whereas you can set up Bitcoin in a multi-signature way so that if you travel the world, you still have ways to access self-custodial value that you can send around.

加密泡沫的清算：对 BTC 短期不利，长期有益

Um and so there is a use case there. Uh and but I think from a price standpoint, from a pure investor standpoint of just buying it and holding it and not really using it, um you know, when people can buy Bitcoin treasury companies, they can buy Bitcoin ETFs, they can buy Bitcoin on exchange, um there's they're comparing that price action to AI uh and I think AI — the rise of AI is kind of sucking a lot of the attention out of it.

Uh now eventually when that stops rising — like we talked before — you when that kind of say trends sideways for a period of time uh and Bitcoin is washed out I think it can reattract some of that capital.

The other big factor is that uh you know I've been bearish on the broad crypto space for many years. I've been bullish on Bitcoin and stable coins but bearish on virtually every other project uh in the space and a lot of that is really shaking out now. uh there's you know right now just kind of the overall use case of broad crypto is very stagnant.

um I think a lot of investors are kind of realizing there's no there there um and a lot of that capital is intertwined so as capital gets out of that broad crypto space it naturally uh in near-term hurts Bitcoin as well. um but I think when the dust kind of shakes out — Bitcoin and stable coins, you know, there's actually there there uh and it's natural for some of the air to come out of that broader crypto space uh and it's healthy in the long run even though it can be kind of painful in the near term.

四年周期：为什么不再相关

Do you also believe then still that the 4-year cycle that's also been — as you mentioned before — you mentioned liquidity before — it's also been linked a lot to the liquidity cycles. Do you think that it's still a relevant model at all or that it's still on track to actually fulfill that let's say uh way of behaving?

I don't think the four-year cycle is really functionally relevant anymore. Uh it still I think has some kind of mental relevance and that people if people expect something they can kind of be a self-fulfilling prophecy. Uh but I think for the past cycle or two it's not really mattered.

So earlier on the four-year cycle was really relevant because you had less coins on the market. You had more coins coming out with each block. And so a halving uh — that's what happens every four years roughly, a halving. So there's half as many coins being introduced into the market per block. Uh that really matters.

You know if you have a monetary network with say a 12% inflation supply inflation rate and it gets cut to 6% — that's a really big deal. Uh but after multiple halvings, Bitcoin now has a supply — like annual percentage supply growth — of less than 1%.

Uh and the vast majority of of kind of you know marginal buying and selling of coins — prices are mostly set by how much longer-term holders are selling and at what prices versus how much new demand is coming in. That factor is 10 times bigger than how many coins are hitting the market with every block um just because that number is so small now.

So when you have a say 8% annual supply growth and you eventually have a halving you cut that down to 4%. You know that's less relevant than is 5% of long-term held coins coming to market uh because the owners are concentrated and they want to kind of cash out. That's a much bigger factor than the halving.

当下的关键变量：流动性、贵金属、AI 注意力

So, uh I don't really view the four-year cycle as relevant. I still think liquidity is still relevant. Um I think you know the performance of precious metals and AI are relevant because obviously precious metals compete with Bitcoin on kind of the hard money front. So if those are rising a ton, it challenges Bitcoin in terms of competition.

Uh but then also tech stocks kind of challenge Bitcoin because Bitcoin is tech to some degree. Uh so when you have that fastest horse being AI that also challenges Bitcoin.

Um but you know at the end of the day it's I think the four-year cycle is less relevant. Uh and it's really just about the usefulness of the network and the network effects that the system still has.

八、比特币作为中性结算层：制裁与伊朗

Okay. That's pretty clear. Um and so would you then say that in general also the the neutral settlement layer that Bitcoin is supposed to serve — um had kind of an opportunity during this uh now coming back to the Hormuz uh the Strait of Hormuz crisis where the Iranian regime was saying that it would accept payments in crypto but also Bitcoin.

Um did you see any of that in terms of proof or any potential of that being even the case? I also know that on the other side though the US Treasury had sanctioned specific Bitcoin addresses before and making it then also on the Bitcoin front as well more uh — well, less sanction resistant as well. Um do you have any insights on that?

Lynn：稳定币 vs 比特币的"可没收性"

Well, one is that a lot of times you'll see headlines like "the Treasury seizes crypto" and people are like "look they seized Bitcoin" and it's like no no they said crypto. Uh so a lot of times I mean that's kind of the risk of stable coins uh is that especially stable coins can just be — you can — they're a centralized issuer so you can just go to centralized issuer and say hey freeze these tokens, we've identified that you know these are held by Iran or North Korea or whatever and they can be frozen.

Uh Bitcoin while it you know around the margins it can be taken. I mean, if you find a way to get someone's private keys, if someone stores their private keys online, uh, or you're able to enter their place and take them, uh, you can seize Bitcoin. But much like seizing a nation's gold or an individual's gold, it there's a cost of doing that. Either either war in terms of a country or arrest or intrusion as far as an individual.

Um, and so, without the private keys, you can't get someone's Bitcoin. Uh, and some of the headlines, I think, confuse that for some people that aren't kind of just well-versed on how the cryptography works and how the system works. Um, and there is a difference between how Bitcoin potentially can be seized versus how stable coins and certain other cryptos can be seized.

伊朗的出口难题

Uh, there was some evidence that Iran did turn to Bitcoin payments. Um, the question is what — you know, once they get the payments, what can they do with the Bitcoin? They can't just go on to Coinbase and say "hey we're Iran, we want to go ahead and sell our Bitcoin for dollars." Uh that's where that off-ramp uh runs into issues.

Now Bitcoin is a global market uh and it's not just like a stock where it might trade primarily on one exchange. Um, Bitcoin is this decentralized thing that can trade on multiple exchanges. There are gradual ways that Iran can offload their Bitcoin for other types of value. Uh, but there are frictions with it.

Uh, generally speaking, the bigger the network gets and the more trading hubs there are for it, the easier it gets to offload it, right? So um, the less kind of dollar-centric it becomes, uh, it'd be easier for regimes like that to offload it.

Um uh but at the current time I mean I think that they have to be mindful of size uh that they can offload some uh but they can't just you know get any amount of Bitcoin that they can and then just turn around and get dollars or yuan or gold uh for it uh easily.

区块链追踪与"污染"比特币

H, but what I meant — maybe I'm not sure I was if I was clear enough by sanction — is blacklisting certain addresses, doing blockchain data analysis. I mean blockchain analysis: look at the blocks and trace it back to certain addresses and then block certain bridges, just like you mentioned, exchanges or mixers or all kinds of other avenues where those Bitcoins can be then processed and then also punishing let's say the actual actors — the companies that then dealt with the Iranian regime and so on so forth, having then tinted Bitcoin and whatnot to be able to actually then close off the scope of possibilities to even transact in Bitcoin and as you also mentioned in stable coins.

And um I think also recently Scott Bessent mentioned that they were all freezing or even quote-unquote grabbing wallets from the Iranian regime that were dealing with stable coins.

九、稳定币：特洛伊木马还是真实需求？

And uh this is kind of a good bridge for my next question is how far do you think that the strategy that the US government is pursuing since a few years now of saying no to CBDC but yes to stable coin and kind of using that as — well I call this a Trojan horse — like to CBDC in some form um to export the dollar on a global basis and to create infrastructure around this uh in order to also um on the other side still create more demand for US treasuries um through like the creation of the synthetic dollar — um did you like have some thoughts about this or how do you see this play out in general?

Lynn：稳定币的需求是有机的

Yeah, I'm bullish on stable coins. I, you know, I've been bullish since several years ago the market cap was like 30 billion and now it's, you know, 300 billion round numbers. Uh, and, you know, I think eventually that they'll get into the over a trillion. Uh, maybe maybe eventually into the multiple trillions.

Um, so I think that there's a lot of demand there. The ironic thing is that the US doesn't really have to do much. They just have to not stop it. Uh, so the US could, if they wanted to, kill most dollar stable coins by saying, "Hey, if you issue a stable coin, you're sanctioned. We don't want stable coins." So they could do that but simply by not doing that uh there's a growth of stable coin demand that is pretty organic.

货币摩擦的全球地图

Um uh you know if you kind of look at the world uh I mean Africa has something like 40 currencies. Uh Latin America has something like 30 currencies. There's plenty of currencies in Southeast Asia. All of these are frictions. All of these are border frictions.

Um uh and stable coins are a very efficient way to transmit value and to hold short-term value, assuming you're not a sanctioned entity, assuming you're not in Iran or North Korea where like, you know, the US and the stable coin issuers can shut you off from it.

If you're just someone in Argentina or someone in Nigeria or someone in Malaysia, um, they're a very effective tool to access a less inflationary currency than generally their local currency. Uh, and to pretty seamlessly store and transmit near-term value.

隐藏成本：稳定币持有者被默默稀释

It does debase over time. Uh, you're basically holding dollars without yield. Uh, so you're holding something that the supply of it is growing by 7% a year and you're not getting paid. The stable coin issuer is getting paid.

Um uh and you know I think that again we have to keep numbers in mind. So if we go from let's say 300 billion in market cap to 1.3 trillion. So let's say we add a trillion in market cap that'll take time. Uh and that's 6 months of US deficits. That's 6 months of treasury issuance. So the sheer numbers of how many treasuries are out there, how many treasuries are going to continue coming to market versus stable coin demand — it's a relevant factor uh but it's not as though it's a magic fix for the fiscal dominance that the US finds itself in.

uh and the major cost is that people that choose to hold stable coins for more than just working capital are getting debased. I mean, again, they're holding something that grows in supply by, you know, dollars grow by 7% a year on average, and they're not getting paid a yield in the way that they if they held treasuries directly. Um, uh, and of course, they're not holding something that's kind of dilution resistant like gold or Bitcoin or high quality equities or real estate.

十、黄金超越美债：储备资产的历史性转折

M and do you then still see this as a let's say a form for the dollar then to be able to compete again like against now gold in that sense because gold has overtaken US treasuries as official central bank reserve since more than a year. I've seen it's a bit of a weird thing — I'm not sure what you think about that — like this ECB report now stirs up again this fact that has been like that actually surpassed that level.

Gold has surpassed that level of I think 22% or something and now it's around 30% of share of foreign reserves um more than a year ago, but now it's news again. um and it's kind of like this battle of who is going to either stay the global reserve asset or not or is it just a shift then in the composition of having the global reserve asset just being gold as an anchor and then having the currency still being the dollars just to do transactions and the denomination?

Uh how do you see then this evolve over time now?

Lynn：黄金回归是一个持久趋势

Yes, like you pointed out, I mean that switchover happened last year uh where gold overtook treasuries. Uh the reason it's getting a little bit of renewed headlines is because the ECB says it — so when an official source says it, it gets kind of renewed interest among the public that watches that.

Um and I do think that's a really relevant trend because for I mean for obviously for a long time, gold was the primary reserve asset. Uh, you know, really starting from the treasuries and onward. Uh, the treasury became kind of the major reserve asset. And it really kind of peaked in say the 2000s — like you had kind of the UK infamously dumping some of its gold at at the bottom.

Um and so the combination of poor price performance of gold and just diminishing tonnage held by central banks uh while they were holding more and more treasuries — that was kind of the peak of treasuries versus gold.

Uh and really since then especially since 2009 there's been this gradual shift back to where central banks are gradually increasing their tonnage of gold. Uh and when you have combined that with price increases of gold uh doing way way better than treasuries uh that combination of higher tonnage and price has made it so that gold is now a larger reserve asset than treasuries once again for the first time in quite a while.

And I think that's a durable trend. I think it's going to continue to be the case and I think over time it'll even grind higher um and kind of reassert itself as a dominant reserve asset.

储备货币的四大功能：黄金只占一个

uh as far as kind of reserve currency status. The tricky thing there is although it's labeled "reserve asset" it kind of refers to central bank reserves — it's really kind of four major functions of a reserve currency uh at least as we kind of modernly think of them.

So one is as that reserve asset.

Another one is that it serves as the most liquid trading pair uh for most currencies. So when you have over 100 currencies in the world, the number of combinations between any given currency, there's just countless combinations and most of those are not liquid. So if you want to exchange Egyptian pounds for Korean won, there's not really a liquid market for that. Uh and so what you generally do is you trade one currency for the dollar and you trade the dollar for that other currency. So it kind of serves as the bridge for almost every other trading pair except for a handful of others that might have liquidity. It's basically on one side of the transaction for 90% of exchange transactions.

Another big one is cross-border funding. So if a company in Brazil wants to borrow money uh you know they can borrow their local currency but many emerging markets uh it's you know there's a limit to how much they can borrow in their own currency from say foreign investors. They might want just a sounder ledger. So they'll lend in dollars. Um and that's a kind of cross-border debt uh and that cross-border financing activity is another key function of the reserve currency status and that's still with dollar — that's not with gold.

Uh and then the fourth one would be unit of account. So energy contracts and just in general international contracts are often priced and defined in dollars uh as kind of just the most liquid shared ledger for international relations to happen in.

美元仍在吞噬欧元的市场份额

Uh and so all of that is a powerful intertwined network effect that I think is going to persist for quite a while. Uh even as gold starts kind of reasserting itself as the primary reserve asset again — something that stores its value better uh than the treasury especially as we enter this ongoing fiscal dominance, you get more and more treasury debasement and dollar debasement uh while gold is still pretty supply constrained.

uh of course the other tricky thing is that um the dollar — while it's not really kind of rising in prominence anymore — it is around the margins eating into the euro's market. Uh so it's like the euro is underperforming the dollar in terms of globalizing itself as an asset.

Um and so you have on one hand gold taking market share from the dollar as a reserve holding, but the dollar is still kind of holding up because it's beating up its weaker competition, which in this case is the euro.

Um so I — I mean it depends on which factor you look at — all the different functions of the dollar that I mentioned. I think most of them are going to keep persisting for quite a while even as the store-of-value function is increasingly gold.

十一、Tether 的黄金购买：稳定币巨头的算盘

And now to tie this back, um also Tether has been the biggest purchaser of gold uh in 2025. I mean they had obviously not much or close to zero anyways but they've even bought more than the central bank of Poland which was already very aggressively buying gold. Uh, and this is a crypto company we're talking about that also until then was collateralizing its own um mechanism or just backing off its balance sheet with Bitcoin.

So, why do you — how is your interpretation of this move from Tether?

Lynn：两个独立的资金池

Well, yeah, they've had a couple phases because they've also added Bitcoin to the balance sheet. They've added gold. Now yet there's — yeah you can separate kind of two — there's like they have to back up their products with the associated product. So whether it's dollar stable coins or they do have that gold basically effectively a gold stable coin. So Tether and Paxos for example have gold stable coins.

Um so they have to back up their units with the amount of treasuries or gold uh to have that.

留存收益的投向：稀缺货币

And then there's a question of with their excess — you know with their retained earnings uh you know their surplus — what do they want to store it in? Naturally they want to store it in something scarcer than dollars.

Um, so they benefit tremendously from their stable coin business because all the stable coin holders are getting paid zero. Uh, while Tether's able to hold treasuries and other types of dollar assets and they're getting to benefit from all that interest expense with pretty low operational expenses to maintain their product.

Um, but when it comes to their retained earnings, they say, "Okay, we're making billions of dollars." So the main things they can do with it, they can hold Bitcoin and gold uh scarce monies. Uh you know the people there are Bitcoin bulls and they are gold bulls and then they also go out and make equity investments. Uh because equity is a type of scarce value as well. Uh so it makes perfect sense that they put their retained earnings into something that is scarcer than dollars.

十二、投资组合展望：下一个机会窗口

And as to conclude um now assets uh I would like to know from you uh which are the assets that you see have more upside potential now in the coming quarters — two quarters — by the end of this year. Um you've mentioned in your last newsletter that you flagged silver and Brazilian equities as being very underappreciated plays. Um why those and is there any other as well that you're having a close look?

白银：从极度不对称到对称

Yeah. Well, silver had a big price run. Uh, so I still hold silver, but I'm less bullish on silver at higher prices than I was at lower prices.

拉美金融股：被遗忘的角落

Um I'm still bullish on Latin American financials. Um I do think that as we enter this ongoing US fiscal dominance, the emerging markets that have dollar liabilities uh will generally get an uplift from their weakening dollar liabilities.

Um many of them like for example Brazil — their central bank has been very hawkish uh really really high real rates uh and I think eventually they'll be able to reduce those and kind of benefit Brazil's economy in the process.

um and just in general there's like a lot of the foreign capital is just not there anymore. It's gradually been coming back a little bit because those things are not underperforming the way that they were throughout the 2010s and the early 2020s. Uh but they're at this kind of inflection point.

where you know it is funny — during the 2025 year, the year of kind of trade wars, if you went up to most people and said you know what, one of the best performing assets this year was Colombian and Brazilian banks — like nobody would have guessed that — but it's just basically they were left for dead uh you know global capital had totally come out uh and now just by not going out anymore and just around the margins a little bit coming back in um it's kind of booming.

uh And and just like uh we talked about how bubbles can kind of occasionally form. I mean, sometimes these things will do so well in a given 6 months or a given year that they need a breather from time to time. But I still think the multi-year runway for say Latin America financials and certain emerging market equities in general has a pretty long runway ahead uh for many years. There's obviously geopolitical risk, there's domestic risk. Um but I think that there is a lot of opportunity in those names.

Bitcoin、黄金、能源股的中长期视角

Uh I think you know Bitcoin in the 60-plus thousand range uh is cheap. Um you know I don't necessarily have a bullish 3 or six month view. I'm not bearish on it in that time frame but I can't really tell you what it's going to do in say a 3 to six month period. Uh but looking back 5 years from now I think that the 60s is an attractive buying range.

Um, uh, I, you know, I think that while gold got ahead of itself, uh, I don't think it got overvalued. Um, so gold's in a consolidation, I think eventually it probably consolidates for quite a while, but then breaks up rather than down.

Um, I'm still bullish on energy companies in general. Uh even again, I mean, if you get an announcement tomorrow, "hey, the Strait's open, we have total peace in the Middle East," uh and energy flows again, then sure, energy stocks probably don't have a great year. Uh but I still view them as an important part of a portfolio because you have these fortress balance sheets that make money um uh and they will often move in a different way than many other equities uh which is valuable for a portfolio.

Uh, and so I do expect decent performance out of them, especially when you include dividends that they pay over a multi-year time frame.

投资组合哲学：把风险分散开

Um, uh, and so, yeah, I think there's plenty of opportunities out there. Not everything's a bubble. Some things are cheap. Um, it's generally things that have some risk. Uh, but when you have a handful of pockets of those, I mean, if three out of five or four out of five work out and one or two out of five don't work out, you've at least spread your risk out uh accordingly.

白银和矿业股：不对称性已消失

M and you've mentioned — I mean when I mentioned silver that was from last year obviously not from recently — from last year's conversation. Um do you still see that there's a — I mean you've just mentioned you less bullish on that — you still see silver but maybe also mining stocks as a great upside potential?

Yeah I mean silver did tremendously over the past year. Uh but because silver — because mining stocks and everything did so well — I'm less bullish going forward. Uh it's not that I'm bearish, it's just that I view the asymmetry as gone.

Meaning that, you know, when silver was like $18 an ounce, uh but then even when it was in the high 20s or 30 an ounce, um you know, it's like there's not much downside really. Uh but it's like it could easily triple, right? And that's the kind of numbers that we got.

Um, and you know, when gold was under 2,000 an ounce, it's like, you know, I can't really like — there's not really any strong reason for why it could go lower meaningfully, but there's nothing stopping it from doubling or tripling.

Um, uh, but as you get that doubling or tripling or, you know, depending on which metal we're talking about, um, it's a little bit more of like — it's less asymmetric. Like I wouldn't be shocked by a 30% drawdown in silver, nor would I be surprised by another 50% increase in silver. it just it's just that it's more of that kind of volatile trading range where I feel like I have less of a conviction on what it's going to do in a given say three-year period.

Uh and same thing for mining stocks. Um uh so uh yeah, I generally at the moment I'm leaving those markets for others. Uh I'm still a long-term holder of gold, silver, and platinum. Um but I'm just less kind of viewing them as a trading opportunity uh per se.

结束语

All right, Lynn, thanks a lot for your valuable knowledge as always.

Hey, hey, hey.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个意想不到的观察
### 1. "发达国家正在变成新兴市场（轻量版）"——这是个被忽略的范式倒置
Lynn 提到一个很容易被一带而过的概念："emerging market light"（轻量版新兴市场）。但这其实是整个对话最具颠覆性的洞察：**美国、日本、欧洲部分国家正在显现新兴市场的金融人格特征**——本币计价的股市创新高，硬通货计价的股市却惨淡，消费者信心创历史新低。
这意味着什么？**S&P 500 的新高不再是繁荣的信号，而是货币病变的体外症状**。就像 2018 年阿根廷股市以本币计价暴涨 80%、以美元计价却血崩——投资者所看到的"牛市"，本质上是他们逃离自己货币的脚印。这是一个跨领域的连接：**医学上发烧不是健康，而是免疫系统正在搏斗**；股市的名义新高也是同样的体温读数。
### 2. AI 泡沫与"卖 10 美元的 20 美元钞票"——它和 19 世纪铁路狂潮的同构性
Lynn 用了一个绝妙的比喻：AI 公司在"用 10 美元卖 20 美元钞票"。但他真正在说的是一个更深的模式：**所有改变文明的技术，其首次资本化都伴随着"补贴用户—投资者买单—基

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Is there real evidence of AI job displacement? – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"白领大屠杀"的悖论：劳动力市场正在演出一场"集体共谋的幻觉"

这段对话里最容易被忽略的一句话，其实是 Imas 提到的"协调装置"（coordination device）——企业之所以裁员，并不是因为 AI 真的取代了人，而是因为不裁员就会被市场解读为"没跟上 AI 浪潮"。这是一个极其反直觉的发现：裁员本身不是生产力的结果，而是一种信号成本。企业宁可让自己变得更糟，也要避免被资本市场打上"AI 落后者"的标签。

这其实是一种典型的信息瀑布（information cascade）——和 1990 年代日本企业争相引入 ERP 系统、2010 年代品牌争相开微博、2021 年公司争相宣布"元宇宙战略"的逻辑一模一样。讽刺的地方在于：真正的 AI 红利可能流向那些敢于不裁员、安静使用 AI 的公司，而高调裁员的企业反而在用真实生产力换取叙事估值。

二、跨领域连接：这场"自动化恐慌"和 19 世纪的"自动钢琴危机"惊人地相似

很少有人意识到，1900 年前后美国曾经爆发过一场针对自动钢琴（player piano）的恐慌——音乐家工会预测 90% 的现场演奏者将失业。结果呢？十年后，录音业、广播业反而创造了比所有钢琴酒吧加起来还多十倍的音乐相关工作。这正是 Trammell 引用的 O-Ring 模型 + 需求弹性论证的历史先例：当一项任务被自动化，剩下不可自动化的那一环（在那时是"现场情绪传递"，在今天可能是"判断力、品味、责任承担"）的稀缺性反而暴涨。

更深一层的隐藏真相是：资深软件工程师需求上升、初级岗位增长放缓，并不是 AI 在"吃掉初级"，而是 AI 把"经验"这个原本缓慢积累的资产，重新定价为奢侈品。这意味着我们正在目睹一场"经验通胀"——年轻人被剥夺的不是工作，而是积累经验的阶梯。这是比失业本身严重得多的系统性问题，因为它是一种延迟爆炸的债务：5 年后没有"中级工程师"可补充时，企业才会发现真正的危机。

三、令人意外的元层观察：这场对话本身就是 AI 影响劳动市场的"测不准原理"现场

注意 Imas 反复强调的一点——"你得眯着眼才能看见信号"。这句话的真正含义远超表面。它揭示了一个深刻的认识论困境：

当一个现象同时是经济变量、媒体叙事和自我实现的预言时，"测量"本身就在改变被测对象。

这与量子物理的观察者效应、社会学的托马斯定理（"如果人们把情境定义为真实的，那么它们在结果上就是真实的"）形成了奇妙的同构。Yale 预算实验室越是"看不到 AI 失业"，那些坚持 AI 失业论的人就越倾向于寻找叙事而非数据；而企业 CEO 在财报电话会上越是宣称"我们用 AI 替代了 N 个岗位"，就越会反向制造出统计假象。

由此引出一个令人不安的洞见：我们可能永远无法"实时"知道 AI 是否在大规模摧毁就业——因为在它真正发生前，叙事会先一步抵达；在它真正发生时，叙事又会掩盖统计信号。我们将只能在事后五到十年通过历史学家的回顾才能确认。这是一种新型的"经济学黑箱"，与气候变化的滞后性、复利的不可视性属于同一类系统性盲点。

📝 转录文本

引子：白领大灭绝真的发生了吗？

主持人：People talked about whether there's a white collar apocalypse already. Is there any evidence that suggests that there is mass automation or unemployment as a result of AI already?

一、数据里的"几乎没有信号"

Imas：Um, I think there's a lot of people are looking at it. So this is an area where there's like a lot of eyes and a lot of data being produced. The Budget Lab over Yale is doing really good analysis on this. They just recently released a report, and I think you really have to squint to see anything happening.

Like, basically, if you want to take kind of an approach across the entire economy — and looking at even looking at like software engineering, the most exposed sectors — there's just not really anything going on. There might be a little bit of a signal about like junior developers getting jobs less than before. But that's like a "less than before" rather than a level shift. As in, there's actually an increased demand for senior software engineers if anything.

Um, and so if you look at the trend, it's kind of like — for junior managers, it's a bit below trend.

主持人：So, as in, you're saying the growth is slower than before.

Imas：Yes.

主持人：But there is still growth even on entry-level software engineers.

Imas：Exactly.

二、那些"找不到工作"的毕业生：是 AI，还是叙事？

主持人：And what do you think is going on with the anecdotal evidence of graduating college students saying that they're finding it harder to find CS jobs or something?

Imas：I think that's anecdotal evidence.

主持人：You think it's always been hard to get jobs for some people, and now it's getting turned into an AI narrative? Same with the layoffs — where it's probably just normal layoffs, and they're turned into AI layoffs?

三、协调装置：被叙事裹挟的裁员潮

Imas：Yeah, I mean, you have to be careful with all of this. I think there are these like — you know, there are these public coordination devices.

Let's say we get into a narrative where, if you're a firm and you're not laying people off, then you're seen as like not adopting AI enough. So then you're going to just get a cascade effect of firms like just needing to keep up with the Joneses in terms of starting to lay people off.

And that's kind of super worrying, where actually the firm might be actually worse off after the layoffs than before the layoffs — but it's just doing the layoffs to have the perception that, "look, look, we're not behind the times, we're using AI." Like, you probably heard these anecdotal stories of like these token counters, that you have to maximize tokens and things like that.

So again, right now we don't really have any evidence of a white collar bloodbath.

四、O-Ring 模型与需求弹性：为什么自动化反而可能创造工作

主持人：And is that surprising at all? I feel given the fact all these things AI can do — this is the story as old as time. If you automate some complementary task, the overall bucket of things that the human labor which complements the automation will increase in value.

Trammell：So this is one of the statistics that's really important for that argument: elasticity of demand.

You take the old O-Ring model of jobs. So again, jobs is a series of tasks. Let's say the AI automates like 9 out of 10 tasks. One task is not automated. If that person can now focus in on that task, the job will become more productive.

If that translates into a price effect — where the product is actually cheaper — and if that demand responds enough, where now it's being bought more, it's being used more, the service is being used more, that could actually lead to higher hiring.

主持人：Right.

Trammell：And a lot of people on the internet have been kind of making that argument very generally, saying: look, if anything in the data, we're seeing an uptick in software engineering.

主持人：Right. Yeah, yeah.

Trammell：Which suggests that, at least for now, given the way the jobs work, it might be a lot.

结语

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
