But I think the bigger story is outside of what might be called the top 10 clubs in Europe — which we've mentioned before in terms of the money list, the power list. That top 10 is spread quite equally between English clubs and non-English clubs. The issue, and I remember us covering the topic at the time, is when you then go from 10th to 40th, those 30 clubs include the entire of the English Premier League outside of the big ones who are in the top 10.

So it really is very, very dramatic when you see the spending capacity of a club like Villa, and compare it against what might be thought to be peer-group clubs in other major European leagues — Villa are able to spend two to three times as much. It's really, really dramatic.

If Villa hadn't won the Europa League, Forest would have done, and that's true. And frankly, if you look at the Conference League — the Conference League could be won probably by any other club in the Premier League, going right down to the clubs that are relegated. Chuck Wolves in the Conference League, arguably, they'd have a decent chance. They'd have a good shot.

To answer your original question — yes, I'm sure that UEFA were relieved that the English Premier League teams didn't win all the…

1989 年的意甲幽灵

Do you know the last time that a country supplied all three of the winning teams in European competition? Would it be Italy? Yes. Take a guess. Which year? Think about their era of dominance was pre-that. 2003? That was way before that.

Yeah, it's 1989, which is right in the middle of Serie A's real era of dominance, which by the turn of the century was already dissipating. I think AC Milan won the European Cup that year. I couldn't tell you who the other two were. Answered on a postcard.

But what's interesting about that is that at that moment of absolute dominance, Italian football was still only a decade away from losing it all.

So I think — and I hope — that English football is better prepared for really building off the back of it, rather than seeing itself diminish. And as long as it doesn't dent the quality of our Premier League… which is a bit of a challenge when you've got half the division — I think nine out of the 20 teams in the Premier League are playing European football next year. Will that lead to more Premier League matches where there is a bit of a drop-off in quality, certainly in entertainment terms, because some of the players are a bit more tired? Based on what we've been saying for the last few weeks, you'd imagine so, wouldn't you? Possibly, probably, yes. But anyway, a story for another day.

四、南安普顿、Dragan Solak 与"我很抱歉你不开心"式声明

Quick look at the Southampton situation. As of this morning, Tuesday morning, Dragan Solak, the owner, released a statement yesterday on the state of the last few weeks and how the club's looking to move forward. He said some interesting words, so I'll read them out:

"I'd like to address the EFL decision one final time before we close this chapter. We accept that mistakes have been made at the club level, rules have been broken, there must be consequences, and I'll take responsibility for that. But I also believe the sanction imposed is grossly disproportionate. The level of breach combined with the outcome of previous cases makes this feel incredibly harsh. It's the fans as well as the players who suffer the most, many of whom have already made arrangements for Wembley once the semi-finals went ahead. I also believe that the competition itself loses credibility. Suggesting that decisions made off the pitch can see results on the field simply wiped out cannot be good for the game."

Do you know there's a situation where people say, "I'm sorry you're upset"? Yeah, that's exactly it, isn't it? Right? "I'm sorry you're upset." And you sort of actually — I'd rather you hadn't said anything at all. I'd rather you just kept your mouth shut, because that makes me feel both simultaneously unheard but also patronized. So please shut up.

In this situation, I think honestly, he is speaking there — and I'm not saying this is wrong, by the way; as a former communications advisor I might very well say this to him — that is addressed to his fan base. That's what it's addressed to. It's basically saying, "Right, come on guys, we're all one, we're all here, we've been harshly treated, let's get a siege mentality going."

He subsequently goes on to brief that he's not going to be firing or dismissing Tonda Ekert, which is massively surprising, because clearly what happened was gross misconduct. No one can debate that.

Now, Tonda Rekord might be dealt with separately by the FA, and Southampton as a football club may be put in a situation where Tonda Rekord effectively is unable to exercise his duties. That might be preferable financially for Southampton than actually firing him and going through that process.

But putting all that to one side, what that message is, is basically saying: "Right, we've taken the punishment, and there's nothing we're going to do to change that. But I need to use this moment to reunify the club behind its leadership."

Events of the next weeks and months — I wouldn't want to be an executive in Southampton right now, because when these things hang over clubs, they just occupy vast amounts of mind space, negative energy, meetings, lawyers, etc. It's just a horrible, horrible time.

Whatever is said externally, internally those finger-pointing — obviously from people who've suffered, particularly if you're a player who's seen what could have been an enormous increase in pay then not happen — "I've suffered because of that guy, that guy, and that guy, and they're still here." So there's a big internal job that they've got to do to try to get everybody back on the same page.

He's basically highlighted all the reasons why the fans would have a right to be upset with the club and tried to put it on the EFL, saying that it's the fans that suffer. Well, yeah — they suffer because of your actions, not because of the EFL punishment. "I also believe the competition itself loses credibility." Yes — it loses credibility because of your actions, not because of how you were punished.

All for him going "we want to draw a line in the sand and move on from this" and then say "we're going to keep our manager" — basically, we know what football is like. If he isn't banned, let's say by the FA, and he is in the dugout next season, every single game, away fan bases will be doing something that will not allow this to be parked. So it seems like it's at odds with each other.

A quite extraordinary decision if they do stick by. And I suspect, as you've said, that there's an ulterior motive to doing that structurally from a business perspective.

There might be. I don't know the terms of the contract. I don't know what legal advice they've had, but it may well be that he's hoping that the FA does his dirty work for him.

But don't forget, the slight challenge here, if you are the Southampton owner, is that effectively this is a head coach who, after a significant period of underachievement at Southampton, had over a period of six months brought a high level of performance, very high level of performance — because they knew what their opponents were doing.

Very good, Charles. Over that period, they were probably the best side in the division. And you can feel him not wanting to let go of that bit of alchemy, right? Because however much we say that managers only make so much difference, however much we say that everything behind the scenes matters more — I've been there before as an owner and chief executive when you think: "We've got everything right here. The players are fit. We've got things sorted out behind the scenes. We think we've got a squad that should be doing better than this." And you're waiting for that moment of alchemy to happen when it all comes together and you then fly off like this. And that is exactly how promotions happen.

He's reached that point where he's gone, "Ooh, it's happening. It's happening. Every week, you're winning 2-0, 3-0. It's happening. We're on the magic carpet right now." And suddenly, this happens. It's like, well, you are going to get rid of your manager, aren't you? And there'll be a big bit of him thinking, "God, do I have to? I finally got this right. This finally feels right."

So we shall see what the FA does. We shall see what Southampton do in response. As you say, if he's still there next season in the Championship, then there'll be no end of jollity. But there we are. Maybe Southampton take the view that siege mentality, "let's fight together" — and that, as we said earlier on about Liverpool, that can be a very powerful thing. Completely.

五、Kirsty Coventry 与"我不相信付钱给运动员"

Charlie, my phone blew up last week. Not again. Yeah, it blew up because one of our favourite breakdown contributors, IOC president, Kirsty Coventry, has been back at it.

Oh, no. Yes. The gift that keeps on giving.

Kirsty Coventry, the head of the IOC, the president of the IOC, the lady who was so brilliant and competent that she actually beat Sebastian Coe to that job, having never had a senior executive job in her life before.

If viewers and listeners remember, early on in our tenure as breakdown co-hosts, in a press conference, when she was asked a question that she didn't know the answer to, she said that she was going to fire her press officer because they hadn't predicted the question. So since then, we've coined this phrase, "pleading a Kirsty" — which is when you haven't got a clue what you're talking about, just fire a junior member of staff. And I can tell viewers and listeners, as the ever-diminishing group of Charlie's staff demonstrates, that we've got a few things wrong and been a bit ignorant at times recently.

But yes, Kirsty has continued on her merry way. And her most recent contribution to the sports industry discourse has been to put her size… I don't know what, she's a swimmer, probably quite large feet. Size eight or nine feet. Right in another mess.

那句话：I don't believe in paying athletes

It's a very simple quote, actually. Unlike the bible I just read out, it very simply says:

"I don't believe in paying athletes. I come from a small country. I came from a sport that doesn't necessarily pay athletes very well, and I still don't think we should be paying athletes at the Olympic Games."

This was said in an interview at Eden Park in New Zealand. It was captured on a video. It went all over Twitter. It was a short conversation. And it needed more context, which was later provided — she gave clarity. She said she opposes prize money specifically for just the medals rather than broader athlete support. She's basically saying she doesn't want it to only benefit a small number of athletes.

A slightly more defensible position. That was her clarifying quote. So basically, having put a foot in it by saying that she doesn't want to pay athletes — we'll come on to the whys and wherefores of that in a moment — a big storm blew up with lots of athletes going, "Sorry, what? You really, really don't want to pay us. Okay, we thought before there was a conversation going on, but you've just literally said there's no point in the conversation. I don't want to pay athletes."

She then came up with a clarifying quote where she attempted, under heavy guidance from those communications officials that she hadn't yet fired, to put a little bit more context around it.

But what it does is it brings a spotlight back onto — Kirsty makes you laugh so much, doesn't she? It's the blithering incompetence. I just can't believe it. And also, you're looking at a comms strategy. We talked about comms strategies. When Keely Hodgkinson comes out, she goes "Eesh, this is going to age well." You've got your athletes who are massively impacted by this.

为什么这是个"活的"议题

Let's just revisit why it's a live topic. It's a live topic because the IOC, over a four-year cycle — that's the gap between the two main summer games, which also includes a winter games halfway through that cycle — over that cycle, the IOC makes around about $8 billion.

Obviously, they have some head office costs and so on, and they have a commercial department who helps make that money. The whole thing is totally legitimate. But nonetheless, $8 billion, which if you then divide it by the number of years in which it's earned, is $2 billion a year.

I think I said on a recent breakdown — put a finger in the air and say: of that money, how much of it is attributable to basically the main Summer Olympic sports, mostly being athletics, but also being Kirsty's own home sports — swimming, and one or two other of the big sports — which really command the vast majority of the attention at the Summer Olympic Games, the Summer Olympic Games in turn being miles bigger than the Winter Olympics.

Just put a finger in the air and say: let's just call it half. We're probably being a bit conservative there. It's probably more than that, but let's just call it half. So let's just say that $1 billion a year is being generated by the athletics and the swimming, the main Summer Olympic sports.

And currently, they are giving no prize money to those athletes who then create those special moments at the Olympics.

与网球大满贯对比

Some weeks ago, we were discussing the putative strike by the tennis players at the French Open. What happened to that, by the way? Anyway, that's a story for another day. Funny, I seem to watch Aryna Sabalenka an awful lot on TV for somebody who's meant to be on strike.

But we were discussing it in that context because the tennis players were saying, "We are currently given about 17.5% of Grand Slam tournament revenues, and we want to see that being more like 25%." And I discussed why I felt the Grand Slam organisers should hold firm on that and say, "You're already unbelievably well paid, and these days even down to a hundred in the world earning $600,000–$700,000 a year — how much further down that do we owe people a living? It's arguably not a lot further than that."

I'm not somebody who is one of these people that, "OK, the athletes basically deserve everything and everyone else can go hang." Because it's absolutely true that the IOC does have a duty to the broader Olympic movement. It does have all sorts of federations in parts of the world which don't necessarily produce Olympic champions, so where they put money to try and help foster athletic development. So that's all absolutely fair. And Kirsty could have made those points, I think, with a bit of clarity.

But really, instead of asking to go from 17.5% to 25%, the athletes — the runners and the swimmers and co. — are basically currently in the position of saying, "Can we just have something? Can we have something which helps us through the years after a Summer Olympics? Because we don't earn much in our year-to-year sport. This is our one moment in the sun. This is what we're all aiming for. And we're very happy to come to the Olympics. We want to be at the Olympics. But this is the moment when the event that we're taking part in collars in vast amounts of revenue, and currently we get zero."

But Kirsty tried to weaponize that in part of that conversation as well, by saying "if they want to play in nice stadiums or feature in nice stadiums and be part of these amazing events, that's what a lot of the money goes towards." But don't forget — those stadiums aren't paid for by the IOC. They're paid for by the host city. Sorry, Kirsty, I hate to keep on picking you up on your own blithering incompetence, but that is just a total red herring.

90% 分配的迷雾

Their comeback will be that they distribute 90% of that revenue to international federations and governing bodies. Now, while that may be well and good, there is very little system of accountability within those systems as to how the athletes then get paid from that. To put it mildly.

So you don't have a situation where your athletes are then really benefiting from that. That will go into so many different areas. So you're still getting a group of people expected to dedicate an entire — well, a young — life to featuring in a games that will leverage them as brands. Their name, image, likeness will be completely exposed by the organization, and they will receive no money for competing in it.

The money that they may receive — World Athletics led the charge on this in Paris in 2024. They said that they will pay, and they did pay, $50,000 to a gold medal in athletics. That's good. It's a good start. But the reality, again, of what $50,000 does for you in the context of how much you've had to put into your training and career to that date is very, very small.

Matt Richards 的 15,000 英镑

Matt Richards — British swimmer, gold medalist, brilliant guy. Had him on the show. I picked out a comment that he said when I spoke with him a few months ago. He said:

"I was on £15,000 when I won gold. And you have to be —"

And when he says "I was on £15,000," that is the allowance that was being given to him by the British Olympic Association.

"I was on £15,000 when I won gold. And you have to be a multi-gold medal winner in multiple years to get to the top band of funding, which is £28,000."

That's what Adam Peaty level. This is 23 years old, double Olympic gold medalist. He'll be going again in LA. He's been very vocal actually since. He's actually been very prominent this week. Super smart, really invested in all of this. And there's no inflammatory comments coming out around it. It's very much baked in the theory.

历史背景：从 1000 万到 80 亿

You can't live off the legacy of the values the Olympics was built on in the early 1900s. I actually had a look and saw how the revenue-generating commercial entity of the Olympics has changed throughout that time.

In 1950, the budget for the Olympic Games was $10 million, which is the equivalent of $140 million today. From the 70s, 80s onwards, you started to build a commercial powerhouse. The level of production that goes into it, the stadia that goes into it, the sponsorship, all of this — the minute you create that, you have to start paying the key contributors to that, which is still the athletes. Whatever that percentage is to make that make sense, fine, you guys go and have that discussion. But with that being zero, in a sport where this is the pinnacle of your athletic prowess…

People have thrown this back recently. They said, "Yeah, but the Ryder Cup — we have this issue every time." It's like — ah, but it's different, because do you know what McIlroy and DeChambeau and all of that lot are getting alongside it? $5 million paychecks pretty much every time they turn up on a golf course or at least win a golf tournament. So this, for the athletes, is their opportunity to make money. That's their cycle. And outside of that cycle, it's few and far between.

Enhanced Games 的阴影

What happens — and the reason, Charlie, finally why I find this even more extraordinary — is a timing perspective. Because what did we talk about last week in trying to defend the purity of athletics? We talked about the Enhanced Games. The Enhanced Games happened. Kirsty makes these comments.

If I'm an athlete and I'm looking at this — she's sitting here at the pinnacle of my elite performance, and these guys are sitting here going, "I'll pay you a million dollars to break a world record, 250 grand to win an event, and some, and some." I'm starting to see this come a little bit closer together. If I'm an athlete who is sitting here going, "I cannot make this work under current circumstances" — and that's the threat.

"如果我是 Kirsty 的公关顾问"

Let me try and imagine myself to be in the unfortunate position of being one of Kirsty's communications people. I'm just asking you to imagine that for a moment. So you're in some office at IOC Central, knowing that if any point in time your boss makes a mistake, you're probably going to get fired. And in any case, even if it isn't your mistake, she's going to go and say something stupid anyway.

But nonetheless, you are basically having to give advice. So I think this is what I would say:

"I don't think it's appropriate for the Olympics to itself pay prize money, because this is the pinnacle of the sport, and we want people to come here for the greater glory. And furthermore, if we put all the prize money into that one event, those athletes who happen to be injured at that particular time are basically losing out on that money for the entire cycle. And that just makes it massively disproportionate. Just one mistake in one event and suddenly they don't earn anything. So instead, what we're going to do is distribute portions of the IOC's earnings to the governing bodies of those sports, and make sure that they spread that as prize money in the intervening years in between our Summer Olympics, to make sure that our athletes are able to put bread on the table during that period, to make sure they're able to come back as good as they possibly can be."

I think that would be a totally logical thing. If they said, "Right, OK, who have we got here? We've got the IAAF — sorry, World Athletics. Is it called World Athletics? World Athletics. So we are going to work with them on how that is apportioned throughout the cycle. We're going to work with these various different bodies on how they use IOC money to make sure the athletes are earning okay in between the Olympic Games."

I think that would be a perfectly logical and reasonable thing to do. And they could then still keep the Olympics itself as something which is unpaid.

What is not tenable is for them to say — remember that the Olympic Games does not exist in a commercial vacuum. When they go and attract a commercial sponsor to get involved with them, that is a commercial partner who's apportioning marketing budgets to the Olympics that they then will not have to apportion to World Athletics or the various different other bodies. So they're sucking in all of the money which might otherwise be spread more evenly throughout the four-year cycle on different events and different circuits, and then basically saying, having sucked it all in: "No, no, we're going to keep that all for ourselves."

投票机制的政治

Now, there is one last thing I want to say on this, Charlie, which is that part of what drives behind this is the voting mechanism for the IOC. So rather like with FIFA — and the reason, frankly, why Kirsty is in that position rather than Sebastian Coe — is that each individual Olympic federation, each individual country gets a vote.

And the way in which FIFA presidents and IOC presidents keep their presidency going is basically by doling out goodies, financial goodies, to these individual federations. And dare I say it, sometimes those things might end up not always being put in the places where they should be. Putting it very politely. Keeping it political, yeah. Thank you very much.

So effectively, that funding model is what works for the president, the current president of the IOC, whoever that president might be.

It's rather like in UK politics, where we have a first-past-the-post voting system. When a political party is out of power, it always says, "Well, we should have proportional representation. We should have proportional voting." And then the moment they get into power, they say, "Well, I tell you what, this first-past-the-post system ain't half great, isn't it?"

It's a bit like that here, where if you were to say to Kirsty, "I tell you what, of your $2 billion a year, we suggest that you take a billion of that and hand it out to the governing bodies of these sports to make sure the athletes are okay" — there'll be a bit of her thinking, "Well, how am I going to get the votes of all those small countries who are relying on me to push a few million quid in their direction to, I'm sure, do some very good things? Because that's how I get re-elected. If I go to them and say I'm pulling that money out to give it to the athletes, that's going to cause a big problem for me when somebody comes along and challenges me for the presidency of the IOC."

So once again, the power mechanism is completely out of touch with the outcomes.

风险："What are they going to do?"

So the downside, which you've just alighted on — just be clear on the jeopardy. There's a bit of these things where you can say, and I say this with Grand Slam tournaments: "Look, what are they going to do, right?"

The thing here is, as you've outlined, in the case of tennis players who are earning $20–25 million a year, a big chunk of which is coming from the Grand Slam tennis tournaments, the "what are they going to do" question is very opposite. Is Aryna Sabalenka really going to give up three or four million dollars because she feels so strongly about prize money? As it turns out, no, she won't.

Now, when it comes to the Olympics and athletes — particularly those who are already big stars, who may well already have won a gold medal, so they've already scratched that itch — and then the Enhanced Games comes along and says, "Tell you what, since Kirsty says she doesn't want to give you any money, you kind of haven't got a lot to lose here. You'll already always be an Olympic gold medalist. How about if you come and earn some money?" — I'd be very tempted. Which is just what you're saying.

Exactly. And whether it's the Enhanced Games or anything else, it leaves them wide open. And it comes back to a point we made the other day: they've never really had genuine competition at this level.

I saw they sit on $5 billion in cash reserves. So it's not like the cash isn't there.

六、女子职业足球：增长神话的拆解

We talk a lot about this, don't we, Charlie? Pay in general sport, pay in sport costs. Because it's a critical element of running a sports business. In any sports business that you're running, a large chunk of your cost base and therefore your business model is basically: how much do we pay the athletes?

We're about to come on to that in a moment. That's why I said — because whenever we've talked about football, it's generally swung the other way. It's been out-of-control wages and cost, far too much going to players based on the proportional revenue that is generated by clubs and federations.

We've been wanting to touch on women's football for a while, and we've been looking for the right time to do it. I've been a bit wary, Charles. I'd be a bit wary too. I'm a bit wary, and I'll tell you why. Because I've spoken about it and written about it in the past, and you find yourself in a bit of what I would call almost a — not a no-win situation, but you are basically a sitting duck for some fairly cheap shots.

So therefore, I think the only time we've mentioned it on the show so far in the last five months that we've been doing the show was really in relation to women's rugby. And I mentioned in passing that I felt that the big success that women's rugby was having provided lessons that could be learned by professional women's football, but which would not be learned. And then we sort of parked it.

But eventually you come round — when a topic is a significant topic, at some point we wouldn't be being ourselves if we didn't address it. And last weekend was, apart from the UEFA Champions League final, the following day after that was the Women's FA Cup final here in England.

Women's FA Cup Final 的数据真相

In the final, Man City beat Brighton 4-0 in front of a crowd of 45,000 people. Now, 45,000 people is a lot of people. It's equivalent to having a full Villa Park. It's not nothing. But for the showpiece final of the whole year in a sport which we are told by numerous of the cognoscenti of the sports world — but actually often the investment world — is the big coming sport, the booming sport, the sport which is growing faster than anything else, it is, I think, a disappointing number.

We have to go back to the 2023 Women's FA Cup and find that 78,000 people attended that match. Now, obviously the Women's FA Cup final should not be expected to have the same level of prestige as the men's FA Cup final, and that's reflected in the ticket prices. The ticket prices are, I think, the phrase is "cheap as chips" — so £15 for an adult, £10 for a concession, £5 for a junior. You're talking about the equivalent pricing for a non-league men's game. They're basically saying, "We don't want to price anybody out." That really doesn't price anybody out. Even somebody on a very low income or on welfare can afford 10 quid, 15 quid. That is absolutely achievable. So we want everybody who wants to come should be able to come.

Weather was great. No men's matches on. So no clashes. And 45,000 people, down from 78,000 people.

"增长"叙事的数据陷阱

This is part of a broader pattern, which is that for all the noise that there is out there that women's professional football is growing, the hard facts that people in the sports industry like me look at — is it's shrinking.

But Charlie, I saw a report that said the 2025–26 attendances were 2.7% higher than they had been before. When you say "they had been before," you mean last season. And that season in turn was 10% lower than the previous season. So in other words, attendances this season are 7% lower than they were two seasons ago. You didn't buy it on that.

Do you know the reason for that, which is ridiculous in this reporting? If you take Arsenal out of that, Arsenal account for 41% of WSL attendances.

So the headline number that I saw reported was 2.7% increase in attendance on the previous year. But Arsenal had contributed to that. If you take Arsenal out of it, it was a 17% decline over the last two seasons.

So just please, everyone clock that: if you take Arsenal Women out of these numbers, you're looking at double-digit declines in attendance at a time where we are being told that this is a booming growth story.

与女子橄榄球的对比

Now, just go back a few weeks — women's professional rugby. One of the biggest shows we've ever done. Yeah, the biggest audio show we've ever done. And there were an awful lot of people communicating around — "I had no idea." And I'm so glad that you chose that topic, because I simply had no idea that women's professional rugby is growing attendance-wise at 92% a year. And not over one year — over multiple years.

When you look at their showpiece occasions, the attendances are increasing by 15, 20, 25, 30% on their showpiece, from what were already quite good numbers. Their international matches are packing out Twickenham at 80,000. And this is a semi-pro and amateur sport. This is a sport where there is a member of the England national team who's a dentist.

So I just want everyone to bear that in mind in the debate that we're about to have, which is that this is not about being pro or anti women's sport. This is about how to create a growth sport, how to create a successful sport, how to create a sport which actually has a meaningful commercial future. And what we're going to look at now is what women's professional football has done and ultimately why it is failing. Is that fair enough?

It is fair enough. And just to add to it — and whether it's men's sport or women's sport, we have to treat everything fairly. Absolutely. And with the same review. We say now almost weekly: no one has a right to exist. You have to earn your fandom. You have to earn the money that you generate. You have to earn your right to be a competitive sport. That's what this is really a look at.

简史：1991 至今

So women's elite league football has existed in this country since 1991 with the FA Women's Premier League National. And effectively, elite women's football — which at the time was as popular as women's rugby is now, a mixture of sort of semi-pro/amateur — was put into a league system. There was, in the first instance, no relegation. So it was a closed league. And then they opened it up.

It's quite interesting that when it started in 1991, it was actually a closed system, a franchise system, as women's professional rugby is. We'll come back onto that as a theme about where things might have gone wrong.

It then changed, and they did bring in promotion and relegation to the division below that, which was an amateur league effectively. Then they innovated further: they decided to go into playing in the summer rather than in the winter. Why? Because they didn't want to clash with men's professional football. They wanted to give their sport every opportunity to attract broader football fans at a time of year when both their wallet and their mind space wasn't being totally taken up by men's professional football. That was 2011 until 2016–17, I think.

Then in 2017–2018, they went back into winter football, so matching up with the men's professional season, and the women's championship — that's the second division — started to go on a more serious basis. So you started to move towards a system much more similar in every way to the men's system.

And it was around this time that some of the independent teams that had grown up started to struggle a little bit, and some of the teams which were attached to even small men's professional clubs — the Doncaster Belles and the Millwall Lionesses, these types of clubs which were either attached or not quite attached or semi-attached to smaller men's professional clubs, where effectively they almost had the air to become bigger entities themselves — those clubs started to drop away, and you started to see them being replaced by the women's arms of the bigger clubs.

In 2018, the top division turned full pro. So this is still under the leadership and the governance of the Football Association. It was a department of the Football Association. The women's football department oversaw the elite football leagues until just before COVID, really.

But there was always a sort of feeling within the FA, "Look, at some point we would rather not oversee this." And I think that's legitimate. I think that's absolutely fair. I haven't got any sort of problem with that.

Carney 报告：错位的起点

But what they did is they commissioned something called the Carney Report. That is a former elite women's player called Karen Carney, who had recently retired from the professional full-time game. And she was commissioned to write a report on what the future should look like for elite women's football.

What you said there to me sounds like they got it right originally and they've now got it wrong again. They've fallen into the traps. The biggest challenge that I see seems like this determination to replicate the men's game, which by all accounts, if it was created today, if you had a fresh slate, would be very, very different to how it was created back in the day.

God, yes. Why is that happening is a broader question. But I think the key is there are so many routes. There are societal routes. There's the types of fandom that both of these sports individually create that make it completely impossible to replicate in the same way. Because, let's be very clear, this is a sport created from resistance, really. Women were not allowed to play football from 1921 to 1971 in this country. I wrote it down. "Totally unsuitable for females" was the comment.

So even just looking at the feeling that must have existed behind the scenes, you can already see where this desire for parity, this desire to be locked into something that the men have because we've been marginalized from this for so long, comes from. I completely get that, and I don't think you're about to pull that down.

But what we really want to see is — this isn't about being the same as something else. It's about creating the best product for the people that most want to engage with it.

Yes, exactly right.

"生产者利益"还是"消费者利益"？

So Carney herself said that the aim of this was to effectively create two fully professional divisions, Women's Super League and WSL2, or the Women's Championship. And the aim of that was to create world-leading standards for players, staff, and fans.

Right — I just want to unpick that, because it relates to what Charlie has just been saying. Always listen to what people say. What people say is, generally speaking, what they mean.

The aim of this whole exercise was to create world-leading standards for players. Oh, and staff and fans. Players first. This whole thing has been created by what we call producer interest.

Producer interest, for those who don't know the term, is when an entity or a business or an industry is set up fundamentally for the benefit of the people who work in it. The whole thing is set up to make life as easy as possible and as well paid as possible for the people who produce it, rather than being run to the benefit of those who consume it.

But professional sport is the most brutal Darwinian industry you can possibly be in, because it's all about the scores on the doors. It's fundamentally about what the punters want in every sport. If there are enough punters who pay enough money, either by going live or by watching on TV, creating the eyeballs which then drive higher TV rights, then — and only then — is there the money to push towards the producers, the players and the coaches and so on.

But effectively, women's professional football has started from the other end, which is saying: the primary consideration here, the primary target, is to pay the players really well.

The justification, if I was to explain it the other side — because we'll definitely get this — is "but the only way in which we're going to make this sport attractive to the general public is if we have the very best players playing here. The only way in which we will get the very best players in the world playing here is if we pay the very, very highest salaries." So we're back on to pay.

But we're looking at it through the other end of the prism. So you're starting off the Olympics where vast amounts of money is generated, and then the question is, well, surely some of that should be given to the athletes. Here we're saying there's no money being generated, but there is an underlying presumption that "world-leading standards for players" is the main point of this.

Karen Carney 的原文与 Lucy Fraser 的"理性之声"

Just a quote from Karen Carney here:

"Professionalization across the top two tiers, the aim of which is to attract the best players in the world, and for a lack of diversity to be addressed, more investment in facilities and access for females."

So there's a whole bunch of societal stuff packed in there — diversity, access for females. There's a bunch of stuff about attracting the best players in the world and paying world-leading amounts. But nowhere in there is there any sentiment around "we need to try and work out what the paying spectator might want," because ultimately, in the long run, that's the only way in which we'll have a worthwhile industry.

Now, there's another quote from this time. This is now three years ago from the person who was then the Secretary of State for the Department of Culture, Media and Sport, a lady called Lucy Fraser, who was in the then Conservative administration. This was around the time that Rishi Sunak took over from Liz Truss. So 2022, 2023.

Lucy Fraser said:

"What we need to do is to make sure that the women's game is more commercial and that it attracts more broadcast income. Because if we do that, then the women will be enabled to be paid more."

That was the voice of good sense.

I was on a couple of conference calls with Lucy Fraser at the time. I was a director of a football league club. And she was astonishingly ignorant about football. I mean, eye-wateringly ignorant about football, but not a stupid person. She can understand that in professional sport, for the performers to be paid well, stuff has to be done to warrant the spectators becoming interested and paying money, which can then be transferred to achieving this entire thing.

But that piece of work that Lucy Fraser was recommending was never done. It was never actually genuinely considered what is it that we need to do to actually generate income. It was simply assumed that there would be big broadcast deals. It was assumed that attendances would go up and up and up. It was assumed that massive commercial deals would land.

None of that has happened. And that in turn has led to a rather sort of resentful tone where there's almost like societal pressure being placed on the broadcasters and on big commercial: "You should be spending more money on women's football. You should be sponsoring women's football, because if you don't, it shows that you don't really care about women." There's that read-across being made and that pressure being put on.

Why does that need to happen? The reason it needs to happen is because there aren't the punters turning up and there aren't the eyeballs on the TV to make that an automatic commercial transaction. And that is where the fundamental problems come from.

转播权与收视数据

Just leave you here for a moment with what the TV rights deal is. There are 140-odd games being shown on TV in this country, shared between Sky and the BBC, mostly on Sky. And it's a deal which is £65 million over five years. So you're talking about around £12–13 million a year, shared between all of the teams in the Women's Super League. That's about kind of the same level we're talking about with the National League — the fifth division of men's football.

And then some of the audience numbers that are going with that — there was the marquee match Arsenal Women against Chelsea Women on Sky this year, got a peak audience of 70,000 with an average audience of 59,000. You're going a long, long way down the pecking order. There are sports which you haven't even heard of that get those types of numbers.

So whether it be live in-stadium attendance, whether it be eyeballs on TV, whether it be commercial rights deals, whether it be genuine commercial deals as opposed to ones which have been apportioned out of men's club sponsorships, the numbers have consistently disappointed.

The result of that is that we're seeing huge losses being incurred by the women's arms of the big men's professional football clubs.

"Activated Audience" vs "Fan Base"

So what happens? England win Euros in 2022. That creates a massive buzz. We then have what we would call an activated audience, not a fan base. And this is the difference that I think people have really struggled to get to grips with.

Activated audience will be there for a short amount of time, but it is much different to a fan base that will turn up on a Tuesday night when it's pissing with rain and be there consistently throughout a season, giving you their attention and their money.

I had a look at some statistics just in the US, because that's the market held up as the real prominent one for women's football. I just wanted to see whether the quality of that product was recognized there maybe in a different way to what it is here.

The US women's national team achieved a peak attendance of 32,000 in 2025 for a friendly against Brazil at a 70,000-capacity stadium in Los Angeles. That was the only crowd at their 15-won-five home matches to break 30,000, and only three others attracted more than 20,000 spectators.

So even in the prime market for women's football, there is an attendance issue. There is a challenge with getting enough people through the gates.

薪资结构的失控

Product. Product innovation, let's just call it that more broadly.

What I do want to talk about just is pay. It's where we started the conversation, but we've been through a history and a landscape. I just mentioned the fact that Women's Super League clubs are losing such large sums of money that significant clubs outside of WSL1 do not want to be in WSL1, because you lose too much money. That's how unattractive being in it is.

If you are expected just to stand still to lose three, four, five million pounds a year and growing all the time, then of course, for anyone other than a very large men's Premier League club who can effectively afford to make it as a CSR payment out of their revenues of £300 million, then for any other kind of female football enterprise, that becomes a very off-putting thing.

So what have they done to address this? They've moved from a situation of having almost no cost controls whatsoever. Just think about that for a moment. Just as men's football is desperately trying to bring in cost controls and largely succeeding, women's football has bravely decided, "No, we don't want cost controls."

And now they have brought in a cost control. The cost control is that on player wages, you're allowed to spend 80% of your revenue. And then on top of that, either an additional 25% or an additional £4 million, whichever is greater. So either 105%, or 80% plus £4 million, which will be a minimum of at least 110% by definition, whichever is greater.

So if you look at a typical WSL club, taking out Arsenal for a moment — typical Women's Super League club, let's say revenues around about £4–5 million — it can spend basically around £7–8 million on playing wages. So they're basically able to spend about 180% of their revenues on playing wages, at a time when men's football is crunching it down to 60%, 70%, maybe 75%.

So just as men's football is trying to put the genie back in the bottle at all levels — even going down into leagues where frankly the pay is not as big as it is sometimes in WSL1 — just as men's football is trying to get the genie back in the bottle and trying to make its clubs sustainable and safe, women's football is saying, "Tell you what, 150, 160% of revenue spent on playing wages."

That's all great. That's up to £7 million, did you say, for some of those clubs on wages? Well, obviously for other clubs — when it comes to Chelsea and Arsenal, we're talking about £11–12 million.

I was only just thinking it because you said similar to men's. I was in that conversation we referenced last week when I sat down with Grimsby CEO, Polly. She was talking about their wage bill at £3.5–4 million.

Absolutely. Women's Super League clubs, in some cases, edging up towards men's championship budgets. For clubs, in the case of your club, Chelsea Women, that get 6,000 or 7,000 people through the gate and are then spending £11–12 million on playing wages, which is a small men's championship club — you're looking at massive imbalances going on here, particularly as the ticket prices aren't all that great as well.

50% 人口神话与"团结性消费"

When it comes to the growth profile of women's football and women's sports in general, particularly women's football, I often hear the argument — and bear in mind, my professional background is basically trying to flog tickets. So let's not put too much. I'm the guy in the trenches at a sports organization, or advising a sports organization, saying "how can we get more punters through the gate, and when they do come, how can we get them to spend more on food, beer, and merchandise?" So it's not a very glamorous trade, but it does mean that unlike a lot of people who comment on this very freely, I have done this at the sharp end, including for women's professional football clubs at both Sunderland and at Charlton. Had to think about how can we get more people through the gate.

The argument I'm often told about the growth potential of women's sport — but women's football in particular — is: "Well, 50% of the population are women, and they're currently not served at all by men's sport, so we're just leaving this massive open goal, and all these women basically are natural women's professional football spectators."

I don't believe in that read-across. It is a fundamental underlying presumption of a lot of the growth narrative that sits around women's sports in general and around women's professional football in particular — is the thought that basically all women should rather watch women play football than watch men play football.

I have no doubt that there are some women who would and who do. Equally, there are actually some men who prefer watching women's sport than men's sport. I actually just said I prefer watching women's rugby to men's rugby, for instance, as it happens.

So this is not about — you can be a man who prefers watching certain women's sport, and you can very much be a woman who prefers watching men's sport. But there is no entitlement to support just on the basis of gender solidarity. That's not how people spend their leisure pound.

In the few times where I've had disagreements with people over this, the first question I'll always ask if someone says anything about this discussion topic is, "Do you go and watch a women's football match?" And the majority of the time, the answer is no. They go and watch a men's football match if they want.

Who is it? Who are you having that conversation with? People around, friends. Is this girls or boys? Girls. If the question comes back, "Do you go? Do you go?" then their answer the majority of the time is no.

男足的部落主义 vs 女足的社群

The reason that men's football is where it is is because it has a tribalism. And look, there's a really bad element to a lot of men's football fandom. What women have been able to do for women's sport in the community side, and this is a deep conversation — the groundings of women's football, we mentioned it in marginalization, but how it's been built out of that, the openness, the inclusivity, the communities that it serves, the fans that it does generate — that is an amazingly valuable thing in sport.

Men's football: toxic aggression, tribalism. There are things in there that are absolutely not what we should aspire to. But unfortunately, those same cultures, they produce attachment, routine, a sense of belonging. And that, if we're looking at this fundamentally, for me, that is the difference. Women's football doesn't have that kind of cultural grounding, and therefore, it's much harder for them to create the fan base in the same way that men's football has.

So it comes back to the point of why be the same product?

But it has to, doesn't it, Charles? Because who else is going to pay the subsidies? That's the problem. So just to go back — in other words, if there are massive losses being made every single year, who is going to pick up those losses?

Karen Carney effectively has created those losses, OK, through her report and the FA's acceptance of that report. And what was never addressed in her report was who pays the bill. I know from speaking to people inside women's professional football that the assumption has always been: "Well, the men's clubs should pay it. It's part of their social duty."

什么是真正有价值的——以及"车前马后"的问题

I just want to tell you what I think is great about girls' and women's football. Speaking as somebody who's got a little six-year-old girl who plays football every week, runs around in her Charlton Athletic kit with her friends every single week playing football — the sport which I want to see her play the most is football. So the opening up of opportunity for as many girls as possible to come in and play football is one which I wholeheartedly support.

Number two, I believe in every sport there should be the opportunity to take that up to an elite level. I think it's absolutely crucial to provide the aspiration to drive those who are ambitious to be able to take it up to an elite level. So yes — an elite league, absolutely yes, that should exist.

So what we're talking about here is not whether there should be women's football, girls' football, not whether there even should be elite women's football, elite girls' football. The question is simply a sports industry one, which is: has the cart been put before the horse here?

In other words, in predetermining that these athletes — who not many people want to watch — are going to get paid at the same level as other sports who are watched by an awful lot of people, and then creating this enormous financial void, effectively, which is a black hole which people just have to throw money into to keep the Carney Report's dream alive — is that actually serving the long-term interests of elite football in this country?

And because what it does do, and just leave you this thought, is it makes it vulnerable. It makes it really vulnerable.

I'm seeing some stuff happening. A couple of seasons ago, Wolves, who were in the Premier League at the time — their women's side was promoted, or was in the league which promotes into the Women's Championship. And Wolves basically said, "No, we don't want it."

I saw the other day that Plymouth, who have run a very successful girls' and women's program for a number of years, have made the decision that they no longer want to be a semi-professional club. They want to go back to being an amateur club. And one of the reasons for that is really super interesting: "Hang on a second, what are we actually doing here? We're bringing in players from all areas outside of Devon and Cornwall to try and make us competitive in a system where there are no business and financial reasons for us to be elite and super competitive. And in so doing, we're actually narrowing the opportunities for local girls to be able to make their way through into a competitive first team."

So actually, what are we doing existentially? What is the point of what we're doing?

And that is the question I ask about the Carney Report. That is the question I ask about Women's Super League — which is, what are we actually trying to do? Why could we not have stuck with the original thought, which is a closed league, maybe playing in the summer, establishing franchises in the right parts of the country where everyone has access to them, not pinning them into men's teams, not expecting men's teams to pick up the bill — and instead, as women's rugby is doing, gradually growing an ecosystem that finds its own place in the world and can then not be vulnerable to when a men's football club decides, "You know what, we need to cut costs, sorry, we're just going to get rid of you."

Good stuff. Well managed. I'm not sure about that. I think you'll find we're in the stocks, Charles. But there we are. Let's see.

七、F1 赞助：从烟草到 Gucci 的十年裂变

Gucci 入主 Alpine

F1 sponsorship, Charlie. F1 sponsorship. This comes off the back of Gucci Racing coming into the space. If any of you saw last week, there was a pretty big announcement as Gucci became the title sponsor of Alpine from the start of next season.

Sorry, just in case anyone can hear sound effects — that's not my tummy as a result of not having had lunch. There's an enormous thunderstorm happening outside, which might well be the God's judgment on Charlie and Charlie discussing women's professional football. It's quite incredible, isn't it? The studio is literally like a reinforced box as well, and we can still hear it.

Back on to Gucci Racing, Gucci Formula One. So reportedly $150 million multi-year deal to be the title sponsor of Alpine. How many years was it, Charles? I couldn't find. I said multi-year. Multi-year could be anywhere between two and five. My guess would be a three-year deal. I think five years would be a long time. You're talking about $50 million a year for three years. Based on where the market's at at the moment, that would be a complete guess, and it is a guess because, as you said, it hasn't been specifically outlined.

Either way, Gucci and Formula One — not as a minority partner on a car, but actually taking full control of the naming rights of one of the 11 teams. It's quite an extraordinary development, and just the latest iteration, I would say, of the shift in types of companies that are now building value off the back of Formula One, really in the last five to six years at scale.

Liberty Media、Drive to Survive 与"反方向看望远镜"

So I thought it was worth us having a quick look at this sponsorship piece, because it's highly relevant for Formula One and what it actually shows about the development of the sport. But it's also a really interesting pointer to how all sports need to be looking — and how this market, and the key commercial driver — we're talking about women's football there and the challenge to generate money — are you speaking to the best people to actually help you do that, because the landscape has changed in the last 20 years significantly, and nothing shows it better than Formula One.

But if we just give a little bit of the oversight of Formula One as a commercial entity — let's not get bogged down — it's been huge since Liberty Media bought it. Drive to Survive gave it a massive profile. I actually looked this up, Chas — it's almost 10 years ago. Yeah, incredible, isn't it?

Well, it was nine seasons of Drive to Survive. So I'm just going to sort of hold my hand up here to sort of dinosaurism. I was working in Formula One at the time, and this was 2017. And I remember having a dinner with a bunch of Formula One journalists. Formula One journalists were like sort of Stockholm syndrome victims — they were locked in this relationship with Bernie Ecclestone where they couldn't live with him, couldn't live without him. He would haul them into his motorhome and give them a few tidbits about the politics behind the sport, and in return would expect the sport to be publicized.

And they just couldn't imagine a world without Bernie. Since so much of the drama around Formula One was effectively being generated by and around one man, if you took that man out, what might happen?

It was quite funny, because I remember that the first two American representatives of Liberty who came into the paddock — one was called Chase Carey, distinctive-looking fellow with big moustaches, and then there was a commercial guy called Sean Bratches. They arrived in the paddock. A significant portion of the press pack that currently covers F1, both for the English/British media but also for what you might call the international media, are British journalists. And Fleet Street British journalists are somewhat cynical, skeptical people at the best of times. But this particular group who were in Stockholm syndrome with Bernie — when they saw these characters turning up in the paddock, talking in that slightly Ted Lasso style about what was going to happen to the sport — believe me, they didn't. And I have to include myself in this, because I remember the dinner when it was all being discussed, and I was not the one there saying, "Do you know what, I think this is all going to work."

Well, my goodness me, have they (slash we) all been proven wrong in the last 10 years.

It comes back, doesn't it, to when we talk about how a sports organization needs to behave once it's taken over an entity. A lot of people saying, "Well, you know, Formula One's been successful from Drive to Survive." It's like, yeah, but they'll forget that the executive put in to go and make these decisions needs to be of the quality to understand where opportunity actually lies. These things don't just happen.

Do you remember the distinction I made a few moments ago when talking about women's professional football, about the distinction between producer interest and consumer interest? Funnily enough, F1 under Bernie started with producer interest. He himself was a team owner. And he basically said to his fellow team owners — albeit who were slightly grander teams than he had been — "Hey, look, if you give me the rights to the TV rights, the whole thing, and I can put the whole thing together, I can make us all rich."

So it had always been run as this sort of thing where it's Bernie, former team owner; Ron Dennis, Frank Williams, all these guys; and then the Ferrari people — all starting basically from "this is our thing, we need to make as much money out of it as possible." Okay, what can we do to make money out of it?

And Liberty, as far as I can tell, being a media organization fundamentally previously, looked at it through the exact other telescope. They said, "OK, we will look after the producers exceptionally well if we work out what the consumers actually really want." And they then made a bunch of bets on what the consumers would actually want: take a helmet off a driver, bring the real characters from the paddock to the forefront of the sport. You can't have learned to someone like Günther Steiner.

Cost controls. Cost controls was huge. Really the biggest. To make it more competitive.

The first thing they did was: more visible, more entertaining, more characters brought to the fore. They thought that's what the competitive world of global sports eyeballs needs — "for them to buy into our sports, our industry, we need to give them more of what we think they want, and if that happens then a whole bunch of other really good stuff will start to happen." With that extra audience will come more sponsorship; with more sponsorship and more cost controls will come more investors; with more investment coming in, the whole thing will lift off even more. And it's just been like some sort of business school case study of how to get it right.

商业数据：F1 进入"全球三巨头"

There's no better way to test the effectiveness of that than to actually look at the commercial appeal of it. Some of these numbers:

$2.54 billion was generated in 2025. Second highest of any sports series in the world. And only just behind the NFL — about $100 million behind.

So the top three commercial generators in the world are: - NFL first, ~$2.6 billion - Formula One second, ~$2.5 billion - Premier League third, ~$2.1 billion

You're talking about three sports entities in the world that are hauling in more than $2 billion a year of commercial revenue. And now Formula One is one of those three.

And Mercedes contributed $558 million of that. Doesn't that show you how competitive it is? Because we just said they're a fifth of the total. There aren't that many teams.

So that means that even teams like Alpine, who are the ones who have just brought in Gucci as their main sponsor — we just reckoned $50 million a year to be their title sponsor — even those teams further down the grid… Back when I was working in F1, they were all on their knees desperate for sponsorship. We listened to Claire Williams when she was — and she was telling us 10 years ago she'd be selling the family silver to try and get sponsors in. Now the battle for $50–100 million title deals per year is rife.

2000 vs 2026：赞助商画像的剧变

This is where there's a really interesting shift. It's the who. Technology, AI businesses coming into this space.

In 2000, your sponsors — this is going to be hilarious — were Shell, Marlboro, B&H, British American Tobacco, Orange — and Red Bull was actually a sponsor of Sauber in 2000. Tobacco spent $250 million with almost every car having a tobacco partner.

2026: Oracle, HP, Mastercard, Revolut, Atlassian, Visa Cash App, Gucci. LVMH have a $1 billion 10-year deal with Formula One Central.

You have gone from booze, petrol, fags, with maybe a telco company in there. Just starting to happen, yeah. To some of the most innovative, valuable entities in global industry.

Technology sector is the biggest contributor to that sponsorship with $769 million. That's a rise of 40%. And that's your Oracles, CrowdStrikes, TeamViewer, who you sponsor Man United. AI companies have emerged as a huge participant in this now, with things like Anthropic leveraging Claude, Eleven Labs. And then financial things — whether it's just traditional financial services, or fintech, Mastercards, Revolut. These kind of platforms that have recognized that there's a huge value to be had while trying to speak to a younger audience and converting them into higher-value, should we say higher-ticket sponsorships. Formula One's the place where they're going to do this.

赞助商的"为什么"——Revolut 的逻辑

Then there's a why. And this is where I think we hit a massive problem in sports sponsorship in general at the moment. Identifying it is one thing: who can we reach out to? But what's the compelling proposition to put in front of them? Do we know fully what we have at our disposal, i.e. rights holder, individual asset? Do I understand fully what I can give to this partner to make that valuable to them?

Formula One is a brilliant way to show how teams have started to realize that. I spoke to Antoine Le Nel at the start of the year, who's the Chief Marketing Officer of Revolut. This was a few weeks before they did their big launch with Audi. And some of the things he said on the show were fascinating when trying to understand why a multi-billion fintech was taking on a, let's say, between $50–100 million partnership with the Formula One team. Which, by the way, is about the same as a front-of-shirt sponsorship for one of the biggest football clubs in the world.

So it's a huge investment. It's like sponsoring Man United on the front of the shirt. And he said this:

"This is a massive sponsorship decision. This is a huge business decision. A company like Revolut will activate more than a company like Aramco. For a sport like Formula One, a super-innovative tech company is bringing the sport to the audience as well as the sport bringing us to the audience. The incredible thing with this sport is we're doing a great job of modernizing it, but we're building on its legacy. It's really hard to take IP from scratch and develop it without any legacy."

What I really liked about that is he wasn't saying that we're suddenly extracting value that hasn't existed before. He's saying even the tobacco companies, even the petrol companies, they always extracted a level of value out of Formula One that they wanted to return to. The difference was they were just speaking to a slightly different type of consumer.

So what a Revolut has realized now is that to hit the objectives that they have in a sponsorship category, Formula One has become a place where the people they most want to speak to are spending their time. We talk about attention economies and the competition variables and all of this. The hardest thing to do when you're trying to build commercial validity is to showcase that you can sell those businesses to their audience. And Formula One's doing a great job of doing that.

And even when he started breaking down the markets, he said, "Well, they're in 17 of our 20 markets." So what he meant by that is, "I can split that total cost across 17 different budgets. So while it may be, let's say, $70 million, just split it down the middle, that can go across 17 different countries. And that makes that a far more investable commitment than it would be just lumping a whole $70 million out of one P&L."

Also: Audi, Revolut — premium brand, but it's relatable. He said it's an "accessible luxury." He'd looked at it. He said: Audi — premium brand, not accessible to the majority of people. Ferrari — did I say it? You said Audi. That's what I was thinking, slightly puzzledly.

As a man who has driven an Audi in the past, I've never thought of myself as being a plutocratic sort of Bentley driver. So you meant Ferrari. Ferrari — non-accessible. Audi — accessible. Premium brand, accessible. Revolut: want to be a premium brand on the finance side, but we want to be accessible.

So that's the level of detail. Now, that's just at the surface. Then you start to go into what they really want. I won't just speak for Revolut on this, but let's speak for the technology companies in general. What they really want to be doing is integrating products into organizations and then into the everyday lives of the audiences that they're interacting with. So if it's a payments app, how can we make sure every transaction is Revolut-driven, if we're going to use that example — or Airwallex for McLaren, or Mastercard for McLaren, or whatever?

They want to have the teams that are using them or sponsoring them actually providing value, not just from "oh, this is a nice shop window — Premier League problem, oh, we sell a shirt space, we sell a sleeve, we sell a hoarding." No, no, no. We want to get you integrated into our business.

Sometimes more convincing the other. Do you remember our infamous HP statement when they got involved with Ferrari? Oh, brilliant. We weren't quite clear that somebody in Ferrari using an HP computer was definitely going to revolutionize Ferrari as a technological innovation hub.

销售一个真正能买的东西

So yeah, obviously I agree with you that there has been a much more creative perspective element to recent partnerships than was historically the case. But I just want to come back to something that you said a few minutes ago, which is: it's matching the audience that the sponsors want with stuff that you have.

Only so many people out there in the world, even in our audience, have actually been in the business of going out and selling sponsorships. But I believe that in this room, we have two. Where you want to be is when you walk into the room knowing that what you have to sell is actually genuinely in their interest to buy.

But all too often when you're selling a sports sponsorship — like, as you say, just a front of shirt for a football club — you're kind of aware that, are you really going to be able to deliver something that really makes a significant difference to that business? Being cognizant of the fact of what their corporate aims are.

All too often the answer to that is not really. What you're hoping is almost you catch them in a moment of weakness, or you catch a marketing director who happens to be a football fan — even better if he happens to be a football fan of the club you're trying to sell him — or just that they all just have it as part of their general allocation to sports as a whole: "We probably should be involved in football in some way, shape or form."

But what we're talking about here is something much more sustainable, which is that global corp after global corp is deciding in a very deliberate way that the best way for them to gain access to high-spending segments of the global audience is through Formula One. And I looked down that list of Formula One corporate sponsors — the ones who sponsor F1 as a whole as opposed to individual teams. And it was almost like it's got to the stage where it's almost a bit embarrassing not to be a sponsor of Formula One.

"Are there any really large international corps that are not sponsoring? Are you guys poor? Hard times? Really? So you've actually made a decision not to get involved in F1. What does that say about you? You don't want to be involved in the fastest-moving, most innovative sport on earth, which also has really affluent global audiences. What does that say about you?"

So that is over 10 years.

从 Bernie 末期到 Liberty 时代：商业模式的本质

Towards the back end of Bernie's time, of course, the tobacco sponsorship had largely gone for regulatory reasons. But it was tough times in the sponsorship time, in the teams at that point — because the tobacco had gone and it hadn't been replaced with new categories. I remember speaking to the McLaren salespeople around that time, 10, 11, 12 years ago, and they were like, "This is really, really tough."

Just to explain Formula One teams' business models to people: they, of course, do not themselves get ticketing revenue. Ticketing revenue is coming into a mixture of Formula One Central and the venues themselves. They do get a central distribution payment, but that's never going to keep them afloat.

So fundamentally, they are actually sponsorship businesses. Sponsorship is the lifeblood of Formula One teams. And really, largely outside of the works teams — which obviously do have greater support from the infrastructure of the car brands that own them — in terms of the independent teams, they've always had to survive on the sponsorship.

Gucci Racing 的品牌意义

But you know what, jolly, it's astonishing. It's also — it's not even just the money. It's watching Gucci create Gucci Racing. Create a new logo. You're talking about proper heritage brand here. This isn't them going, "Do you know what, we've got a bit of budget, let's go and test it out." This is proper buying.

Are you a Gucci guy, by the way? I would love to be. As in, do you have any Gucci garb? No? No, Gucci loafers, obviously, you wear occasionally. I haven't… You're not in? You're genuinely my problem if I'd ever wanted them. No one makes big enough shoes. Oh, this is back into Norwegian ski jumper stuff. It's a big problem.

But no, it lives in premium, like elite premium. And the thing about premium brands generally is, it takes a huge amount for them to shift branding. So to have this alongside the deal where it's now got its own category — God, if you are Alpine, this is so exciting. We could do so much with this. This will be so much more valuable to them as a racing team than just some of the cash that comes through, if they nail it.

给足球俱乐部的警告

But the challenge is, these organizations — if you're a football club, they're there for you too. AI companies need credibility and cultural relevance fast. This year I think there's 11 or 12 or 13 football clubs that last year in the Premier League were sponsored by betting companies on their front of shirts. From next year, betting companies will no longer be allowed on the front of shirts in the Premier League.

Where are you going for these new partners? These people, these companies, should absolutely be on the radar. But what you can't do — and this is where the challenge has dropped previously — is you can't go in there with your little deck showing your front of shirt, your sleeve, and your perimeter advertising: "We are a historic club with an established fan base."

Structured partnership programs, tiered, multi-touchpoint, activation built in. It's not just fabric space. Show them the audience. Show them the integration. Show them the reach. Show them the — don't give all our secrets away, Chaz.

But in all seriousness, you're looking at this now as a massively impactful moment in sponsorship. If you're actually breaking down the difference in spend, there is a lot out there that you can grab onto. But you have to be good enough and you have to understand how to go and speak to these organisations. And that's where you need to level up.

收尾：十年后的回望

It's a way of reflecting back on 10 years since the acquisition. And if somebody had said down the track that all of these massive luxury brands would be piling into it, that teams would be making profits and their valuations would have gone up by 10x, that skeptical press call would have been looking extremely disbelieving. So it's an amazing job. Well done. And let's see what the next five to 10 years hold.

Brilliant. A lot covered today, Charlie. I think that I can do all that. Thank you so much. Thank you, Charlie.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：女足"生产者利益"陷阱与奥运"生产者利益"悖论是**镜像的两极**
主持人在节目里同时讨论了两件看似无关的事——Kirsty Coventry 拒绝向奥运选手付奖金，与英国女足超级联赛过度向球员倾斜薪资。表面看，一个是"该付而不付"，一个是"付得太多"，恰好相反。**但它们本质上是同一种结构性病灶：决策权与消费者之间的链条断裂。**
- 奥运：IOC 总裁靠各国小型联合会的投票当选 → 钱必须流向"选票"而不是"运动员"或"观众"。 - 女足：Karen Carney 报告把"为球员创造世界级条件"放在第一位，"球迷"在排序末尾 → 钱流向球员而非产品打磨。
两者都是 **producer interest** 凌驾于 **consumer interest** 之上，区别只是"生产者"是谁——选票生产者 vs. 球员生产者。这暴露了一个被人忽视的系统真相：**任何运动的崩坏，最早可观察到的信号都是"它的预算结构里，最终消费者排第几"**。
## 二、跨领域连接：F1 的复活与 Liberty Media 的"望远镜反转"，其实和女足的失败、奥运的傲慢

item.title: [SemiAnalysis Weekly] Ep. 14 - Finding Miscompiles For Fun, Not Profit | Justin Lebar & Jordan Nanos
item.dc:creator: SemiAnalysis Weekly
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的发现：成熟度悖论

最让人意外的观察是 Justin 指出的"代码量越少，编译器越脆弱"这一隐藏规律。我们直觉上认为 GPU/ML 编译器是前沿尖端技术，理应更精良。但真相恰恰相反——C++ 编译器之所以"健壮"，并非因为工程师更聪明，而是因为它每天被亿万行代码"压力测试"。GPU 编译器缺的不是天才，而是暴露面。

这揭示了一个更深的系统性真相：软件质量不是被"写"出来的，而是被"磨"出来的。这与生物免疫系统惊人相似——免疫力不来自基因设计的精巧，而来自反复暴露于病原体。ML 编译器就像一个生活在无菌室里的孩子，看起来健康，实则脆弱。LLM 介入的本质，是给这个无菌系统人工合成了一个"病原体库"。

跨领域连接：Fuzzing 是搜索，LLM 是阅读——这是认识论的范式转移

Justin 描述的两种 bug 发现方法，对应着两种截然不同的认知模式： - Fuzzing = 行为主义心理学：不关心黑箱内部，只通过输入-输出来推断异常。代价是边际收益递减——找到的 bug 越多，剩下的越藏得深。 - LLM 阅读代码 = 现象学解读：直接进入"意识内部"，理解意图与实现的偏差。

这个转变与科学史上从天文观测到理论物理的跃迁结构同构。第谷靠观测积累数据（fuzzing），开普勒-牛顿则通过阅读"宇宙这本书"导出定律（LLM）。Justin 在采访中无意间触碰到一个深刻问题：当我们能"阅读"一个系统时，我们就不再需要那么多"实验"它了。这也是为什么 Ultra Code 的 token 效率提升了 10 倍——它跳过了无意义的试探。

隐藏的激励扭曲：为什么没人愿意花一万美元防火

Justin 那句"没人因预防火灾被授勋"看似随口一说，实则点破了软件行业（乃至整个现代组织）最深的结构性病灶：可见的英雄主义压倒不可见的预防工作。这与公共卫生领域的"疫苗悖论"完全同构——疫苗越成功，越没人记得疫苗的功劳，反而会因为"看起来没必要"而被削减预算。

这里有一个令人不安的预测：随着 LLM 让 bug 预防变得便宜（1000-10000 美元），那些不使用 LLM 做预防的团队将不再有"我们没资源"的借口。这意味着软件 bug 将从"技术问题"转变为"道德问题"——一个可以用一个工程师一周薪水避免却没避免的 bug，性质上接近"过失"。Justin 写这篇文章的真正杠杆点，不在于教技术，而在于重新定义责任的边界。

一个值得警惕的元层观察

最后一个让人脊背发凉的细节：Justin 承认他改写 LLM 写的代码时，有 50% 的概率会被 LLM 反过来指出 bug。这意味着在编译器这种极端复杂的领域，人类工程师的可靠性已经接近 50/50。我们正在悄悄进入一个时代——人类不再是"质量的最终守门人"，而成为协同验证网络中的一个节点。这不是 AI 取代人类，而是一种更微妙的状态：专业权威的扁平化。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Hello everyone, welcome back to 7 OS Weekly. This week I'm with Justin Labar. We're going to talk about an article that he put up last week called Finding Miscompiles For Fun, Not Profit. Also known as "you don't need access to Cloud Mythos to spend $10,000 in an afternoon."

Justin, welcome to the show. Hot off a $10,000 afternoon. How are you feeling? How are you doing?

Happy to be here.

[gasps]

文章概览：两种编译器与两种方法

Okay, at a high level, can you can you talk me through what you covered in the article? It wasn't one of our longest articles recently, but it's actually gotten the most most likes on Substack of something that we put out in a while. So, I think people really enjoyed this one.

Happy to happy winning some internet points here. What I went through in the article was the process of finding bugs in two compiler stacks.

The first one being Nvidia's closed-source compiler for PTX. So, PTX is is this low-level assembly or maybe physical high-level assembly language that's emitted by various compilers and then it goes into that's the input into PTXAS. And the output from PTXAS is the machine code that actually runs on the GPU. So, the process of finding bugs in that and also the process of finding bugs in LLVM.

Um and I looked at two backends in LLVM, um the AMD GPU backend. So, that would be if you write like hip code and it compiles to plain. Um and also the x86 backend. And the shared code between them that's sort of um used by all of most of the backends in LLVM.

两种 Bug 发现方法

And I looked at kind of two different ways of finding bugs in the compilers.

The first way is kind of a more traditional way called fuzzing where essentially you generate random programs and um run them through the compiler and then somehow check that the output after you compile the code is the same as before. And there's some subtleties to that that we can go into if we want. But essentially that's all you're doing. So, I did some fuzzing.

And then the other thing I did was LLVM or LLM assisted bug finding, where I just literally unleashed the agents on the code and said, "Please read the code and find bugs." Um and we talked about the difference between these two approaches, and kind of also speculated a little bit on what might be possible in the future.

动机：为什么 ML 编译器更可能有 Bug

Awesome. Can you talk a little bit about the motivation to do a project like this? Like where it came from in your mind, and then we can dig into you know, what exactly you did in more detail.

I've been working on compilers for a decade. Compilers really for GPUs and machine learning. And find and bugs are bugs are terrible in compilers, but I think actually it's kind of worse in the ML world than it is in the CPU world.

Um and that's because if you think about like a regular C++ compiler, it's had hundreds of millions, maybe billions of lines of code run through it. And if there's a miscompile, like there's some chance that someone is going to notice. That chance goes up the more lines of code you run through the compiler.

In contrast, like when we're compiling for GPUs, um there's just so much less GPU code in the world.
