And this announcement doesn't change that. You're not gonna say, "Hey, Google, I'm looking for machine learning engineers in San Francisco who have a background at startups," because it's not built for that kind of thing.

So there is an opportunity to build a new type of search engine that's meant for extremely deep, complex queries that businesses really care about and agents really care about.

为什么从零开始：思想实验与 The Bitter Lesson

Yeah, absolutely. And to the extent — you talked about starting to build Exa five years ago, and then it feels like in the last five years, the world has completely changed, which is actually, in my opinion, a great thing to happen to you and to Exa while you're building, because you can bring in this new technology versus trying to either fight it or have some sort of innovator's dilemma. Maybe share a little bit more on how you decided to build Exa from the ground up, and what parts were the hardest or have been the hardest? And then how has it changed as the world of LLMs have changed?

Building from the ground basically — in 2021, we were like, we could build a better search than Google. I don't care how long it takes. I guess we were young, high energy, just ready to do anything, devote our lives to this.

And there was a thought experiment that really excited me, which is that: wait, I could totally build a better search than Google right now, and here's how I would do it. For every query, I would take all the trillion documents on the web and I would run GPT-3 over it. I would say, does this document match the query? Does this document match the query? And that would filter it down to the top 10 documents, and that would be better than Google.

The problem is that would cost like $10 billion per query. And so then it became an optimization problem. But at least there was an existence proof that it's possible to build a better search than Google. And that was very inspiring for me.

So then it was like, okay, how do we optimize the hell out of that? Transformers had gotten really good at the time, and Google wasn't really leaning into it. And we just had this deep belief in the bitter lesson, maybe more than Google did, which was for search — if we could develop neural systems where you pour more compute, pour more data into it, it just gets better and better for the thing we're optimizing for, then you could actually just totally be better than Google.

2022 年 11 月发布：与 ChatGPT 的十四天

When we released this to the world in November 2022, it was actually shocking. Andrej Karpathy retweeted it. It got pretty popular on Twitter. It was like this new way to find information. I think it was the first time people were like, "Holy cow, it's possible to find things beyond Google."

And then, by the way, two weeks later, ChatGPT came out. So then people realized, okay, there's another new way of finding information outside Google with LLMs. And that was very critical to us.

So we released our first search engine to the world a year and a half after starting Exa, November 2022. Two weeks later, ChatGPT came out. To me, I was at NeurIPS and I saw the announcement. I played with it. I was like, this feels like GPT-3 but a little bit better UI. And I went back to looking at research papers.

But to the world, I think it was the first time they met this new creature. And it was just easy to use, which was a very big learning: okay, you make something easy to use, it's very important, obviously.

Anyway, AI started really taking off. And then in early 2023, people started asking us for API access to our search engine that they had used based on that Twitter announcement in November 2022. And that's when we realized — oh wait, we could start serving this search engine to these, not quite agents, because that wasn't the term at the time, just these AI products, these AI workflows. And they're going to want comprehensiveness. They're gonna want to search in these more complex ways.

All the ways that we as nerds in 2021 wanted to search, agents were very similar. So that was another interesting realization: we're not normal. I'm not a normal consumer. I want to get really deep into any topic — and so do agents. And so it was cool that we were building a search engine for ourselves; it ended up being the exact same search engine for agents.

为 Agent 设计搜索：一只为树懒做的外科医生

This is a big paradigm shift. Even as investors, we're thinking like, hey, it's not humans who are deciding the dev tool that wins. It's actually agents. Personal anecdote — I think I told you this one, but we have a family open club, Michael Claude Berg, and we wanted to give it web access. And he was like, "I recommend Exa." Before we invested, I was like, sure, I'll go with whatever you recommend, right? So it sounds like there's this nice dovetailing of how you were intending to build Exa in the first place to what agents want. But how do you think about what agents want? That's sort of the holy grail right now — "I don't care about the database I'm using; my agent's gonna select Convex or Supabase." These are entire tailwinds that are making some of these companies. How do you optimize for that, think about that?

I've been thinking about this for a long time since the first agents. We were an early search engine, and the first AI products came to us because they were like, okay, they could use a search API.

And yeah, the world of agents searching is just completely different from human searching. I guess you make the analogy of agents to humans is like humans to sloths.

Imagine we had a surgeon — and actually thought of this as a dog. I'm picturing that Zootopia DMV scene.

Yeah, that's what I'm thinking too. It's like, imagine we had a great surgeon for sloths and then humans came around. They're not gonna want to use that same surgeon.

So you should think of agents as these crazy creatures that have infinite — time is meaningless for them. They just want to make complex queries very fast and analyze it really fast. And they want perfect output for their human users. So you want to build a surgeon for that.

So what matters for that type of creature? Well, lots of things:

Complex queries. You need assertions that can handle complex queries. You do not want that creature to have to simplify its complex need for its user into simple keyword phrases because you're just losing information. So you want something that can actually semantically handle complex queries, but also handle keywords because sometimes you literally want — hey, I have this complex chemical formula, I want that to be part of the document.

Toggleable / controllable. You want a tool that can handle both semantic queries and keyword queries — really just expose all the fundamental toggles to the agent, because the agent has the patience to make a domain filter here and a keyword filter there. With Google, you search something and you're like, "No wait, that's not what I want," and then you try to change some keywords and it's just missing it. It's not very controllable or toggleable. You want the opposite for an agent because the agent is going to keep searching until it gets to its outcome. And you don't want it to have to make ten thousand keyword queries and still never get to its comprehensive information. You want it to make a few queries and get comprehensive information.

Comprehensive results. An agent doesn't just want 10 results or 10 pieces of information. It wants everything. Because imagine you're an investor — you don't have to imagine that. If you're an investor and you're looking at biotech companies, you want complete information because you're making very important monetary decisions, and you don't want to miss anything. You don't want any FOMO. You're missing some critical startup that exists that actually reflects well or badly on the current one you're thinking about. So you want your agent to have complete information about every topic. With Exa, you could search something and then get not just 10 results or 100 results, but 1,000 results or 10,000. And increasingly, agents are wanting this.

Both lower and higher latency. You also want lower latency because agents search faster than humans. But at the same time, you want higher latency because certain applications don't care about latency at all. Another big thing with serving agents is extreme customizability — we're serving businesses, we're serving agents that are very different. Some want super low latency, some want super high latency, or latency doesn't matter to them.

It's hard to express how different — I have a list of 20 different ways humans and agents are different. And when you just build it from scratch for agents, you make fundamentally different architectural decisions.

为什么造一个更好的搜索引擎，既更容易也更难

So maybe just to go back to this point you made on model intelligence improving and how that's kind of changed the game in search as well. And I want to pose this thought to you that I'm sure you've heard before — but given the fact that model intelligence is getting better, it sort of can almost make up for or do some of the heavy lifting in this user signal that Google has collected over 20 years for PageRank, etc. It can actually help get over that hump. So my question to you is, how do you think about this trade-off of compute, latency, cost? There's all these trade-offs that have to happen in terms of what you're actually using, and to your point, it's a big optimization exercise. Like, how do you think about what to optimize? And I know you have different products, so maybe your answer is, "well, depends on the product offering," but bring that into it as well.

It's easier and harder to build a search engine for AI agents. And so I'll explain why.

Oh, interesting. Yeah, please.

I knew that'd spark your engine.

Why is it easier? First of all, this whole click data — Google has an insane amount of human click data. It just doesn't matter that much for serving agents. I remember saying this years ago; people thought that was crazy, but it turned out to be right. Human click data is great for humans when you want to find results that humans click on. If you get a huge amount of human click data, you could train on that, and Google can understand what you mean even when you don't even know what you mean.

However, agents just don't benefit that much from clicks. Maybe a little bit in terms of the ranking signal — it's valuable for agents to know what humans think is valuable. That's a very minor thing. So it's interesting that all that click data that Google has accumulated just doesn't really matter for agents. It's a whole new ballgame.

There are also other things. Google probably had hundreds of people working on re-ranking, because that was a complex thing before LLMs. But now with LLMs, you could have a re-ranker that you just call an LLM and you have one engineer working on it. Obviously, we have more than one engineer working on re-rankers at this point because you don't want to just call an LLM — you want to train your own models and make them faster, higher quality. But you don't need a team of hundreds; you could do it with a couple people.

So how have we, a team that has always been below 100 people, been able to build a search engine that's better than Google in all sorts of ways? Well, it's because LLMs unlock new types of techniques, and also because serving agents, you don't need all the click data and data that Google has been collecting.

But it's also harder. Why? Because the requirements for a search engine now are getting more and more intense. I wake up in the morning and customers love us, but we still have customers being like, "Wait, why can't you be perfect at this search? Or what about this search?" They're constantly pushing us towards the edge because we're serving business use cases that have deeper and deeper needs — billion-dollar investments on the line. This has to be perfect. And that's great because it's pushing us towards perfect search.

If traditional search engines had 99.9% quality or reliability, these new search engines for agents need 99.99%, 99.999%. This is a great thing because it's pushing us towards that dream of perfect search I've always been dreaming about. It's very similar to LLMs — like Opus 4.6 comes out, everyone's really happy, this is working on 99.9% of use cases. And then when the next version comes out, everyone wants that thing, because the extra nines of quality are so important in this new agentic economy.

搜索会被商品化吗？什么部分还很难？

Yeah, you know, it's interesting. When we were doing our diligence in the space, which has taken place over the last few years now, some people came to us with the opinion that search is just getting commoditized. And I think we look at that and, if you go out there and search for information we know is out there public, you can't find the answers to everything still. So the fact that it's commoditized, you'd have to kind of divide up — oh, what type of search is commoditized? What is the type of search that's still really hard and what is the key to unlocking that? Is it data partnerships? Is it a technical breakthrough? How do you think about the edges in terms of pushing forward the frontier of search?

Yeah, I guess what does commoditized mean? It means over time, will it just not matter which tool you use?

I would argue that LLMs are getting commoditized faster than search is. And the reason is because you don't need to run the smartest model over every cell in your Excel sheet when you're trying to find competitors or something. Most of knowledge work does not require the smartest model. You could just use an open-source model that's big enough, and now the infrastructure for running them is very good — it's pretty cheap. You could just reuse open-source models for most of knowledge work.

Not that the crazy smart LLMs don't have huge value — they do, in terms of inventing new science and math, and in certain cases you want to find any bugs in your code or something like that. But increasingly, if you think of knowledge work as concentric circles of difficulty, a huge amount of that surface area is covered by off-the-shelf models you get right now.

On the other hand, with search — when you are trying to enrich every cell in your Excel sheet with competitors or people you're trying to recruit, then every extra nine of quality in search really matters. I'd argue that a lot of knowledge work is actually a search problem, not only an intelligence problem.

What are some examples where search is not good? I do think company and people search is the most easy-to-see and most valuable to people. Every company in the world has to search over companies to sell to, or has to search for people to hire. You could ask yourself if finding companies to sell to or finding people to hire is a solved problem. I think every company would say, no, it's not. That's why people are constantly switching tools, trying out new tools — we just don't have comprehensive information over all the people or companies we want.

That's a really good example. That's something Exa is leaning very deeply into — go-to-market intelligence — because we care a lot about it. It's also very useful to use internally. We have companies to sell to and we have people to hire. It's been great to dogfood. It gives us some advantage.

一个被低估的洞察：搜索是被伪装的元问题

You didn't ask this, but I think one beautiful thing about search is that a lot of important problems in the world are actually search problems, dressed up in a different way.

Say more. What's the example?

Okay — political polarization. I would argue that's a search problem. There are people out there who want to understand the world, but they're getting fed information that's misleading in some way or straight up wrong. And if everyone had information that was accurate and controllable and comprehensive, I think most reasonable people would be reasonable. Because our information environment is so chaotic, so polarized, it's causing reasonable people to be unreasonable. By the way, I'm part of it. I'm sure I have incorrect beliefs on all sorts of political things because my information is not perfect. It's something I really want to solve.

Loneliness is a search problem.

No one thinks it's lonely. Say more.

A lot of people are feeling lonely in modern society. Well, it's because they're not finding people to hang out with or to be in relationships with. So loneliness is a search problem. Especially in city life, it's hard to find other people with similar interests or who might bond with. With a perfect search engine, with whatever information people are willing to share, it would help.

For example, I have a lot of crazy ideas about flying cars. I really want flying cars. I hate cars on the road. I would love to go to a group of people talking about flying cars. I'm sure I would become great friends with those people. I can't just be like, "Find me all the flying car enthusiasts in San Francisco." I would love to at some point be able to do that.

And this gets to your earlier question — what allows for this differentiation? You can start to see that search is a bigger thing than people think. People think of search as: in 1998, you see a text box, you type in a few keywords, you get a few things. No, search is way broader. Search is coordinating the human species around anything we're trying to do.

How does search become less and less commoditized? It's always about really good retrieval and really good data. If you do perfectly on both those things — you have all the data and you have the best possible retrieval — that is perfect search. So it is both accumulating all the web's data, accumulating data that's not on the web, and then training extremely powerful models to search over it. Index and Retrieve have always been the two pushes at Exa on the engineering side since five years ago.

数据的政治学：与内容方共存

Can you say more about the data element? And no need to share any secret sauce there, but it does feel like the web is getting increasingly closed, to some extent. But there's a lot of fear out there from data providers — "we don't want to be Stack Overflow'd," right? Especially if your business model is around impressions you serve to humans visiting your site. How do you think about that interplay with data providers and making sure you can get to the path of perfect search, but work with these data providers that are maybe becoming more closed?

Yeah, so I want to get to the ideal world where you have perfect search and then everyone's incentivized to create amazing content, even more so than before. I actually think there's an opportunity here to create a system where content providers are making more revenue because they are participating in this massive agentic economy.

I like to think from first principles. The first-principles idea here is that the agentic economy is going to be huge. We're all going to have agents, and those agents are going to be participating. You can imagine it's like agentic economy and cyberspace — they're doing commerce, they're reading information. It's like everything we do on the internet, they're doing, but a thousand times bigger.

So there's a massive amount of value in this agentic economy — meaning money. Instead of hundreds of billions of dollars going to one company, what if $50 billion a year went to one company and the other $150 billion went to all the content providers? There are ways to distribute the value in this new agentic economy that are more favorable towards providers of content. And by the way, it won't be $200 billion, it'll be a trillion dollars a year of value. So if we could figure this out, there's an opportunity for everyone to do amazingly.

编码 Agent 与 Web Search

I loved all the use cases you talked about. There's go-to-market, there's also — I'll put it in the loneliness bucket — finding people you can connect with. One that you didn't talk about that I just want to pause on really quickly is coding. Can you share more about why Web Search makes coding agents more powerful for this use case in particular, and why Exa is such a perfect fit for the coding use case?

Yeah, for sure. Every agent at some point — agents are like humans. At any point when you're coding, or when humans used to code, you would have to look up information. You want the most recent technical documentation, or you might look at a blog for inspiration. Agents are very similar. They really want the freshest information so that every line of code they write does not have some critical error.

Especially with coding agents, the stakes are so high that every extra nine of quality matters. These coding agents are very intelligent right now, but in terms of retrieval quality, they've been in the dark ages — like the early 2000s in terms of search quality. Search can be way better over coding: technical documentation, SDKs — just perfect retrieval over these things. That was our goal. We're not yet perfect, but we're extremely good at search over any sort of coding material.

So when a bunch of coding agents try us — like Cognition, for example. We power Devin now, and they've just found when they tested it that it makes Devin way better, way more accurate, makes way fewer mistakes, which really matters. And this will just continue.

By the way, you can also think about coding agents as: every agent is going to want to do everything. It's not just searching over code. It's also at some point searching over the world's information, being up to date with the news. The coding agent will become your agent. I think coding agents and just general agents are going to merge. It's an interesting trend.

Right, no, I mean, it's clearly Codex is not for developers, right? It's sort of like the everything app.

Tokenpocalypse：检索如何让小模型表现得像大模型

I wanna bring in — mostly because it's all over Twitter right now — and I think it might be an interesting tie to Exa: this topic of token maxing, tokenomics, just the fact that, you know, Uber is talking about how they're spending too much. ServiceNow hit their budget for the year already. Microsoft talked about pulling their Claude Code licenses.

Tokenpocalypse.

Tokenpocalypse — yeah, okay, there we go. Better word for it. We've talked about this before, but in terms of how you think about search actually making token consumption more effective and efficient, can you share more perspective on that? And to the extent you can, what results are you seeing on that front?

Yeah, sure. So retrieval can help solve the tokenpocalypse because we should not be using gigantic models for every task. People are starting to realize this — you should use a family of models of different sizes. The big model decides what to do and it dishes out commands to the small models. And those small models can be way more accurate and reliable if they're using retrieval. So retrieval helps small models act like big models, in a cheap way.

We do save our customers a huge amount of tokens because they can use smaller models and use retrieval. We've also put a lot of research effort into how to extract only the most relevant information from documents so that these models don't have to consume too many tokens — any sort of input tokens can dramatically increase spend. So we could save 20X on cost for customers compared to other providers by being very efficient in what information from the web the agent actually sees.

But in general, smaller models using retrieval is much more efficient. Andrej Karpathy had a tweet about this — I keep mentioning Andrej Karpathy on Twitter.

I know, we all do. Yeah, it's great.

He had a tweet about this a couple of years ago, where it was: the trend is towards smaller, raw intelligence modules using tools. The cost of the model is determined by the number of weights, which determines the cost of inference. If you're wasting those weights on information about the world — like the capital of France or this random blog you read — you're wasting weights. Those weights should be focused only on intelligent processing.

You could probably get to models that are one billion, even less than a billion parameters that are extremely hyper-intelligent and completely unknowledgeable. It's like Einstein who never saw the world. That's how I think about it. And then it uses tools that are very cheap and efficient. That's a much more efficient world that will help solve this compute shortage that is affecting everybody.

Do you think — this is more of like a hot take moment — but do you think that reality will start to take place second half of 2026? Because right now we're in this phase where everyone's playing with the biggest, best model that just arrived. And to your point, probably overspending. When do you think this reality kind of sets in?

I mean, the reality is definitely starting. I don't have the exact — trends are everywhere. When does it become noticeable? I would say by end of 2026, it's very noticeable.

Wow, okay. Kind of a hot take, actually.

作为研究实验室的 Exa：RL 与检索

So you talked about doing the research that you're doing. And, you know, I think Exa is sort of famously structured as more of a research lab, honestly, than what people are calling application layer companies, infrastructure companies. This is sort of a research lab focused on search. And coming out of that, one of the things that we were most excited about, frankly, is just the exciting cutting edge work that you guys are doing. One of those things was actually search as it pertains to RL and there's a lot of efficiencies there, et cetera. I wanted to just flag that because it was an interesting finding. I think you used Tinker — shout out to Thinking Machines. But say more about what you guys are finding there and also just what research directions you guys think about as being important.

Yeah, at a high level, a lot of the big ideas in training LLMs apply equally well to training search models. For example, we do pre-training of embedding models, we do post-training of embedding models, we do RL on search tools. A lot of these things that are working in LLMs work in retrieval too, which is kind of interesting. And you don't hear a lot of people talking about it.

In that RL blog post, we basically tried — people RL on a search tool, but we haven't seen as many studies testing different search tools that you RL on. So we simply RL'd on SERP (so like Google wrapping) versus Exa, and found that RL'ing on Exa does way better. It both uses fewer calls, so it's more efficient, and then it's higher performance.

This makes sense because, again, Exa was designed for agents to use, and so it just allows agents to make more complex queries — really capture what they actually want, as opposed to having to compress what they want into shorter phrases that are more for traditional search engines.

In general, our research is the bitter lesson — just scaling laws in lots of different directions. Some of the ones I mentioned: post-training, pre-training, RL.

We've been pretty under the radar. People don't realize how much research we're doing. We don't publish all of it — we don't publish much of it. But there is a lot to go in search, and I don't think people realize that. We realize it because we've just been obsessed with it. But the biggest thing is actually we're being pushed in all these crazy directions because of our business model and who we serve. We're not serving two billion consumers who are kind of all the same. We're serving over 5,000 businesses that are pushing us in all these crazy directions. Every day it's like, "Why can't this be higher quality over companies or people? Why can't this be faster? Why can't the information extraction be even higher quality?" Research always follows need. So we have insane amounts of needs at Exa to do better, and that's why we do all this crazy research.

Benchmark 与 Ground Truth

So I guess kind of tied to this is — I wanted to ask you about how you think about benchmarks and hill climbing. And I'll say this sort of tongue-in-cheek, but we've noticed that, especially maybe among in your space, but also in other spaces, there seems to be benchmark maxing or whatever you want to call it. And, you know, of course, to no surprise, everyone is always at the top of their own benchmarks.

Yeah, that's how you know something's wrong, that's how you know.

Exactly. So, and obviously you have this relentless pursuit of ground truth. And also self-improvement — I think you're the first to admit, hey, here are the areas we could be better on. We're trying to improve on a continuous basis. But how do you internally think about what benchmarks matter? What is ground truth for you guys in terms of, hey, we're actually better on this front, but worse on this front?

Yeah, 100% the evals have been benchmaxed in retrieval. There aren't too many evals in retrieval, so that's one problem. There aren't that many standard third-party retrieval evals, and they've been benchmaxed and they're not really good representations of agentic search, of what agents actually need.

So it is a problem in the industry where customers can't really know what is true, which is sad. It also demonstrates the need for a really good search engine to distinguish what is true. It's just another example.

But the ground truth for customers is their own A/B tests. When we do A/B, literally they are testing us versus other providers on their use case. If they have enough data to do A/B tests, that's the best. Often they make their own evals. So sophisticated customers will make their own evals; super sophisticated customers will run A/B tests. Customers who just want something might just look at evals that are published online. But certainly for the sophisticated customers when they test us, it becomes a lot clearer who's on the top.

We want to improve this ecosystem. We want to be the research lab that helps improve the ecosystem, publishes things, and even if we're not at the top, we want to show. So you'll see more coming out there.

预测：Agentic Search 在 2030 年大于 Google Ads

So you predicted that agentic search will be a bigger business than Google search by the 2030s. Say more about that. What trends are you seeing that lead you to believe this?

Yeah, I mean, you could get this from basically estimating the number of searches — so the number of LLM calls and the percentage of LLM calls that require search, and then the cost of the search, and then you just play it out. The trend has actually been pretty clear. We've had pitch decks from years ago where we kind of predict where things would go. Maybe we're off by a quarter here or there, but it follows a trend.

If you follow that trend, even conservatively, you get to a massive TAM for agentic search in the 20s — even before late 2020s and then early 2030s.

It's hard to express how many searches will come from agents. Humans on average make a couple searches a day. But agents — when everyone has a personal assistant and every single software tool you use is going to be checking its work with retrieval — the number of searches is going to be... we say thousands because that is understandable and grokable to people, but really it's going to be millions at some point.

The world will be filled with search in a way that the world is filled with electricity. It's a fundamental infrastructure that powers everything. I think less of search as perfect information and more — the world will be filled with the highest quality information.

When the world is filled with something, it's usually a large TAM. If you just play out the numbers, we think it will be bigger than Google ads in 2030. Not that ads won't be a huge part of the world too — ads are important for commerce and that might be a percentage of the agentic search economy too. But the numbers here are insane. And it really comes down to a belief: do you think LLMs will eat the world? Will eat all software? We have always believed that. It seems very true. It seems even more true every month.

瓶颈：从基础设施到数据，再回到检索

What do you think — and there's some debate on this on the LLM side, on the training side too — but what do you think is the bottleneck today versus, let's say, three years from now? I feel like five years is too far to predict. Is it, I think you've said in the past, it's no longer intelligence in terms of bottlenecking search. Is it data, accessible data? How do you think about how that evolves?

Initially the bottleneck is going to be actually the infrastructure, which is kind of interesting. No one realizes, but if you actually get 10x, 100x, 1,000x more searches on Google, the infrastructure to handle that is insanely large. It just hasn't been built yet. So we're really excited to explore all sorts of cool new vector databases that have super high throughput. In the same way there are compute bottlenecks, there'll be infrastructure bottlenecks. It'll be solved at some point.

Other bottlenecks are data bottlenecks. Agents are increasingly going to want to ask questions about the world, and that data might not be on the web. It might not even be recorded anywhere. So there will be this trend towards how can we accumulate all the world's data — literally unearth the world's data.

I'm really excited about this because the world is filled with information and it's not all recorded. The history of humanity — when we were hunters and gatherers, there were all sorts of information; then they started writing things down, and the amount of information in the world has been skyrocketing. There's still so much information that's not recorded. You go from the first clay tablet — or really the first paintings on caves would be arguably the first time things were written down — and then clay tablets, and then newspapers, and then obviously we have the digital age. But there's so much information in your head, satellite images that are not in the world's soup of information that we could search over.

To fully understand the world — fully understand how crop yields are going to affect some company, or what are people thinking about the world, or how do we unite the world — that requires understanding the world at a deeper level. So I think the bottleneck will be data in a lot of ways.

And then once you have all this data, now the bottleneck will be retrieval again.

Right, right, right.

Imagine — this is a crazy idea. As we expand as a species, thinking very far in the future, in the solar system there's so many things going on. There's so much data being accumulated. The retrieval over that is very expensive.

There are really fundamental, interesting questions here. Right now, the web is, call it a trillion pages that matter. What happens if the web were a thousand times bigger, like a quadrillion pages? Meaning everyone started uploading data. Any search algorithm that works over a trillion pages might work over a quadrillion pages, but it might be a thousand times more expensive. And that's not practical because if anything, we want search to get cheaper, not more expensive. So what kind of search algorithms could work over a quadrillion pages? These are fascinating questions that I don't know if I've ever heard anyone else talk about.

SpaceX 实习与 Elon 的领导风格

Well, actually I was going to say — at least thinking about when we're living on Mars or whatnot, there's probably one other founder that has thought about that extensively: Elon. And I listened to your — you went on the Latent Space podcast last year, one of my favorite podcasts, and you talked about actually working at SpaceX. I'm kind of curious, how was that experience? And are there elements of Elon's leadership style that you've taken as CEO of your own company?

Yeah, well, first of all, the internship was magical. For example, I saw the first landing on the barge.

Wow.

Yeah, just outside Mission Control. I'm getting tingles just thinking about it. Just seeing people coming together to do something magical — that was very inspiring and made a big impact on me.

In terms of my leadership style, I like to think that I've incorporated some of what I think are the best aspects of Elon.

Detail-oriented. He gets into details of everything. For better or worse, I do that too at Exa. Everything from the algorithms, the vector DB algorithm, to the office space — making sure every part of the office really inspires and excites and shows our passion. And that goes across everything from our marketing to the engineering to go-to-market.

Obviously, it doesn't scale. So one thing I've been learning as we scale the company is, what details can I choose to go into? I like the eagle metaphor of — I'm an eagle flying above the company. And when I see some detail that I think should be important to fix, I go dive down and go into it and then come back up. I think Elon has some of those properties.

Obviously, Elon is all in every day for, what is it, decades? I've only been doing it for five years, but I intend to do it for decades.

Memetic naming. One last thing that he does really well, which is he's very good at memetic names and inspiring through memetic things. You realize that SpaceX's mission is not "improve rockets to get to orbit" or "make rocket travel really good." It's "make humanity interplanetary." That's a really good memetic thing.

So I think a lot about the names of projects. When I do a team standup every Monday in front of the whole company — we have this double-floor office where people now are surrounding on the top floor, it's really cool, it's like a stadium — I give a speech and I like to simplify what we're doing into: what is the core? What is the memetic core? And come up with a cool name. And that inspires.

By the way, a name is really important because when you have a company of a certain size, people are constantly communicating in ways you're not part of those conversations. And the name really grounds the mission of that project. I could think for a day just about a name.

山羊与 Exa：两个命名的故事

So, totally divergent, but on the topic of names — one, I love, Goat. Talk a little bit about the symbolism of that. Besides "greatest of all time," obviously. And then also, Exa. You shared before what Exa means, but talk more about why you named the company Exa.

Okay, so the Goat thing — basically, there was a coffee shop that opened up next door, and the mayor of SF kept posting about it. So a lot of people go — it's a really cool coffee shop, Hedge Coffee. So many people were walking by, we were like, we gotta get them to look at what Exa is.

We have some posters about Exa on the entrance to our office, but we were like, how do we get them to stop? And I was like, just put a goat. I don't know why I thought this, but just put a goat there. Actually, some part of me wanted a real goat. I don't know how we'd maintain the goat. I think the ROI would still be valuable. But then instead we bought a nice fake goat. I love that goat. And we put the swag on him.

So people stop by. Tons of people stop by. It's so beautiful. Even on Saturday and Sunday, which is when the coffee shop is very popular, people come by and I go and I say hi to them. I talk to them. I tell them about Exa. It's actually very beautiful. We're actually an important stop for kids on their walk to school. You know when you were a kid, you had those stops — you would move to the ice cream store. The goat is a stop. And they're always taking pictures. Very cute.

Starting the recruiting funnel early. That's good.

But it's interesting how much those things matter. We've hired people because of the goat.

Anyway — Exa. I think Exa is a great name. I love Exa. One value of Exa is it's a great prefix. Exa-anything, Exa-data, Exa-this, Exa-that. But Exa means 10 to the 18th, which is in contrast to Google, which is 10 to the 100th. The idea there was: we're kind of overwhelmed with information. Even though you could technically find lots of things on the web on Google, that doesn't mean it's organized and you get the highest quality knowledge. Here it's like 10 to the 18th — you're extracting the most important information. It's still a very large amount of information, 10 to the 18th, but it's not overwhelming. That was one of the ideas.

Great name. We love the name as well. And the goats. My kids love the goats.

反 Grindslop：文化与"眼里的火"

So I wanted to — this topic feels a little bit over-talked about, but it's been in the news more recently, so I'm going to bring it up:

I don't know if you saw, Will Manidis wrote an article recently called "Grindslop." It's almost this backlash. I pulled a quote from it. He said something about: "We're witnessing a phenomenon that masquerades as discipline, but represents perhaps the most extravagant squandering of economic surplus in our civilization's history." So, extreme words. And he talked about all of these articles that are glorifying the grind, all of these things that maybe are more relevant to work-life balance, etc. So I guess I'll start with saying — one of the things that I was actually most impressed and excited by when I visited Exa at, God, I think it was like 8 p.m., was that there were a ton of people in the office. And it wasn't just that they were there for FaceTime, because obviously that's not what you and Jeff care about. But they were excited about what they were working on. And I think really importantly, they were excited by who they were doing it with. So just say more about how you think about that culture of going all in, and what do you think the important things to balance are in culture?

Yeah, this is critical to me, and it should be critical to any company that wants to do great things. People need to be super excited about what they're doing and have a ton of fun.

You might have also heard at 8 p.m. — people are just laughing.

Yeah, oh yeah, absolutely.

We laugh a lot at Exa, and it's almost too loud sometimes, and it's distracting. So you have to have headphones for everybody.

It's very important people are having fun, and that's necessary for doing the best work of your life. I would say, having fun, just good vibes — but also it's very important that people work on the projects that are most exciting to them at any point.

With all these AI tools, anyone — especially on the engineering side or even on the marketing side or ops side — everyone can work on whatever they want. For example, we had someone who built the vector database who was like, "Hey, I want to go train models." I was like, okay, just go do it. He didn't really have experience in training models, but I was like, "You're really smart, you'll learn it in like two weeks. And then you'll just do amazing work." And he's done amazing work.

So I make sure everyone's working on exactly what they want to work on. And luckily in search — at least in our space — in any direction we improve things, it will be good for the business. So it happens to turn out somehow that what people want to work on and what we need perfectly aligns. I don't know how that's possible, but yeah, everyone's working on exactly what they want to work on. They're also working on multiple projects because AI tools will enable you to be productive in parallel.

And then what we're doing is just super cool. It's hard to find these days really exciting, humongous projects and missions that other people aren't doing. And this one happens to be — organizing the world's information, making perfect search. That's very exciting and important for the world, but also really hard problem. And engineers are really excited about that. And on the go-to-market side, we're selling this search to the whole world, and we're selling to some of the coolest companies. They get to talk to all these hot companies and give them a search that really helps their products. It's an exciting space. It's very important to me. So I hope we maintain that forever.

招聘：眼里的火

Well, maybe just to end on one last question — and I'll say, first of all, Exa is hiring. And you told me once that you actually still interview every single person at Exa, or who comes to interview for Exa. And so I'm curious what — you've attracted some of the most incredible talent. Junior, senior, everything in between, very high slope, very experienced. And I'm curious what it is that you look for when you do that last final interview, and how you've been able to get these incredible people to come to Exa.

People might not like this word, but I look for passion. It's like someone's just like a fire in their eye. Do they really care about what we're doing? Or some aspect of doing it. Like, "I really want to scale this thing." Or "I really want to sell this to everyone."

Why is that important? It was important five years ago, but I think it's especially important now because the world goes to who is most passionate, who's most agentic — because you can now do anything. With agentic tools, you could literally do anything. And what matters most is how much you care about the end result. And then your judgment and everything. So I look for the fire in the eye, because if you have that fire, you could literally do anything.

It's actually crazy how meritocratic the world is becoming because of these agentic tools filling the gaps.

Yeah, absolutely. Well, thank you so much, Will. This was great. Such a fun conversation. Appreciate you and very excited to be partners.

Awesome.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、Google 最深的护城河，正在变成它最大的盲区
最反直觉的观察是 Will 那句几乎被一笔带过的话：**Google 二十年积累的点击数据，对 Agent 几乎没有价值**。
这不是一个增量判断，而是一个范式判断。Google 的飞轮是"人类点击→排序信号→更好结果→更多点击"，这套机制建立在"人类懒得打长 query、只看前 10 条"的人类学假设之上。可一旦搜索的主力用户变成 Agent——它们打超长复杂 query、要 1 万条结果、不在乎点哪条蓝链——那么人类点击就不再是"先验信号"，而变成"噪音偏差"：它告诉系统"普通人喜欢什么"，而 Agent 恰恰要的是"普通人懒得找的东西"。
这是教科书级的**资产反转（asset inversion）**：曾经构成垄断壁垒的数据，在新范式下成为系统性偏置。类似的反转在历史上发生过——胶片相机的光学积累在数码时代变成包袱，诺基亚的渠道关系在 App Store 时代变成枷锁。Google 的特别之处在于，它的护城河失效不是被新数据淘汰，而是被"用户物种的更替"淘汰。
## 二、"深度怪人"和 Agent，本质是同一种

item.title: [a16z] Foundation Models are a Commodity | Benedict Evans on a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：AI 革命的"递归自指"特征

Benedict 不经意地抛出了一个被绝大多数人忽视的深层规律——LLM 在某种意义上就是"计算机"，所以人们用 LLM 做的第一件事就是制造更多的计算。这其实是技术史上一个反复出现的"自指悖论"：PC 出现后，人们用 PC 做的第一件事是制造更多计算机；互联网出现后，人们用互联网做的第一件事是搭建更多互联网基础设施；现在 LLM 出现，人们用它做的第一件事是写代码——也就是制造更多 LLM。

这不只是巧合。它揭示了一个隐藏规律：任何足够通用的计算技术，在早期都会被用来"喂养自己"，直到通用性溢出到外部世界。这意味着我们现在判断 AI "只在 coding 上跑通了 PMF"，其实是在描述一种普遍的早期形态，而非 AI 的局限。下一个突破不会是"在某个领域被慢慢使用"，而是某个领域突然像 coding 一样集体相变——历史上每次这种相变的间隔大约是 18–36 个月。

二、跨领域连接：电信运营商悲剧 × McKinsey 永生之谜

Benedict 的电信类比所有人都听过，但他真正没说透的洞见是：为什么 McKinsey、Bain、BCG 这类咨询公司在每一次技术革命中反而越来越值钱？答案藏在他自己的话里——"咨询公司的价值是被允许进入公司、和那些本来不能交谈的人交谈，并且不会被解雇"。

把这个洞见和电信悲剧并置，会出现一个惊人的结论："基础设施层"和"咨询层"是同一个硬币的两面。基础设施层赚不到钱，因为它解决"已知问题"；咨询层永远值钱，因为它解决"无法被写下来的隐性知识问题"。LLM 恰恰只能学到能被文档化的知识，而企业 80% 的真实运作来自从未被写下来的隐性流程——这才是为什么 Foundation Model 公司无论多强大，都无法吃掉真实经济：真实经济的运作逻辑本身就不在训练数据里。

由此推论一个让人意外的预测：未来五年最大的赢家不是 OpenAI 或 Anthropic，而是会出现一种新型混合体——"AI 原生咨询公司"，它们的护城河不是模型，而是"被允许进入企业内部"的资格证。

三、被严重低估的"消费者剩余陷阱"：你的系统正在悄悄被吞没

Benedict 提到了一个看似不起眼的观察："如果 DCF 从一周变成 10 秒，你不会做一个 DCF 收更多钱，你会做 50 个 DCF 收同样的钱"。这是 Jevons 悖论的金融服务版，但它真正的含义远比表面更狠。

把这个洞察跨界投射到任何一个系统性领域，会出现一个令人不安的图景：所有依赖"思考时间作为稀缺性"的职业，其商业模式的底层结构都将被静默重写。律师按小时收费、医生按问诊次数收费、咨询师按项目收费——这些定价机制都假设"专业判断需要时间"。当时间成本趋近于零，这些行业不会消失，但会被迫从"按时间计价"切换到"按结果计价"，而绝大多数人尚未意识到这是一个比"AI 取代工作"更深远的范式断裂。

真正的"啊哈时刻"在这里：Benedict 提到企业软件难以做"outcome-based pricing"，因为很难把每次按键和 P&L 挂钩。但这恰恰反向暴露了一个隐藏机会——第一个真正解决"将动作链路与商业结果可信归因"的工具，将自动成为 AI 时代的 Stripe。它不是模型层，不是应用层，而是一个全新的"价值溯源层"。所有人都在盯模型和应用，但没有人在搭建这个层——而它可能才是这一切真正的 chokepoint。

四、附带洞见：一个 Benedict 自己也没说破的悖论

他反复强调"我不知道"是一种诚实的智识立场，并嘲笑那些催他做预测的人。但他的整个论证结构本身就有一个被他刻意忽略的盲区：他用"过去三次都这样"来论证基础模型会成为 commodity，但又警告"过去不能预测未来"。这不是矛盾，而是一种隐藏的策略——他实际上在赌"元规律比具体规律更稳定"。这个赌注本身值得被单独研究，因为它定义了在不确定性中如何做系统性决策的一种新范式。

📝 转录文本

开场速览

Mobile didn't need to wait for the Internet. The Internet didn't need to wait for PCs and PCs didn't need to wait for consumer electronics and semiconductors and so on. So you always got this accelerating adoption.

Benedict Evans is a tech analyst known for his presentation "AI eats the world." He sees AI differently than the world, spotting patterns others miss and dives into how people really use AI.

They built this amazing piece, incredibly sophisticated, very expensive global infrastructure with enormous growth in use all the time. And it changed all of our lives, and we all pay for it, and they didn't make any money from it because all the value moved up the stack.

The place that's got product market fit right now is Coding. Anthropic's gone from whatever it was, 9 billion run rate at the end of last year to $47 billion run rate now. That's all software, isn't it? So what happens when someone else in some other field gets something working?

One of the characteristics of tech is that the moment that you understand something and you know what's going to happen is the moment you should move on to something else.

Google said that the risk of under-investing is riskier than over-investing. The investors are kind of looking at all these companies and saying...

一、回顾过去一年：变化与未解之谜

Benedict, welcome back to the A16Z podcast.

Thank you.

Last time you were here we were discussing the first iteration of your presentation "AI Eats the World." You since wrote it almost a year and a half ago at this point. We're gonna — you always begin your presentation with your "what are the big questions" — but I'm curious this time, before getting into the weather questions going forward, I want you to reflect on what have we learned since you originally made the presentation. What's played out? Let's reflect back on what's changed in the last year?

So I think we have much more of a sense of diverging product strategy. We have much more of a sense of kind of competitive tension that goes beyond just "make a bigger model faster with more compute." We've had several iterations of OpenAI strategy in particular, from sort of "everything all at once" yesterday to, "oops, no, maybe we should double down on coding."

Clearly, agentic coding started working, and so all the focus in tech has kind of narrowed in massively onto that as something that has an absolute product market fit in the sense that the customers are pulling it out of your hands. And of course, that comes with the supply crunch around capacity and price imbalance — imbalance of supply, demand, capacity, CapEx, pricing that we see at the moment.

That's the big shift. We had a moment of like, "this is working and exciting, but we're not quite sure what we're going to do with it." Right now, it works for coding. Will it work for anything else? Yes, almost certainly, but that's what's working right now. And so we've got this much narrower focus.

Otherwise, the chart numbers keep coming up, the models keep getting bigger, the CapEx keeps growing, the usage keeps growing, people using this more. But most of the fundamental questions you might have had two or three years ago didn't really have answers:

  • We don't know if there'll be a winner in the models.
  • We don't know if they can capture value up the stack.
  • We don't know how much the models can do.
  • We don't see a way that consumers will use this daily rather than weekly with the technology we have right now.

So all of those big questions are still open.

二、Coding 为什么率先跑通？

And just on the coding, could we have foreseen that that would have been the use case that really would have taken off? What's your reflection on that?

Well, deterministically, you could have said, "well, look, who's messing about with this stuff? Software developers. What are software developers going to try and make work? Software development." So at a very kind of simplistic, naive level, well yeah, the stuff that should work is software development first.

It's software development, just as — I often compare this moment to like the internet in like '97, '98, but it's also like the PCs in the early '80s or the late '70s. It's incredibly exciting. It's not quite clear what it's for and it doesn't quite work yet. And clearly the first thing that people did with PCs was make computers. And the first thing that people are doing with LLMs — and in a sense LLMs are computers — is to make more compute. And so that's not terribly surprising.

I think the shift has been at the beginning of this year, clearly that agentic coding went from being kind of useful to really changing everything. And I'm not sure you could have... Clearly there were people who would say, "well, this is going to be able to do absolutely anything." And so they will say, "well, yes, look, I told you." But I don't think anyone could have deterministically predicted exactly when that was going to happen and that it was going to be coding that would work first.

对工程师与团队结构的影响

And what have we learned about, say more about what this means for engineers, junior engineers, senior engineers, the jobs discussion, how teams are organized, et cetera. What have we learned so far?

I don't think we've learned anything. This didn't work six months ago. And everyone is scrambling around trying to work out what it means. And you can get very, very into the noise and the detail and "what did somebody say at a party yesterday? Oh my God, that's how it's all going to work." It's going to take a couple of years for this all to settle down. If nothing else, because of the pricing — we've got this enormous crunch between the demand and supply and hence the pricing.

So we don't know what a team's going to look like. I think people are asking new questions around the obvious one of: do you hire junior people? And if so, what are they doing? And why were you hiring junior people in the past? And were you actually hiring to do the thing that they did or were you hiring them to do something else? And so if you automate away a class of stuff that used to get done by people, then what will happen?

And that sort of becomes much more real now in software development because you're actually automating a bunch of stuff that used to be done by people. So those questions are kind of now rather than theoretical. But I don't think anybody can possibly say they kind of know what the market structure is going to look like or what the career of a software engineer is going to be in three years time. I think you'd be insane to think that you could know that yet.

三、OpenAI 的战略漂移与 Anthropic 的聚焦

Talk about OpenAI. Talk about what's most surprised you, or how have you kind of made sense of their strategy development and the questions that they have going forward?

Well, you know, it's always been such a tranquil drama-free environment. So, I mean, obviously they've had the issue with Fiji Simo having to take a medical leave, which kind of shuffled things up a bit.

Look, clearly, the second half, last quarter of last year, the question was: well, the models are the models, but what else? And how do we get people to do other stuff with this? So we'll do ads, we'll do e-commerce, we'll do shopping carts, we'll do payments, we'll do a browser, we'll do a social video app, we'll do everything. "Ask ChatGPT for 15 ideas for what we could do to build value on top of infrastructure, and then we'll do all of them." It's almost literally what it looked like.

And then Anthropic, with having less capital raised, said, "no, we're going to focus on coding," and they got coding working. Whether that was a deliberate strategy or kind of they stumbled into it is for other people to say, but clearly that worked. And then OpenAI kind of swing around and like, "okay, well, clearly that's the thing."

But the question kind of still remains, like the stuff that's working right now is software development and some things in some other fields. Then there's a lot of people who are excited about using this around the edges and using this for some things. But it's very unclear how it is that this is instantiated as product and taken to the other 90 percent of people.

We still see in the data that sort of 10 percent of people are daily active users and 30, 40 percent of people are weekly active users. And if you're only using this once a week, then you haven't achieved Nirvana yet. And there's clearly this kind of very widespread between people in the Valley who bought a cluster of Mac Studios and are running open Claude all day versus those other 40% of people who said, "yeah, it's kind of useful. I used it last week for something." And I'm like, how do you bridge that?

I don't think that question... Software is a place where that's really, really bridged — jumped over that bridge. Then there's a lot of other places where people are scratching their heads and using it up to a point. Then there's a lot of places where corporations are using it to automate some specific back office process where you're not asking the user to work out what they do with a new tool. Instead, you're saying, "okay, here's a problem that we can solve."

企业一次解决一个问题的现实

I go and talk to companies outside America and outside of tech and talk to consultants and investors, they're looking at those one-at-a-time point solutions. So I spoke a couple of days ago to a commodities company, and they want to use LLMs to get better predictions on their cash flow because they deal with all sorts of small producers, and they don't necessarily know when their invoices are going to get paid, and it's a very low margin business — so that's a big deal. And so they want to use LLMs to get better cash flow forecasting. That's a very different thing from going to ChatGPT or Claude and saying, "hey, give me a summary of my meetings this week."

四、与移动/PC 浪潮的三层类比

Now, can you share how did this compare with mobile or other sort of platform shifts in terms of early user adoption on sort of the weekly or daily user?

So I think there's a bunch of different ways to answer this.

第一层：加速吸纳

One of them is we're always standing on the shoulders of giants and the growth is always compounding. So mobile didn't need to wait for the internet or cellular networks. Mobile data, mobile internet didn't need to wait for cellular data, but it didn't need to wait for the internet to happen. And the internet didn't need to wait for PCs, and PCs didn't need to wait for consumer electronics and semiconductors and so on. So you've always got this accelerating adoption.

And when your old boss, Marc Andreessen, was working on Netscape, there were double-digit millions of PCs on the entire planet. So no, you couldn't have 900 million weekly active users because there weren't 900 million PCs. So there's always that acceleration. So that's one point.

第二层：早期一切都很难用

The second point is at the early stage of any of these shifts, it's not really clear how it's going to work and nothing works. I'm just about old enough to remember this. I'm not sure how old you are, but anyone in their 30s doesn't really remember a time when it was completely normal that you'd be working and then everything on the screen would just freeze and you just have to crawl under your desk and unplug the computer and then pray that some of what you've done in the last hour might still be there. That just doesn't happen anymore.

Go back to the '80s and you bought a sound card. Well, that's $300. You want to have sound on your computer? Okay, that's $300 and that's the weekend to make that work. I remember this, trying to get this stuff to work. And the same thing with the internet — you've got to get a floppy disk that has TCP/IP on, and it's slow, and none of the stuff that you need to do existed. And the same with mobile.

And we're kind of at that stage, and of course, it's not clear which of these things are going to work. And that's the same thing now — like a browser's going to work, is it going to be this, is it going to be that, how's this all going to fit together? And there's a gap between what's incredibly exciting and the small number of people who are willing to put their work in to get something to work, and just turning that into a thing where you can just press a button and it all happens.

第三层：定价错位与 2009-10 的移动数据账单

The third point here is — and there's a much more tangible observation — that the pricing crunch that we've already mentioned looks to me a lot like what happened with mobile data instead of 2009-10, where suddenly people got bills for like $5,000, $10,000 of data on the one side. And on the other hand, if you had flat rate data, which is kind of what happened in the US with the iPhone, the AT&T Cingular launched the iPhone with flat rate data. And then unfortunately everybody buys iPhones and then they get 3G and people start watching YouTube and the whole network goes down because they just don't have capacity to do that.

It's funny, there are still people in tech who don't understand that cellular networks have marginal cost. They have to add more capacity and that costs more money. And so the networks kind of had to scramble to get the cost curve aligned with the infrastructure, the pricing system aligned with the underlying cost and aligned with perceived value, which they kind of did with capped bundles and fair use and throttling and so on.

But the other side of that — and that's exactly what you see now. It's like on the one hand, you're paying $20 a month and you get 10 grand worth of tokens. And on the other hand, you messed about for a couple of days and you get a bill for 10 grand and you're like, "what the hell is this?" That's exactly what you see. You literally see these stories now, which is exactly what happened in 2009, 8, 9, 10. Also what happened in 2001 and 2 and 3 with GPRS.

价值如何流向上层

But I think the other interesting part of that analogy is that since then, mobile data traffic has risen by something like 1,500 to 2,000 times. And the mobile networks collectively have revenue of about a trillion dollars. And they spend about $200 billion a year on CapEx. And the stocks have been flat for 20 years, and all the cool stuff got built by somebody else.

And they kind of all thought that they were going to build all the cool stuff. I worked for a phone company that had a banking license because they thought they would do mobile banking, which now seems absolutely insane. But that's kind of the point: they built this amazing piece of global, incredibly sophisticated, very expensive global infrastructure with enormous growth in use all the time. And it changed all of our lives and we all pay for it. And they didn't make any money from it because all the value moved up the stack.

And this is, of course, absolutely, as I said earlier, this is one of the absolutely central questions for LLMs: can the model do the whole thing or do you have to have 300 apps built on top of it? Can you just go to the model and say, "do my taxes for me"? Or do you need to have a TaxThing that uses AI that might use some AI in 10 different ways inside it? And if not, then what is it to be a foundational model provider? Is this just commodity infrastructure that gets sold at marginal cost?

Which somehow seems to be a very difficult concept for people to grasp right now, because you can sell all the tokens you can make, so you can price it at ROI. But over the next couple of years, we've got like a trillion or two trillion dollars of CapEx coming down the pipe and the models get 100X, 200X more efficient every year. And then there's new models and will the models use more tokens or less tokens? But we'll get to a different equilibrium. And why would that equilibrium be one where the model companies have pricing power when the models are all kind of the same, doing kind of the same thing with the same chips? Why would they have pricing power?

It's a long answer to your question, but you go back and look over time — chip companies didn't capture the value. ISPs didn't capture the value. Mobile network operators didn't capture the value. Windows and iOS did, but they were doing something else. They had all these levers to go up the stack. Of course, they had network effects, which models don't have.

The question is: do they end up like the infrastructure layers or do they end up like the operating system layers and capture value and actually get to decide what gets built? The irony of that, of course, is Netscape where Marc Andreessen famously said that he was going to turn Windows into a set of badly debugged device drivers. And then Microsoft kind of crowbarred their way into the market, but it turned out that web browsers weren't the point because all the value was somewhere else.

And so I think there are these kind of swirling massive questions around how this settles out, which comes back through to all of my answers to your question. It's like, you know some of this stuff, but you don't know how it's going to work.

五、为什么基础模型不是产品？

Yeah, yeah. It's unclear whether it looks more like the internet or sort of software where a lot of the value or just better margins happen at the application layer, or sort of the cloud where it seems sort of it existed at the hardware layer. And right now, so far, it seems like NVIDIA and going up, it seems like they have better margins and are accruing a lot of the value, but it's unclear if that will remain the same, or if there will be sort of applications. How would you even begin to predict the answer to such?

Well, to answer this, I mean, there's all these sorts of quotes about how history works. And my favorite one is: history teaches us nothing except that something will happen. And you can always ex post facto say, "well, of course, it worked out like that." But it generally wasn't obvious at the time.

In particular, I remember sort of 15 years ago, a lot of really, really clever people in tech looked at the iPhone and Android and said, "this is open versus closed again. And we're just going to crush the iPhone," which of course isn't what happened. And then I can go and explain why. But all of these comparisons are useful. None of them are predictive. It's always obvious in hindsight.

It's funny, I've done a couple of podcasts recently and I published this presentation and there's a class of comment on this stuff, which is to say, "Benedict, you're not doing your job. You're supposed to tell us what's going to happen. You're supposed to make predictions. And all you seem to do is say, 'well, we don't know.'"

And there's kind of two problems with that. One of them is there's a class of places where I actually do say, "I don't think this is going to work. I think it's going to work like that." Like, I don't think foundation models are a product. I don't think a chatbot is a product. I think the value will be further up.

The other side of this is when you're at this stage in the cycle, there's many paths and you don't know which of those paths it's going to be. And to try and say, "well, I think it's going to be that one" — you might be right, but you do have to be conscious of how uncertain this is and how many different paths it could take. That's the nature of this part of the cycle: all the depths are open. We get to the point where the S curve curves up and it narrows in.

There was a moment when Windows Phone might have worked. In hindsight, no, it probably wasn't going to work. There was a moment when it wasn't clear how mobile was going to work. There was a moment where it was clear, "this is what's happening. Now we move on to the next question."

I'm sorry, I'm kind of monologuing a bit, but one of the characteristics of tech is that the moment that you understand something and you know how it works and what's going to happen is the moment you should move on to something else. You should always be looking for the quiet places where we don't know what the answers are. Because I haven't updated my Apple spreadsheet in like five years because we know what happened. They won. I don't care what this year's iPhone looks like. I don't pay attention to their market share in China. Like, it happened. Next question.

四个推理积木：为什么模型可能成为大宗商品

Yeah. But just to flesh it out, you mentioned the prediction of you don't think foundation models are the product, you think will move up. Explain the reasoning there a bit and what they could look like.

So I think there's like three or four building blocks you can put on the table.

One, it's not clear how you could build a model that was fundamentally better than everybody else's model in some sort of sustainable, differentiated way. There doesn't seem to be a network effect. There doesn't seem to be sort of levers you can pull and a position you can get into where Instagram is or YouTube is or Google search is. And we don't see an equivalent of that for LLMs. Now you have different emphasis — maybe this one's better than that, maybe you like this one more than that. But there doesn't seem to be a fundamental differentiation, fundamental competitive difference between models, except your willingness to spend money.

Second problem is the chatbot itself is kind of a weird, limited V1 UI. And there's some things and some people and some kind of task where it works really well. But for most of the others, you need a bunch of other stuff. You need tooling and it needs to be set up right and needs to have the right data and it needs to be configured and controlled and have the right user interface and people need to have kind of sat down and thought about how this should work.

Because generally, people who are good at using the tool and doing the job that needs the tool are not the same people who are good at deciding what the tool should be. So people who are really, really good at designing print publications are not the people who should create InDesign. That's a different set of skills. And people who are really, really good at doing financial advice are not the right people to design TurboTax. There's different people with different skills.

And you have kind of groping around the middle of this. So you have Claude for this, Claude for that, and you have skills and so on. To me, this is kind of like: well, one question is, who builds the skill? Another question is — well, that seems to be a bit like what you get if you do File > New in Excel: these are templates and they'll take you so far. But at a certain point, people outgrow the templates.

There's a slide in my presentation, which is a quote that somebody said to me on Twitter years ago. He said they were a consultant and half of the jobs were telling people who used Excel to use a database and the other half were telling people who used a database to use Excel. So this is kind of a fuzzy, swirly place of: do you need dedicated software? Do you need horizontal software? Do you need vertical software? But you can't just do everything in Excel. We've all seen the department that runs along on a 10-meg Excel file. I run my business in Numbers, but only in spreadsheets. But there's a certain point where you outgrow that.

And so following that on: well, can the model labs build all of that? Well, of course not. No more than Microsoft or Apple could build every Windows app or every iPhone app. So then do the model labs have leverage? Are they Windows? Are they iOS? And again, well, is there a network effect?

Like if you're a law firm right now and you buy a piece of software — all pieces of enterprise software that A16Z is invested in — how often does the law firm or the manufacturing company or the bank say, "well, does this use Claude or does it use OpenAI because we've standardized on Claude?" Well, no, that's not how it works. Any more than it worked like that for cloud. You didn't say, "well, our company is standardized on AWS." You don't even know what cloud that SaaS product runs on. That's the whole point. It's abstracted away. It's not your problem.

And so the foundation models seem to look more like that. They seem to look more like hyperscalers in that sense, in that they might have competitive advantages. But further up the stack, you don't have leverage, you don't have a network effect, you don't have control.

That sort of prompts me to, incidentally, to say, well, maybe the right comparison here is with semiconductors, where with each generation, it just gets more expensive and so you have fewer players.

大宗商品论的最后一环：定价权

So all of that kind of taken together — well, the models are kind of diff commodities and the chatbot isn't the right UI or the right product, and the companies aren't going to be able to build all of that stuff themselves, so therefore they're low-level infrastructure. And so then, well, do they have pricing power?

Well, you're going to have — pick a number — three to six companies making a frontier model, spending, no one knows, no one honest knows, something between $200 billion and $2 trillion a year on building these models, plus there'll be a bunch of edge and a bunch of open source. Go and ask Martin Casado what he thinks. I don't know. He doesn't know either. He probably has a better way of saying he doesn't know than I do. But we don't know.

So where is this going to settle down? You're going to have, as it might be, half a dozen companies that are all competing to sell this stuff. And so where is the price discipline going to come from, particularly when some of them have got like whole other business models as well, like Google selling ads. So they've got a different attitude to pricing to OpenAI.

And so I think the challenge here is there's a difference between where we are right now and where this should end up, which is kind of a first-year economics student kind of conversation. Right now, we're in this period of extreme disequilibrium of supply and demand and price and CapEx and capacity. But just because demand for tokens is infinite, that doesn't mean that you can't get to a different price equilibrium.

Because of course, that's what happened with mobile data. Demand for bits is infinite, it's grown 1500, 2000X in the last 15 years. But you still got your supply and price equilibrium, and you still got a murderous price war between telcos in most parts of the world. Because fundamentally, you're selling kind of a commodity to people who will swap back and forth. And of course, developers will also swap back and forth.

Now, I'm happy to say that this might be completely wrong. It may be that we get to a world in which there's only two companies that can make an LLM and they have pricing power. Or we get to a world in which most of what we do gets subsumed into the model, or they have leverage further up the stack.

And it's kind of my point about iOS versus Android: just because you can say, "well, it worked like that the last three times," that doesn't prove what's going to happen this time. But it doesn't mean that at least you should sort of ask the questions and you should certainly just say as a sort of a primary observation, this situation right now is transitory. We're in this extreme scarcity and then we have a pricing system and we have a free market and we have a surge of CapEx and like a trillion dollars of CapEx. So those multiples are going to move around. And then what?

六、下一组该问的问题

Yeah. It's a good segue to your point you made earlier of "hey, you know, Apple's Apple — next question." As a segue, what are some of the next questions that you're most focused on or that we should be paying most attention to?

So some of the questions we've already talked about: how far up the stack do the models go? How do the models differentiate?

Another is obviously: at what point do we see more and more classes of use case where the models are good enough and we don't need the most expensive, fastest, biggest, heaviest model in the cloud, and you can use an older model, you can use an open source model, you can have a model running on device? Obviously this is what Apple's going to be talking about in a couple of weeks. How much can you push onto the device where the computer is free — or free to you? Anyway, it doesn't have marginal cost for the developer.

跳出技术：金字塔型行业的命运

Another classic question is — it's almost like the question is to move out of technology. So if you're looking at a law firm or a consultancy or an investment bank, basically anyone in professional services where you traditionally have this pyramid structure, and you can automate a great chunk of what the people at the bottom of the pyramid are doing, what happens?

And the only thing you can say there is if you have never worked at a law firm or never worked at Bain, BCG, McKinsey, probably not going to have a good idea of how this works. You probably don't really know what it is that all those associates are doing. And you also don't really know what it is that the client is paying for. And how do those things get reconfigured?

And so, what does AI mean for finance, based on that internal hiring structure and the kind of products you can create and the margin structure? What does it mean for consultants? What does it mean for the Big Four, for the Big Three, for Accenture, for big law firms, and for advertising? You probably can know some of those questions, but if you're not in that industry, you don't really know the answers.

Netflix 不是科技公司：洛杉矶问题 vs 旧金山问题

The thing this reminds me a lot of is there's something I wrote when I was at A16Z, which I called Content Isn't King. And I also wrote something that said Netflix isn't a tech company. And the point I was kind of getting at is that if you look at Netflix, this whole thing is enabled by stuff that the tech industry has built. But all the questions for Netflix are TV, are LA questions: what shows, how many shows, what kind of shows, what should you pay the talent? Should you aim for awards? Should you do movies? Should you buy sports? What kind of sports? These are all Los Angeles questions. These are not San Francisco questions. No one in San Francisco even knows what the right questions are. They're media industry questions.

And this was kind of my point: all the questions that matter for Netflix have become media industry questions. This is obviously the great tension point about Tesla: is it a car company or is it a technology company?

And so what I'm kind of getting at is that what does this stuff mean for law is kind of a question for lawyers as much as it is, or people who understand a lot about law firms and how they actually work and what they're actually doing and what the client's actually buying from them. Same thing for what does generative video mean for Hollywood? Ben Affleck probably knows a lot more about this than I do. He built a company and sold it for like a hundred million dollars. So obviously he does.

与以往平台变革的根本不同

That's kind of a second question, which is the question moves outside of AI and they become sort of half AI questions, half something else kind of questions.

And then the third level, which is — I probably should have said this earlier — the way that all of this is sort of fundamentally different from previous platform shifts, is that with 3G or the iPhone or the web or whatever it was, you didn't know what was going to happen next, but you knew the physical limits.

Like 1995, you knew that telcos weren't going to give everybody in the world broadband next week, and you knew that everyone in the world wasn't going to go out and buy a PC because the PC cost like $3,000. So you kind of knew the basic physical limits of what could and couldn't happen. And with generative AI, obviously we don't. We might look at our phones when we get off this recording and there's a push notification that says that OpenAI's new model is out and it's like 2% of the price because they worked something out.
